question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
When the acceleration of an object is zero, its velocity is constant.
The acceleration of an object is the slope of its position-time graph.
explanation: Statement A is true because zero acceleration means no change in velocity, implying constant velocity. Statement B is false because acceleration is the slope of the velocity-time graph, not the position-time graph. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Acceleration can be a result of a change in the object's speed.
An object moving with a constant speed in a circular path experiences zero acceleration.
explanation: Statement A is true as acceleration can be due to a change in speed. Statement B is false because an object in circular motion experiences centripetal acceleration even if its speed is constant due to the constant change in direction. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: An object experiencing a constant acceleration has a velocity that changes at a constant rate over time.

Reason: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time.
explanation: The assertion is true as constant acceleration implies a uniform change in velocity. The reason correctly explains the assertion, as acceleration by definition is the rate at which velocity changes. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: A particle starts from rest. Its acceleration (a) versus time $(t)$ graph is as shown in the figure.The maximum speed of the particle will be
explanation:
We know that, area under $a-t$ graph gives us change in velocity https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952034/preptracker/questions/q_106160_explanation_1764952034549.png Given; $u=0 {~m} / {s}$
$\therefore$ Area under $a-t$ graph $=$ Change in velocity
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 10=v-u \Rightarrow 75=v-0$
$\Rightarrow v=75 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The zero velocity of a particle at any instant always implies zero acceleration at that instant.

Reason: A body is momentarily at rest when it reverses its direction of motion.
explanation: Assertion is false because if we take the example of a ball thrown vertically upwards, then at the topmost point of its motion, the ball momentarily comes to rest but acceleration due to gravity keeps on acting on the ball.Reason is true because whenever any object reverses its direction of motion, the sign of velocity changes because it comes to rest for a moment at the point where it changes its direction of motion. The same example of motion of ball given above can be used to explain it as the ball changes the direction of motion after reaching the topmost point when its speed becomes zero for a moment.So, the correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question:
${\rm{Statement}}\,{\rm{I}}:$ An object subjected to velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ has a resultant velocity with magnitude $|v|=\left|v_{1}\right|+\left|v_{2}\right|$.
${\rm{Statement}}\,\,{\rm{II}}:$ The magnitude of displacement is either less or equal to the path length of an object between two points.
${\rm{Statement}}\,\,{\rm{III}}:$ The instantaneous acceleration is the limiting value of the average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
When an object has two velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$, then magnitude of resultant velocity is
$v=\sqrt{v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}+2 v_{1} v_{2} \cos \theta}$
Statement I is incorrect. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957669/preptracker/questions/q_106154_explanation_1764957669269.png Clearly | displacement $\mid \leq$ distance
Statement II is correct.
Instantaneous acceleration $ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta t}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{\Delta t \to 0} a$
Statement III is correct.
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: Statements I, II and III are true.
Statements I, II are true but statement III is false.
Statements II, III are true but statement I is false.
Statements I, II and III are false.

subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving in a straight line. The variation of position ' $x$ ' as a function of time ' $t$ ' is given as $x=\left(t^{3}-6 t^{2}+20 t+15\right) m$. The velocity of the body when its acceleration becomes zero is
explanation:
As per question $x=t^{3}-6 t^{2}+20 t+15$;
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=3 t^{2}-12 t+20$;
$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=6 t-12$
Here, $a=0 \Rightarrow 6 t-12=0 ; t=2 s$
At $t=2 s$, acceleration becomes zero.
So, $v=3(2)^{2}-12(2)+20$
$ = \left( {3 \times 4} \right) - \left( {12 \times 2} \right) + 20$
$ = 12 - 24 + 20$
$ = 8\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: The velocity-time graph of moving object is given in the figure. Distance travelled by the body in the interval of time in which the acceleration is maximum is
explanation:
Maximum acceleration is from $B$ to $C$.
$\therefore \quad {a_{\max }}{\rm{ }} = $ slope of $BC$ curve $ = \frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta t}}$
$ = \frac{{40 - 10}}{{40 - 30}} = 3\,m\,{s^{ - 2}}\,$
${s_{\max }}{\rm{ }} = \frac{{{v^2} - {u^2}}}{{2{a_{\max }}}} = \frac{{{{40}^2} - {{10}^2}}}{{2 \times 3}} = 250\,m$
subject: Physics

question: The area under the acceleration time graph represents:
explanation:

\\\Rightarrow d v & =a d t\end{aligned}$.
So correct option is (3)

question: The velocity time graphs of two bodies ${A}$ and ${B}$ are shown in figure. The ratio of their acceleration is
explanation:

So correct option is (1)

question: Which of the following four statements is false?
explanation:
When a body is projected vertically upwards, at highest point its velocity is zero but still accelerated downwards.
In uniform circular motion a body has constant speed and still have a varying velocity.
In projectile motion direction of the velocity of a body changes, when its acceleration is constant.
So correct option is (2)

options.text: A body can have zero velocity and still be accelerated
A body can have a constant velocity and still have a varying speed
A body can have a constant speed and still have a varying velocity
The direction of the velocity of a body can change when its acceleration is constant

subject: Physics

question: The position $x$ of a particle varies with time ($t$) as $x = A{t^2} - B{t^3}$. The acceleration at time $t$ of the particle will be equal to zero. What is the value of $t$?
explanation:
Given that $x = A{t^2} - B{t^3}$
$\therefore$ velocity $ = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2At - 3B{t^2}$
and acceleration $ = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right) = 2\,A - 6Bt$
For acceleration to be zero $2\,A - 6Bt = 0$.
$\therefore t = \frac{{2\,A}}{{6\,B}} = \frac{A}{{3\,B}}$
subject: Physics

question: The area of the acceleration-displacement curve of a body gives
explanation:
Area of acceleration-displacement curve
gives change in kinetic energy per unit mass.
$\frac{1}{2}m\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right) = Fs = \frac{{mdv}}{{dt}} \times s$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{\rm{Change}}\,\,\,{\rm{in}}\,\,{\rm{kinetic}}\,\,{\rm{energy}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}}}$
$ = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} \times s$

options.text: change in momentum per unit mass
change in kinetic energy per unit mass

subject: Physics

question: Speeds of a particle at 3rd and 8th second are $20 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and zero respectively, then average acceleration between 3rd and 8th second will be
explanation:
Time interval between 8 th and 3rd second,
$\Delta t=8-3=5 s \text {, i.e. } \Delta t=5 s$
Change in velocity,
$\Delta v = 20 - 0 = 20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore$ Average acceleration
$ = \frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{20}}{5} = 4\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

explanation:

explanation:

Reason: Increase in speed of a moving body is independent of its direction of motion.
explanation: If we take 'upward' as positive for a freely falling body, assertion is true, because body acquires more speed while falling. As increase in speed is by gravity, but not by sign convention for direction, 'reason' is true. But it is a separate fact. So correct option is (2) .

question:
Assertion: A body can have acceleration even if its velocity is zero at a given instant of time.

Reason: A body is momentarily at rest when it reverses its direction of motion.
explanation:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. It's possible for an object to have zero velocity at a particular instant but still be accelerating if its velocity is changing.
When a body reverses its direction of motion, there is an instant where it is momentarily at rest before it starts moving in the opposite direction. Example: vertically projected body at highest point.
The reason provides a correct explanation for why the assertion is true. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A body may be accelerated even when it is moving at uniform speed.

Reason: When direction of motion of the body is changing then body may have acceleration.
explanation:
Acceleration is a vector quantity that includes changes in speed or changes in direction. Even if a body is moving at a constant speed, it can still be accelerating if its direction of motion is changing.
Changing direction implies a change in velocity, and therefore acceleration. This is because velocity is a vector quantity that includes both speed and direction.
The reason provides a correct explanation for why the assertion is true. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: The velocity of the particle at any time $t$ is given by $V=2 t(3-t) m s^{-1}$. At what time is its velocity maximum?
explanation:
At the time of maximum velocity, acceleration is zero.
$V=2 t(3-t) \quad V=6 t-2 t^{2}$
Differentiating both side
$\dfrac{d V}{d t}=\dfrac{d\left(6 t-2 t^{2}\right)}{d t} \quad o=6-4 t \quad t=1.5$

question:
The position of an object moving along \[X\]-axis is given by \[x(t) = a - b{t^2}\], where \[a = 8.5\,m,b = 2.5\,m{s^{ - 2}}\] and \[t\] is measured in seconds. For the above situation, match the column I with column II .
A. Velocity of object at $t = 2.0\,s$
B. Velocity of object at $t = 0\,s$
C. Acceleration of object at $t = 2.0\,s$
D. Average velocity between $t = 2.0\,s\,{\rm{and}}\,t = 4.0\,s$
P. $ - 15\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Q. $ - 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
R. $0\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
S. $ - 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
explanation:
Given,$x(t) = a - b{t^2},$ $(a = 8.5{\mkern 1mu} \,m\,{\text{and}}\,b = 2.5{\mkern 1mu} \,m{s^{ - 2}}).$
$\therefore x(t) = 8.5 - 2.5{t^2}$
Velocity of object $ = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 2bt$
A. Velocity at $t = 2.0\,s = {\left. {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right|_{t = 2}} = - 4b$
$ = - 4 \times 2.5 = - 10\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
B. Velocity at $t = 0s = {\left. {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right|_{t = 0}} = 0\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
C. Acceleration of object $\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - 2b = - 5\,\,m/{s^2}$
D. Average velocity $ = \frac{{x({t_2}) - x({t_1})}}{{{t_2} - {t_1}}} = \frac{{x(4) - x(2)}}{{4 - 2}}$
$ = \frac{{\left[ {a - b{{\left( 4 \right)}^2}} \right] - \left[ {a - b{{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} \right]}}{2} = \frac{{4b - 16b}}{2}$
$ = - \frac{{12b}}{2} = - 6b = - 6 \times 2.5\,\,m{s^{ - 1}} = - 15\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Hence, $A \to Q,B \to R,C \to S\, \rm{and} \,D \to P$

options.text: A - P,  B - Q, C - R,  D - S
A - Q, B - R, C - S,   D - P
A - S,  B - R, C - S, D - P
A - R, B - Q, C - P,D - S

subject: Physics

question: A particle starts from rest at the origin at $t = 0$ and moves along $ + ve$ -axis with acceleration $2m{s^{ - 2}}.$ After $4s$, direction of acceleration is reversed. At what time it is at the origin again?
explanation:
$u = 0,\,a = 2 \, m{s^{ - 2}};\,{v_1} = 0 + 2 \times 4$
${v_1} = 8 \, m{s^{ - 1}}$
Now,
$a = - 2 \, m{s^{ - 1}};v = u + at \Rightarrow 0 = 8 - 2t \Rightarrow t = 4 \, s$
${S_1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {4^2} = 16 \, m$
To come to rest $t = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \, s$
${S_2} = 8 \times t - \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {t^2} = 8 \times 4 - {4^2} = 16 \, m$
Total distance travelled in forward direction is $32 \, m$
Return path
$S = 32 \, m,\,u = 0,\,a = 2 \, m{s^{ - 2}}$
$S = \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} \Rightarrow 32 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {t^2}$
$t = \sqrt {32} = 4\sqrt 2 \,s$
$\therefore $ After a time $(8 + 4\sqrt 2 )s$ the particle is again at the origin.
subject: Physics

question: A body travels for $12\,\sec $ starting from rest with constant acceleration. If it travels distance  ${S_1},{S_2}\,{\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,{S_3}$ in the first four seconds, second four seconds and next four seconds respectively the relation between ${S_1},{S_2}\,{\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,{S_3}$ is
explanation:
If the body starts from rest and moves with constant acceleration then the ratio of distance in consecutive equal time intervals is
${S_1} = \frac{1}{2}a({4^2})$
${S_2} = \frac{1}{2}a({8^2} - {4^2})$
${S_3} = \frac{1}{2}a({12^2} - {8^2})$
${S_1}:{S_2}:{S_3} = 1:3:5.$
subject: Physics

question: A car accelerates from rest at a constant for some time, after which it decelerates at a constant rate $\beta $ and comes to rest. If the total time elapsed is $t$, then the distance travelled by the car is
explanation:
Let the car accelerate at rate $\alpha $ for time ${t_1}$ then maximum velocity attained,
$v = 0 + \alpha {t_1} = \alpha {t_1}$
Now, the car decelerates at a rate $\beta $ for time $\left( {t - {t_1}} \right)$ and finally comes to rest. Then,
$0 = v\beta \left( {t - {t_1}} \right) \Rightarrow 0 = \alpha {t_1} - \beta t + \beta {t_1}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_1} = \frac{\beta }{{\alpha + \beta }}t;\,{S_1} = \frac{1}{2}\alpha \,t_1^2$
${S_2} = \frac{1}{2}\beta t_2^2$
$ \Rightarrow {S_1} + {S_2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\alpha t_1^2 + \beta t_2^2} \right)$
$S = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\alpha \beta }}{{\alpha + \beta }}{t^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves a distance $x$ in time $t$ according to equation $x = {\left( {t + 5} \right)^{ - 1}}$. The acceleration of particle is proportional to
explanation:

Acceleration,
$a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{2}{{{{\left( {t + 5} \right)}^3}}} \Rightarrow a\,\alpha {\left( {velocity} \right)^{3/2}}.$
subject: Physics

question: The relation between time and distance is $t = \alpha {x^2} + \beta x,$ where $\alpha $ and $\beta $ are constants. The retardation is
explanation:
$\frac{{dt}}{{dx}} = 2\alpha x + \beta \Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{{2\alpha x + \beta }}$
$\because a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
$a = v\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - v.2\alpha }}{{{{\left( {2\alpha x + \beta } \right)}^2}}} = - 2\alpha .v.{v^2} = - 2\alpha {v^3}$
$\therefore \quad {\rm{Retardation}} = 2\alpha {v^3}.$
subject: Physics

question:
Acceleration of a body can’t be changed abruptly at an instant.
Acceleration can be produced only by change in the magnitude of the velocity.
explanation:
Acceleration of a body can be changed abruptly at any instant by changing force.
Accleration can be produced by change in the direction of velocity also. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
A particle may be momentarily at rest and yet have non - zero acceleration.
The zero velocity of a particle at any instant does not necessarily imply zero acceleration at that instant
explanation: A particle may be momentarily at rest and yet have non - zero acceleration. For example,  freely  falling particle has zero velocity at its   uppermost point but the acceleration at that  instant continues to be the acceleration due to gravity. Option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

explanation:

question: The velocity - time graph for two bodies $A$ and $B$ are shown. Then the acceleration of $A$ and $B$ are in the ratio
explanation:
As the slope of $v - t$ graph gives acceleration, so acceleration $a = \tan \theta $
$\therefore $ From the given graph
The acceleration of $A$ is
${a_A} = \tan 25^\circ $
and that of $B$ is
${a_B} = \tan \,(90^\circ - 40^\circ ) = \tan 50^\circ $
$\frac{{{a_A}}}{{{a_B}}} = \frac{{\tan 25^\circ }}{{\tan 50^\circ }}$
subject: Physics

question:
The velocity of a particle moving in the positive direction of $x = $ axis varies as $v = 5\sqrt x $. Assuming that at $t = 0$, particle was at $x = 0$.
What is the acceleration of the particle?
explanation: ${v^2} = 25 \times \,$ comparing with ${v^2} = 2as$, we have, $a = 12.5\,m/{s^2}$

question: The displacement of a particle is given by $y = a + bt + c{t^2} - d{t^4}$. The initial velocity and acceleration are respectively
explanation:
$y = a + bt + c{t^2} - d{t^4}$
$\therefore v = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = b + 2ct - 4d{t^3}$
and $a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 2c - 12d{t^2}$
Hence, at $t = 0,{v_{initial}} = b\,{\rm{and}}\,{a_{initial}} = 2c$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves a distance $x$ in time $t$ according to the equation $x = {(t + 5)^{ - 1}}$. The acceleration of  particle is proportional to
explanation:
Given,
Distance $x = {(t + 5)^{ - 1}}$   (1)
Differentiating Eq. (1) w.r.t. $t$, we get
$\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = (v) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{(t + 5)}^2}}}$   (2)
Again, differentiating Eq. (2) w.r.t. $t$, we get
$\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = (a) = \frac{2}{{{{(t + 5)}^3}}}$   (3)
Comparing Eqs (2) and (3) , we get
$a\,\alpha \,{v^{3/2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A body is moving with velocity $30\,m/s$ towards East. After $10 \, s$, its velocity becomes $40\,m/s$ towards North. The average acceleration of the body is
explanation:
Average acceleration $ = \frac{{{\rm{Change \, in \, velocity }}}}{{{\rm{Total \, time}}}}$
$a = \frac{{\left| {{v_f} - {v_i}} \right|}}{{\Delta \,t}} = \frac{{\sqrt {30{}^2 + {{40}^2}} }}{{10}}$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {900 + 1600} }}{{10}} = 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement at any point time $t$ is given by $s = 3{t^3} + 7{t^2} + 14t + 5$. The acceleration of the particle at $t = 1\,s$ is

question: The given graph shows the variation of velocity ($v$) with position ($x$) for a  particle moving along a straight line. Which of the following graph shows the variation of acceleration ($a$) with position ($x$)?
explanation:
$v = - kx + c$ (given line with negative slope)
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - k\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - kv$
$ \Rightarrow a = - k( - kx + c) = {k^2}x - kc$
Slope of $a - x$ graph is positive and $a = - ve$ at $x = 0$
subject: Physics

question: The motion of a particle along a straight line is described   by  equation $x = 8 + 12t - {t^3},$ where, $x$ is in metre and $t$ in sec. The retardation of the particle when its velocity becomes zero, is
explanation:
Given, $x = 8 + 12t - {t^3}$
we know $v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
and acceleration $a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}}$
So,$v = 12 - 3{t^2}$
and $a = - \,6t$
At $t = 2s$
$v = 0$ and $a = - \,6 \times 2$
$a = - \,12\,m/{s^2}$
So, retardation of the particle $ = \,12\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moving with uniform speed $v$, changes its direction by angle $\theta $ in time $t$.  Magnitude of its average acceleration during this time is
explanation:
${a_{av}} = \left| {\frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta r}}} \right| = \frac{{\left| {{v_f} - {v_i}} \right|}}{t}$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {{v^2} + {v^2} - 2v.v.\cos \theta } }}{t}$
$ = \frac{{2v\,\,\sin \theta /2}}{t}$
subject: Physics

question: For a body moving along a straight line, the following $v-t$ graph is obtained. According to the graph, the displacement during
explanation: The area  under the velocity-time curve is equal to the displacement. So the displacement  is less in uniform acceleration than in uniform  motion, as the corresponding area is less for the first case.

Uniform acceleration is less than that during uniform motion

question: A particle moves along a straight line, such that its displacement $x$ varies with time $t$ as $x=\alpha t^{3}+\beta t^{2}+\gamma$, where $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ are constants. $v_{1}$ is the average velocity of the particle during its journey between $t=1 s$ and $t=3 s$ and $v_{2}$ is the instantaneous velocity of the particle at $t=3 {~s}$. The ratio $v_{1} / v_{2}$ is
explanation:
Displacement of the particle,
$x=\alpha t^{3}+\beta t^{2}+\gamma$
at $t_{1}=1 s, x_{1}=\alpha \cdot 1^{3}+\beta \cdot 1^{2}+\gamma=\alpha+\beta+\gamma$
at $t_{2}=3 s, x_{3}=\alpha \cdot 3^{3}+\beta \cdot 3^{2}+\gamma=27 \alpha+9 \beta+\gamma$
Average velocity, $v_{1}=\dfrac{\text { Total distance travelled }}{\text { Total time taken }}$
$=\dfrac{x_{3}-x_{1}}{t_{2}-t_{1}}=\dfrac{27 \alpha+9 \beta+\gamma-\alpha-\beta-\gamma}{3-1}$
$=\dfrac{26 \alpha+8 \beta}{2}=13 \alpha+4 \beta$
Instantaneous velocity at $t=3 {~s}$ is given as,
$v_{2}=\left.\dfrac{d x}{d t}\right|_{t=3 s}$
$=\left(3 \alpha t^{2}+2 \beta t+0\right) /$ at $t=3 {~s}$
$=3 \alpha(3)^{2}+2 \beta(3)=27 \alpha+6 \beta$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{13 \alpha+4 \beta}{27 \alpha+6 \beta}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A car travels in a straight line along a road. Its distance $x$ from a stop sign is given as a function of $t$ by the equation $x(t)=\alpha t+\beta t^{3}$, where $\alpha  = 2.0\,m/s,\beta  = 0.01\,m/{s^3}$. Calculate the average velocity of the car in the time interval $t = 2.00\,s$ to $4.00\,s$.
explanation:
Given, equation of distance
$x(t) = \alpha t + \beta {t^3}{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where, $\alpha=2 {~m} / {s}$ and $\beta=0.01 {~m} / {s}^{3}$
Initial time, $t_{1}=2 s$ and final time, $t_{2}=4 s$
As we know that,
Average velocity ( $v_{a v}$ )
$=\dfrac{\text { Total displacement }(x)}{\text { Total time }(t)}$
Since $x(t)=\alpha t+\beta t^{3}$
$\therefore$ Distance travelled by car in time interval
( $t_{2}-t_{1}$ ) will be
$x=\alpha\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)+\beta\left(t_{2}^{3}-t_{1}^{3}\right)$
$=2(4-2)+\dfrac{1}{100}\left(4^{3}-2^{3}\right)$
$=2(2)+\dfrac{1}{100}[64-8]=4+\dfrac{1}{100}(56)=4.56 {~m}$
$\therefore \quad v_{a v}=\dfrac{x}{t_{2}-t_{1}}=\dfrac{4.56}{4-2}=\dfrac{4.56}{2}=2.28 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A cyclist traversed half the distance of a linear track with a velocity of $10\,m/s$. The remaining part of the track was covered with a velocity $v_{1}$ for half the time and a velocity $v_{2}$ for the other half of the time. If ${v_1} + {v_2} = 20\,m/s$, then the average velocity of the cyclist during the completion of the journey through the track is
explanation:
Let $s$ be the total distance traversed by the cyclist and $t_{1}$ be the time taken to cover first half distance $\left(\dfrac{s}{2}\right)$ with velocity $v_{0}=10 {~m} / {s}$
Therefore, $\dfrac{s}{2}=v_{0} t_{1}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_1} = \frac{s}{{2{v_0}}} = \frac{s}{{2 \times 10}} = \frac{s}{{20}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Further, let $2 t$ be the time to cover rest half distance $\left(\dfrac{s}{2}\right)$.
Therefore, $\dfrac{s}{2}=v_{1} t+v_{2} t$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{s}{2}=\left(v_{1}+v_{2}\right) t \Rightarrow 2 t=\dfrac{s}{v_{1}+v_{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow 2t = \frac{s}{{20}}\quad \left[ {\because {v_1} + {v_2} = 20} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Average velocity,
$v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{\text { Total distance traversed }}{\text { Total time taken }}$
$=\dfrac{s}{t_{1}+t_{2}}=\dfrac{s}{\dfrac{s}{20}+\dfrac{s}{20}}$
[From Eqs. (1) and (2)]
${v_{avg{\text{ }}}} = \frac{1}{{2/20}} = \frac{{20}}{2} = 10\,m/s$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: For the following velocity-time graph, the average speed for the motion during first $80\,s$ is
explanation:
As we know that,
Distance $=$ Area under velocity - time graph
$\therefore$ Total distance travelled in $80\,s$
Area of $\triangle O A B+$ Area of $\triangle B C D$
$=\left|\dfrac{1}{2}(A E \times O B)\right|+\left|\dfrac{1}{2}(C F \times B D)\right|$
$=\left|\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 40\right|+\left|\dfrac{1}{2} \times(-10) \times 40\right|$
$=200+200=400 m$
Total time taken, $t = 80\,s$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957667/preptracker/questions/q_106146_explanation_1764957667373.png Now, the average speed
$ = \frac{{{\text{ Total }}\,\,{\text{distance }}}}{{{\text{ Total }}\,\,{\text{time }}\,\,{\text{taken }}}} = \frac{{400}}{{80}} = 5\,m/s$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A body moves in a straight line with speed $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ for distance which are in ratio $1: 2$. Find the average speed.
explanation:
Given that, the distances travelled by the body are in ratio $1: 2$.
Let us suppose that $s$ is the distance, the body travels with speed $v_{1}$. Then, $2 s$ will be the distance travelled by the body with speed $v_{2}$. This scenario is shown in the line-diagram below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957654/preptracker/questions/q_106137_explanation_1764957653731.png For $A$ to $C$,
Average speed $=\dfrac{\text { Total distance travelled }}{\text { Total time taken }}$
$v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{s_{1}+s_{2}}{t_{1}+t_{2}}=\dfrac{s+2 s}{\dfrac{s}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{2 s}{v_{2}}}$
$=\dfrac{3 s}{s\left[\dfrac{1}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{2}{v_{2}}\right]}=\dfrac{3}{\left[\dfrac{v_{2}+2 v_{1}}{v_{1} v_{2}}\right]}=\dfrac{3 v_{1} v_{2}}{v_{2}+2 v_{1}}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: In the diagram shown, the displacement ${(x)}$ of particles is given as a function of time. Particle ${A}$ is moving under a constant velocity of ${9 {~m} / {s}}$. Particle ${B}$ is moving under variable acceleration. From time ${t=0 s}$ to ${t=6 s}$, the average velocity of the particle ${B}$ will be equal to
explanation:
Average velocity ${=\dfrac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time interval }}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951639/preptracker/questions/q_40436_explanation_1764951638481.png Displacement travel by ${A}$ and ${B}$ are equal in time interval of ${6 s}$.
So, the average velocity of ${B}$ is ${9 {~m} / {s}}$.
subject: Physics

question: A particle moving in a straight line covers half the distance with speed  $6\,\,m/s$. The other half is covered in two equal time intervals with speed $9\,\,m/s$ and   $15\,\,m/s$ respectively. The average speed of the particle during the motion is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950893/preptracker/questions/q_40388_explanation_1764950893096.png
$AB = {S_1} = 6{t_1}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$BD = {S_2} = 9t + 15t = 24t\quad \,\,(2)$
As per question $S_{1}=S_{2}$. Thus, from equations (1) and (2)
$\therefore 6 t_{1}=24 t \Rightarrow t_{1}=4 t$
Average speed $=\dfrac{\text { Total distance }}{\text { Total time }}=\dfrac{S_{1}+S_{2}}{t_{1}+2 t}$
$ = \frac{{24t + 24t}}{{4t + 2t}} = \frac{{48t}}{{6t}} = 8\,\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: The position of a particle moving along ${x}$-axis is given by ${x=3 t-4 t^{2}+t^{3}}$, where ${x}$ is in metre and ${t}$ in seconds. The average velocity of the particle in the time interval from ${t=2}$ seconds to ${t=4}$ seconds is
explanation:
Position of the particle is given by$x(t)=3 t-4 t^{2}+t^{3}$
Average velocity of the particle is
${v_{avg}} = \frac{{x\left( {{t_2}} \right) - x\left( {{t_1}} \right)}}{{{t_2} - {t_1}}}$
${v_{avg}} = \frac{{x(4) - x(2)}}{{4 - 2}}$
Now, $x(4) = 3(4) - 4{(4)^2} + {4^3} = 12\,m$
$x(2) = 3(2) - 4{(2)^2} + {2^3} = - 2 m$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {v_{avg}}{\text{ }} = \frac{{12 - ( - 2)}}{2} = \frac{{14}}{2} = 7\,m/s$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A runner starts from $O$ and goes to $O$ following path $O Q R O$ in $1 h$. What is net displacement and average speed?
explanation:
$\because$ Runner starts from $O$ and goes to $O$, so net displacement is zero.
$\because$ Average speed$
=\dfrac{\text { Total distance }}{\text { Total time }}=\dfrac{O Q+Q R+R O}{\text { Total time }}$
$=\dfrac{1 k m+(2 \pi r)(1 / 4)+1 k m}{1 h}$
$ = 2 + \frac{\pi }{2} = 3.57\,\,km{h^{ - 1}}$
So correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Find the average velocity when a particle complete the circle of radius $1 \mathrm{~m}$ in $10 \mathrm{~s}$.
explanation:
When a particle completes one revolution
in circular motion, then average displacement
travelled by particle is zero.
Hence, average velocity
$ = \frac{{{\rm{average}}\,\,{\rm{displacement}}}}{{\Delta \,t}}$
$ = \frac{0}{{\Delta t}} = 0$
subject: Physics

question: The position of an object moving along ${x}$-axis is given by ${x=a t^{2}+b t+c}$, where ${a, b}$, and ${c}$ are constant and ${t}$ is time in second. Find the average velocity from ${t=0}$ to ${t=2 s}$.
explanation:
Average Velocity ${=\dfrac{x(t=2)-x(t=0)}{2-0}}$
${=\dfrac{4 a+2 b+c-c}{2}=2 a+b}$.
subject: Physics

question: An object is moving in a straight line, according to the curve given in the graph, ${x(m)=t^{2}}$. Find the average velocity for the interval ${t=1 {~s}}$ to ${t=3 {~s}}$.
explanation:
${x(t=1)=1}$ and ${x(t=3)=9}$
Displacement ${=8 {~m}}$ and time ${=2 {~s}}$
${\Rightarrow\langle v\rangle=\dfrac{8}{2}=4 {~m} / {s}}$

question: An object moves with speed $v_{1}, v_{2}$ and $v_{3}$ along a line segment $A B, B C$ and $C D$ respectively as shown in figure. Where $A B=B C$ and $A D=3 A B$ then average speed of the object will be
subject: Physics

question: A person travels $x$ distance with velocity $v_{1}$ and then $x$ distance with velocity $v_{2}$ in the same direction. The average velocity of the person is $v$, then the relation between $v, v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ will be.
explanation:
Average velocity $=\dfrac{\text { Total displacement }}{\text { Total time }}$
Average velocity $=\dfrac{x+x}{T_{1}+T_{2}}$
Average velocity $=\dfrac{x+x}{\dfrac{x}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{x}{v_{2}}}=v$ $\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{v}$
subject: Physics

question: A vehicle travels $4\,km$ with speed of $3\,km{\rm{/}}h$ and another $4\,km$ with speed of $5\,km{\rm{/}}h$, then its average speed is
subject: Physics

question: A car travels a distance of '$x$' with speed $v_{1}$ and then same distance '$x$' with speed $v_{2}$ in the same direction. The average speed of the car is
explanation:
As, $t_{1}=\dfrac{x}{v_{1}}, t_{2}=\dfrac{x}{v_{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950996/preptracker/questions/q_39943_explanation_1764950995607.png Average speed $=\dfrac{\text { Length of both traversed }}{\text { total time }}$
$=\dfrac{(x+x)}{\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{2 x}{\dfrac{x}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{x}{v_{2}}}=\dfrac{2 v_{1} v_{2}}{x\left(v_{1} v_{2}\right)}$
$v_{a v}=\dfrac{2 v_{1} v_{2}}{\left(v_{1}+v_{2}\right)}$
subject: Physics

question: The velocity time graph of a body moving in a straight line is shown in figure. The ratio displacement to distance travelled by the body in time 0 to \[10\,s\] is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950990/preptracker/questions/q_39922_explanation_1764950990041.png In graph areas $A_{1}, A_{2}, A_{3}$ and $A_{4}$ are marked
$S=$ Displacement $=$ Vector - Sum of areas
$=A_{1}-A_{2}+A_{3}-A_{4}$
$=(8 \times 2)-(4 \times 2)+(4 \times 4)-(4 \times 2)$
$=16-8+16-8$
$ = 16\,m$
$d=$ distance $=$ algebraic sum of areas
$=16+8+16+8$
$ = 48\,m$
$\Rightarrow$ Required ratio $=\dfrac{s}{d}=\dfrac{16}{48}=\dfrac{1}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A body is moving along a straight line with initial velocity $v_{0}$. Its acceleration $a$ is constant. After $t$ seconds, its velocity becomes $v$. The average velocity of the body over the given time interval is
explanation:
$v=v_{0}+a t$
$v_{\text {ars }}=\dfrac{s_{\text {total }}}{t_{\text {time }}}=\bar{v}=\dfrac{v^{2}-v_{0}^{2}}{2 a t} .$
Correct option is (2).

question: A particle moves half distance with ${v_0}$. The remaining distance is covered with ${v_1}$ for half time and  with ${v_2}$ for the other half of the time. The  average speed of the particle is
explanation:
Let $S$ be the total distance travelled by the particle. Let ${t_1},{t_2}\,{\rm{and}}\,{t_3}$ be the times taken by the particle in the three parts.
${t_1} = \frac{{s/2}}{{{v_0}}},{t_2} = \frac{{{s_1}}}{{{v_1}}},{t_3} = \frac{{\frac{s}{2} - {s_1}}}{{{v_2}}}$
Given ${t_2} = {t_3} \Rightarrow {s_1} = \frac{{{v_1}s}}{{2\left( {{v_1} + {v_2}} \right)}},$
${t_2} = {t_3} = \frac{s}{{2\left( {{v_1} + {v_2}} \right)}}$
The average speed $\langle v\rangle = \frac{s}{{{t_1} + {t_2} + {t_3}}}$
$\langle v\rangle = \frac{{2{v_0}\left( {{v_1} + {v_2}} \right)}}{{\left( {{v_1} + {v_2} + 2{v_0}} \right)}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle having time period (in its circular motion) $\pi s,$ completes half revolution. Its mean speed is [given Radius of circle$ = 100\,cm$]
explanation:
The average or Mean speed is
$\langle v\rangle = \frac{{\pi R}}{T/2}$
= $\frac{{\pi \times 1}}{\pi/2}$ = $2.0 \,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A man leaves his house for a cycle ride. He comes back to his house after half-an-hour after covering a distance of one $km$. What is his average speed for the ride?
subject: Physics

question: If a particle travels $n$ equal distances with speeds ${v_1},\,{v_2},....{v_n}$ then the average speed $v$ of the  particle will be given by
explanation:
Let $S$ be the total distance travelled by the particle.
Average speed $ = \frac{s}{{{t_1} + {t_2} + ....}}$
${t_1} = \frac{{s/n}}{{{v_1}}},\,\,{t_2} = \frac{{s/n}}{{{v_2}}}......$
$v = \frac{s}{{\frac{1}{n}\left[ {\frac{s}{{{v_1}}} + \frac{s}{{{v_2}}} + ....} \right]}}$
subject: Physics

question: If a car covers 2/5 th of the total distance with ${v_1}$ speed and 3/5 th distance with ${v_2}$, then its average speed is
explanation:
Let $s$ be the total distance. ${t_1}$ and ${t_2}$ are the times in which the body covers distances
$\frac{{2s}}{5} \, {\rm{ and }} \, \frac{{3s}}{5}$ respectively
${v_1} = \frac{{2s/5}}{{{t_1}}}\quad {v_2} = \frac{{3s/5}}{{{t_2}}}$
${\rm{Average \, speed}} = \frac{s}{{{t_1} + {t_2}}} = \frac{{5{v_1}{v_2}}}{{3{v_1} + 2{v_2}}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves along the sides AB, BC, CD of a square of side 25 $m$ with a constant speed of  $15\,m/s$. Its average velocity is
explanation:
${\rm{Velocity = }}\frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Time}}}}$
Displacement $ = {\rm{25}}\,m$
${\rm{Time = }}\frac{{{\rm{Distance}}}}{{{\rm{speed}}}} = \frac{{75 \, m}} {{15 \ , m/s}} = 5 \, s$
${\rm{Velocity}} = \frac{{25 \, m}}{{5 \, s}} = 5 \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: If a particle travels a linear distance at speed ${v_1}$ and comes back along the same track at speed  ${v_2}$.
explanation:
Let 2$x$ is the total distance travelled by the particle
Average Speed
$ = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, distance}}}}{{{\rm{Total \, time}}}} = \frac{{2x}}{{\frac{x}{{{v_1}}} + \frac{x}{{{v_2}}}}} = \frac{{2{v_1}{v_2}}}{{{v_1} + {v_2}}}$
Average velocity$ = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Total \, time}}}}$
It comes back to its initial position.
Total displacement is zero. Hence, average velocity is zero.
subject: Physics

question: The numerical value of the ratio of average velocity to average speed is
explanation:
$\frac{{v + 0}}{2} = \frac{g}{2} \Rightarrow v = g$
As the distance is always either greater or equal to displacement.
$\therefore \,{\rm{Avg \, vel}} \le {\rm{ Avg \, speed}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\rm{Avg}}{\rm{.}}\,{\rm{vel}}}}{{{\rm{Avg}}{\rm{.}}\,{\rm{speed}}}} \le 1$

options.text: Equal to or less than one

question: A particle moving in a straight line covers half the distance with speed of $3\,m/s$. The other half of the distance is covered in two equal time intervals with speed of $4.5\,m/s$ and $7.5\,m/s$ respectively. The average speed of the particle during this motion is
explanation:
If ${t_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,2 \, {t_2}$ are the time taken by particle to cover first and second half distance respectively.
${{\rm{t}}_{\rm{1}}} = \frac{{x/2}}{3} = \frac{x}{6}$   (1)
${x_1} = 4.5 \, {t_2}\,{\rm{and}}\,{x_2} = 7.5 \, {t_2}$
So, ${x_1} + {x_2} = \frac{x}{2} \Rightarrow 4.5 \, t_2 + 7.5 \, t_2 = \frac{x}{2}$
${{\rm{t}}_{\rm{2}}} = \frac{x}{{24}}$   (2)
Total time $t = t_1 + 2{t_2} = \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{{12}} = \frac{x}{4}$
${v_{avg}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, distance}}}}{{{\rm{Total \, time}}}} = \frac{x}{{\frac{x}{4}}} = 4 \, m/\sec $
So, average speed $ = 4\,m/\sec $.
subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving along a semicircle of radius 10 $m$ in 5 seconds. The average velocity of the particle is
explanation:
${\rm{Velocity}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{particle}} = \frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{time}}}}$
$\frac{{{\rm{Diameter \, of \, circle}}}}{{\rm{5}}} = \frac{{2 \times 10}}{5} = 4 \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: Choose the correct statement :

options.text: The area of  displacement - time graph gives velocity
The slope of velocity - time graph gives acceleration
The slope of displacement - time graph gives  acceleration.
The area of velocity - time graph gives average velocity.

subject: Physics

question: A car starts from rest and moves with uniform acceleration $a$ on a straight road from time $t = 0\,{\rm{to}}\,t = T$. After that, a constant deceleration brings it to rest. In this process the average speed of the car is
explanation:
For first part,
$u = 0,t = T$ and acceleration $ = a$
$\therefore v = 0 + aT = aT\,{\rm{and}}\,{S_1} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}a{T^2} = \frac{1}{2}a{T^2}$
For second part,
$u = aT,$ retardation $ = {a_1},v = 0$ and time taken
$ = {T_1}({\rm{let}})$
$\therefore 0 = u - {a_1}{T_1} \Rightarrow aT = {a_1}{T_1}$
and from ${v^2} - {u^2} = 2a{S_2} \Rightarrow {S_2} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2{a_1}}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{a^2}{T^2}}}{{{a_1}}}$
${S_2} = \frac{1}{2}aT \times {T_1}\quad \left( {As\,{a_1} = \frac{{aT}}{{{T_1}}}} \right)$
$\therefore {v_{av}} = \frac{{{S_1} + {S_2}}}{{T + {T_1}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}a{T^2} + \frac{1}{2}aT \times {T_1}}}{{T + {T_1}}}$
$ = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}aT\left( {T + {T_1}} \right)}}{{T + {T_1}}} = \frac{1}{2}aT.$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is projected vertically upward with velocity $u$ from a point $A$, when it returns to the point of projection
explanation:
The average speed $ = \frac{{2H}}{T} = \frac{{{\rm{Total}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{distance}}}}{{{\rm{Time}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{of}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{flight}}}}$
$H = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}}$ (maximum height reached by the body)
$T = \frac{{2u}}{g}$ (Time of flight)
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{2 \cdot \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{2u}}{g}} \right)}} = \frac{u}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves along a straight line and its velocity  depends on time as $v = 4t - {t^2}$. Then for first 5 $s$ the
explanation:
$v = 4t - {t^2}$
Average velocity is
$\left\langle v \right\rangle = \frac{{\int\limits_0^5 {vdt} }}{5} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^5 {{{(4t - t)}^2}dt} }}{5} = \frac{5}{3} \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: Consider a body under S.H.M with amplitude  equal to 4 $cm$. The displacement of the body as a function of time is $x = 4cm\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{2}t} \right)$. The average speed and average velocity of the body in time from $t = 0s\,{\rm{to}}\,t = 2s\,$ are
explanation:
${\rm{Avg}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{{\rm{Distance}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = \frac{{8 \, cm}}{{2 \, s}} = 4 \, cm/s$
${\rm{Avg}}\,{\rm{velocity}} = \frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = 0$
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of a body as a function of time is $v = 10 - 5t$. Find the average velocity of the body in the time interval from $0s\,{\rm{to}}\,2s$.
explanation:
Given that $v = 10 - 5t$
The average velocity is $\left\langle v \right\rangle = \frac{{\int_0^2 {(10 - 5t)dt} }}{{2 - 0}}$
$\left\langle v \right\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\int_0^2 {10\,dt - 5\int_0^2 {t\,dt} )} } \right] = 5 \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: The average velocity of a freely falling body $({\rm{with}}\,u = 0)$ is numerically equal to half of the  acceleration due to gravity. The velocity of the body as it reaches the ground is
explanation:
${\rm{Avg}}{\rm{.velocity}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, distance}}}}{{{\rm{total \, time}}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}g{t^2}}}{t} = \frac{{gt}}{2}$
Given that $\frac{{gt}}{2} = \frac{g}{2} \Rightarrow t = 1s$
Velocity after reaching ground $ = gt = g$
subject: Physics

question: Between two stations, a train accelerates from rest uniformly at first, then moves with constant velocity, and finally retards uniformly to come to rest. If the ratio of time taken is $1:8:1$ and the maximum speed attained be $60\,km/h$, then what is the average speed over the whole journey?
explanation:
Given that the ratio of the times is
${t_1}:{t_2}:{t_3} = 1:8:1$
Let ${t_1} = k,{t_2} = 8k,{t_3} = k,$ $a$ is the acceleration and retardation in first and third parts.
${s_1} = \frac{1}{2}a{t_1}^2 = \frac{{a{k^2}}}{2},{v_1} = a{t_1} = ak$
${s_2} = {v_1}{t_2} = 8a{k^2}$
${s_3} = {v_2}{t_3} - \frac{1}{2}a{t_3}^2({v_2} = {v_1} = ak)$
${s_3} = \frac{{a{k^2}}}{2}$
$\langle v\rangle = \frac{{{s_1} + {s_2} + {s_3}}}{{10k}} = \frac{{9a{k^2}}}{{10k}} = \frac{{9ak}}{{10}} = 54\,km/h$
(Given that ${v_1} = ak = 60\,km/h$)
subject: Physics

question: For a body moving with uniform acceleration ‘$a$’, initial and final velocities in a time interval ‘$t$’ are $u$ and $v$ respectively. The average velocity in the time interval ‘$t$’ is
explanation:
The velocity of the body is $v = u + at$
As the velocity function is given
The average velocity is
$\left\langle v \right\rangle = \frac{{\int\limits_0^{t} vdt}}{t - 0} = \frac{1}{t}\int\limits_0^{t} {\left( {u + at} \right)dt} $
$ = \frac{1}{t} \left[ {\int\limits_0^{t} udt + a}\int\limits_0^{t} tdt \right] = u + \frac{{at}}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: Velocity of a body is given in the graph. Find the average speed and average velocity of the body from \[t = 0 \,s\] to \[12 \,s\]
explanation:
From $t = 0s\,{\rm{to}}\,6s$ the body is travelling in one direction.
From $t = 0s\,{\rm{to}}\,12s$ the body changes its direction at $t = 6s$
${\rm{Distance}} = \left[ {\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4 + 2 \times 4 + \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right]$
$ + \left[ {\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4 + 2 \times 4 + \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right] = 32 \, m$
${\rm{Displacement}} = \left[ {\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4 + 2 \times 4 + \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right]$
$- \left[ {\frac {1}{2} \times 2 \times 4 + 2 \times 4 + \frac {1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right] = 0$
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{velocity}} = {\rm{ }}\frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = \frac{0}{{12 \, s}} = 0$
${\rm{Average \, speed}} = \frac{{{\rm{Distance}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = \frac{{32 \, m}}{{12 \, s}} = \frac{8}{3} \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: \[s-t\] graph of a particle in motion is shown in figure. Calculate average speed and average velocity.
explanation:
The given graph is between $s$ and $t$
${\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{distance}} = 20 + 20 + 20 + 20 = 80\,m$
Total displacement $ = 0$ (Finally the body position is zero at $t = 16\,s$)
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{80 \, m}}{{16 \, s}} = 5 \, m{s^{ - 1}}$
Average velocity $ = 0$
subject: Physics

question: Among the four graphs, there is only one graph for which average velocity over the time interval (0, \[T\]) can vanish for a suitable \[T\]. Which one is it?
explanation: In the graph 2, for one value of displacement, there are two timings. As a result of it, for one time, the average velocity is positive and for other time is equivalent negative. Due to it, the average velocity  can vanish for two time intervals.
subject: Physics

question: A passenger arriving in a new town wishes to go from the station to a hotel located 10 $km$ away on a straight road from the station. A dishonest cabman takes him along a circuitons path 23 $km$ long and reaches the hotel in 28 min. What is the average speed of the taxi and the magnitude of average velocity respectively (in $km/h$)?
explanation:
Given, shortest distance between the station and the hotel $ = 10\,km$
$\therefore $ Displacement of the taxi $ = 10\,km$
Distance travelled by the taxi $ = 23\,km$
Time taken by the taxi $ = 28\,\min = \frac{7}{{15}}h$
${\rm{Avg}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, distance \, travelled}}}}{{{\rm{Total \, time \, taken}}}}$
$ = \frac{{23}}{{\left( {7/15} \right)}} = 49.3\,km/h
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{velocity}} = \frac{{{\rm{ Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Total time }}}}$
$ = \frac{{10}}{{\left( {7/15} \right)}} = 21.43\,km/h$
subject: Physics

question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Distance is always positive.
II. Displacement can be positive, negative or zero
III. Average speed is always positive.
IV. Path length is a vector quantity whereas displacement scalar quantity
explanation: Path length is a scalar quantity as it has only magnitude but no direction whereas  displacement has magnitude as well as direction,  so displacement is vector quantity. So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
Study the following statements.
I. The unit of average speed is same as that of velocity
II. If one or more coordinates of an object changes with time, we say that the object is at rest with respect to the given reference frame
Which of the following statement (s) is /are correct?
explanation: Only statement  I  is correct but II is incorrect and since if none of the coordinates change, then the object is said to be at rest with respect to the given reference frame. So options (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
The average speed of an object is greater than or equal to the magnitude of the average velocity over a given time interval.
The two are equal only if the distance and displacement are same
explanation: Option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
The speedometer of an automobile measures the instantaneous speed of the automobile.
Average velocity is equal to total displacement divided by total time taken.
explanation: Option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The average velocity of the object over an interval of time is either smaller than or equal to the average speed of the object over the same interval.

Reason: Velocity is a vector quantity and speed is a scalar quantity.
explanation: As displacement is either smaller or equal to distance but never be greater than distance. Velocity is a vector and speed is a scalar. So  option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The average speed of a body over a given interval of time is equal to magnitude of the average velocity of the body in the same interval of time if a body moves in a straight line in one direction.

Reason: Because in this case distance travelled by a body is equal to the magnitude of displacement of the body.
explanation: When a body moves on a straight path in one direction then the value of distance and displacement remains same so that average speed equals the average velocity for a given time interval. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The speedometer of an automobile measures the average speed of the automobile.

Reason: Average velocity is equal to total displacement per total time taken.
explanation:
Speed meter measures instantaneous speed of automobile. ${v_{avg}} = \frac{{\Delta s}}{{\Delta t}}$
So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A car moves from $A$ to $B$ with a speed of 30 $kmph$ and from $B$ to $A$ with a speed of 20 $kmph$. What is the average speed of the car?
explanation:
Let $S$ be distance between $A$ and $B$.
Let ${t_1}$ be time taken by the car to move from $A$ to $B$ with speed ${v_1}$ and ${t_2}$ be time taken by the car to move from $B$ to $A$ with speed ${v_2}$. Then
${t_1} = \frac{S}{{{v_1}}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{t_2} = \frac{S}{{{v_2}}}$
Average speed of the car
${v_{avg}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{distance}}\,{\rm{travelled}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{time}}\,{\rm{taken}}}} = \frac{{2S}}{{{t_1} + {t_2}}}$
$ = \frac{{2S}}{{\frac{S}{{{v_1}}} + \frac{S}{{{v_2}}}}} = \frac{{2{v_1}{v_2}}}{{{v_1} + {v_2}}}$
Here, ${v_1} = 30\,kmph,{v_2} = 20\,kmph$
$\therefore {v_{avg}} = \frac{{2 \times 30 \times 20}}{{30 + 20}} = 24\,kmph$
subject: Physics

question: A cyclist starts from the centre $O$ of a circular park of radius one kilometre, reaches the edge $P$ of the park, then cycles along the circumference and returns to the centre along QO as shown in the figure. If the round trip takes ten minutes, the net displacement and average speed of the cyclist (in metre and kilometre per hour) is
explanation:
Since the initial position coincides with the final position, net displacement of the cyclist=zero
Average speed of the cyclist
$ = \frac{{{\rm{total}}\,{\rm{distance}}\,{\rm{travelled}}}}{{{\rm{total}}\,{\rm{time}}\,{\rm{taken}}}}$
$ = \frac{{OP + PQ + QO}}{{10}}km/\min = \frac{{1 + \frac{\pi }{2} \times 1 + 1}}{{10}}$
$ = \frac{{\pi + 4}}{{20}}km/\min = \frac{{\pi + 4}}{{20}} \times 60\,km/h$
$ = 21.4\,km/h$
subject: Physics

question: A body falls freely for 10 sec. Its average velocity during this journey  $({\rm{take}}\,g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}})$
subject: Physics

question: A car moves from $X$ to $Y$ with a uniform speed ${v_u}$ and returns to $X$ with a uniform speed ${v_d}$. The  average speed for this round trip is
explanation:
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total \, distance \, travelled}}}}{{{\rm{Time \, taken }}}}$
Let ${t_{1\,}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{t_2}$ be time taken by the car to go from $X$ to $Y$ and then from $Y$ to $X$ respectively.
${\rm{Then, }} \, {t_1} + {t_2} = \frac{{XY}}{{{v_u}}} + \frac{{XY}}{{{v_d}}} = XY\left( {\frac{{{v_u} + {v_d}}}{{{v_u}{v_d}}}} \right)$
Total distance travelled $ = XY + XY = 2XY$
Therefore, average speed of the car for this round trip is
${v_{av}} = \frac{{2XY}}{{XY\left( {\frac{{{v_u} + {v_d}}}{{{v_u}{v_d}}}} \right)}}$
or ${v_{av}} = \frac{{2{v_u}{v_d}}}{{{v_u} + {v_d}}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle having time period (in its circular motion) $2\pi s$ completes half revolution. Its mean speed is [given Radius of circle $ = 50\,cm$]
explanation:
The average or Mean speed is
$\langle v\rangle = \frac{{\pi R}}{T/2} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5} {\pi} = 0.5\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A point traversed $3/{4^{th}}$ of the circle of radius $R$ in time $t$. The magnitude of the average velocity of the particle in this time interval is
explanation: ${v_{av}} = \frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Time}}}} = \frac{{{\sqrt 2} R}}{t}$
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass $m$ moving along a straight line covers half the distance with a speed of $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The remaining half of the distance is  covered in two equal time intervals with a speed of $3\,m{s^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{and}}\,5m{s^{ - 1}}$ respectively. The average  speed of the particle for the entire journey is
explanation:
Let $2 \, S$ be the total distance travelled by the body:
Let ${t_1}$ be the time taken by the body to travel first half of the distance.
$\therefore \,{t_1} = \frac{S}{2}$
Let ${t_2}$ be the time taken by the body for each time interval for the remaining half journey.
$\therefore \,S = 3{t_2} + 5{t_2}\,{\rm{or}}\,{t_2} = S/8$
${\rm{Average}}\,{\rm{speed}} = \frac{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{distance}}\,{\rm{travelled}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{time}}\,{\rm{taken}}}}$
$ = \frac{{2S}}{{{t_1} + 2{t_2}}} = \frac{{2S}}{{\frac{S}{2} + 2\left( {\frac{S}{8}} \right)}} = \frac{8}{3}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question:
A meter scale is given along a path, as shown in the diagram. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951083/preptracker/questions/q_91289_question_1764951082805.png  A point object starts from $O,$ moves to $B,$ then
turns back and reaches at point $C.$ Choose the
correct option.
explanation:
Displacement is ${\overrightarrow{O C}=-10 \hat{i}}$
Distance is ${=O B+B O+O C=90 {~m}}$
Position vector of ${C}$ with respect to ${A}$ is
${\overrightarrow{A C}=10 \hat{i}}$

options.text: Position vector of ${C}$ with respect to ${A}$ is ${-10 \hat{i} m}$.

subject: Physics

question: A clock has $75\,cm,$ $60\,cm$ long second hand and minute hand, respectively. In 30 minutes duration, the tip of second hand will travel $x$ distance more than the tip of minute hand. The value of $x$ in meter is nearly (Take $\pi=3.14$ )
explanation:
30 minutes
$\Rightarrow 30$ revolutions by second hand
$\therefore n_{s}=30$
and $\dfrac{1}{2}$ revolution by minute hand
$\therefore {n_m} = \frac{1}{2}$
$x = {d_s} - {d_m}$
$ \Rightarrow x = 2\pi {r_s}{n_s} - 2\pi {r_m}{n_m}$
$x = 2\pi \times 75 \times 30 - 2\pi \times 60 \times \frac{1}{2} \approx 139.4\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A cyclist starts from the point $P$ of a circular ground of radius $2\,\,km$ and travels along its circumference to the point $S$. The displacement of a cyclist is
explanation:
Displacement is given by $=\sqrt{2} r$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951730/preptracker/questions/q_40381_explanation_1764951729590.png
$\therefore d = \sqrt 2 \times 2 = \sqrt 8 \,km$
subject: Physics

question: A length of path ${A C B}$ is $1500\,m$ and the length of the path ${A D B}$ is $2100\,m$. Two particles start from point ${A}$ simultaneously around the track ${A C B D A}$. One of them travels the track in clockwise sense and other in anticlockwise sense with their respective constant speeds. After $12\,s$ from the start, the first time they meet at the point ${B}$. After minimum time (in $s$) in which they meet first at point ${B}$, will they again meet at the point ${B}$ is time ${t_{\min }=(12)^{x} {~s}}$. The value of ${x}$ is
explanation:
The velocity of first particle is ${v_{1}=\dfrac{1500}{12} {~ms}^{-1}}$ and the velocity of second particle is ${v_{2}=\dfrac{2100}{12} {~ms}^{-1}}$. Let after completing ${n_{1}}$ and ${n_{2}}$ trips, they will again meet at the point ${B}$.
$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{3600\,{n_1}}}{{{v_1}}} = \frac{{3600\,{n_2}}}{{{v_2}}} = t$
Or $\frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}} = \frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{{1500}}{{12}}}}{{\frac{{2100}}{{12}}}} = \frac{{1500}}{{2100}} = \frac{5}{7}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {t_{\min }} = \frac{{3600 \times 5}}{{{v_1}}} = \frac{{3600 \times 5}}{{1500}} \times 12$
$ = 144\,s = {(12)^x}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = 2$
subject: Physics

question: An athlete runs along a circular track of diameter $80\,m$. The distance travelled and the magnitude of displacement of the athlete when he covers ${\frac{3}{4}^{th}}$ of the circle is (in ${m}$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956117/preptracker/questions/q_39995_explanation_1764956116986.png Distance $ = \frac{{3\pi r}}{2} = 60\,\pi $
Displacement $ = \sqrt 2 R = 40\sqrt 2 $
subject: Physics

question: From the \(v-t\) graph shown, the ratio of distance to displacement in \(25 s\) of motion is
explanation:
Area under the graph from $t=0$ to $t=20 \mathrm{sec}$,
$A_{1}=+\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10+5 \times 10+\dfrac{1}{2}(20+10)\right.$
$\left.\times 5+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 20\right]=+200 \mathrm{~m}$
Area under the graph from $t=20$ to $t=25 \mathrm{sec}$,
$A_{2}=-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 20-50 m\left|A_{1}\right|+\left|A_{2}\right|$
So distance covered
$ = \left| {{A_1}} \right| + \left| {{A_2}} \right|(200 + 50)m = 250\,m$
Displacement
$=A_{1}+A_{2}=200+(-50) m=150 m$
$\therefore$ Ratio of distance to displacement is $\dfrac{250}{150}=\dfrac{5}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of car in moving from $O$ to $P$ and its displacement in moving from $P$ to $Q$ are
explanation:
Displacement $=\Delta x=x_{2}-x_{1}$
For journey, displacement of car moving from $O$ to $P$,
${x_2} = + 360\,m\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {x_1} = 0\,m$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta x = {x_2} - {x_1} = 360 - 0 = + 360\,m$
For journey, displacement in moving from $P$ to $Q$
${x_2} = + 240\,m\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {x_1} = + 360\,m$
$ \Rightarrow = \Delta x = {x_2} - {x_1} = 240 - 360 = - 120\,m$
Here, -ve sign implies that the displacement is in -ve direction i.e., towards left.
subject: Physics

question: An athlete completes one round of a circular track of radius R in 40 sec. What will be his  displacement at the end of 3 min?
explanation: The body completes for and half rotations and hence the displacement is equal $2R$ (shortest distance between the initial and final position)
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following can be zero, when a particle is in motion for sometime?
explanation: Displacement  of a particle may be zero,because final position of the particle may coincide with its initial position.
subject: Physics

question: Mr. Eswar drives his car at uniform speed from bottom of a mountain to the top in 30 minutes. At the beginning the speedometer of his car shows 6225 $km$, while on reaching the top it reads 6285 $km$. The total distance covered is
explanation: Total distance covered $ = $ Final reading-Initial reading  $ = 6285 - 6255 = 60\,km$.
subject: Physics

question: The location of a particle has changed. What can we say about the displacement and the distance covered by the particle?
explanation: When location of a particle has changed,it must have covered some distance and undergone some displacement.

options.text: One is $ + ve$, other is $ - ve$

subject: Physics

question: A drunked walking in a narrow lane takes 8 steps forward and 6 steps backward, followed again by 8 steps forward and so on. Each step is 1 $m$ long and requires 1 $s$. Determine how long the drunkard takes to fall in a pit 10 $m$ away from the start.
explanation:
When the drunkard walks 8 steps forward and 6 steps backward, the displacement in the first 14 steps $ = 8 \, m - 6 \, m = 2 \, m$
Time taken for first 14 steps = 14 $s$
Remaining distance he has to cover is 8 $m$. So if he makes 8 steps forward then he falls in the pit. Total time he takes is $14 \, s + 8 \, s = 22 \, s$.
subject: Physics

question: A body is moving along the circumference of a circle of radius ‘$R$’ and completes half of the revolution. Then the ratio of its displacement to distance is
explanation:
The distance travelled is $\pi R$
The displacement is $2R$
$\frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Distance}}}} = \frac{2}{\pi }$
subject: Physics

question: An athlete completes one round of a circular track of radius $R$ in 40 sec. What will be his displacement at the end of 2 min 20 sec?
explanation:
Total time of motion is ${\rm{2}}\,{\rm{min}}\,{\rm{20}}\,{\rm{sec = 140}}\,{\rm{sec}}$
As time period of circular motion is 40 sec, in 140 sec, athlete will complete 3.5 revolution, i.e., he will be at diametrically opposite point, i.e. Displacement $= 2 \, R$.
subject: Physics

question: Velocity of a body is shown in the graph. The graph is a semicircle in shape. Find the displacement and distance in time from 0 \[s\] to 4 \[s\].
explanation:
Displacement $ = $ distance (as the body is travelling in one direction though out time)
$ = {\rm{Area}} = \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2} = \frac{{\pi {r_v}{r_t}}}{2}$
$ = \frac{{\pi \times 10 \times 2}}{2} = 10 \, \pi m$
Here ${r_v} - $ velocity radius, ${r_t} - $ time radius
subject: Physics

question: Velocity of a body starting from rest is shown in the diagram. Find the displacement and distance travelled by the body. From \[t = 0 \, s\] to \[8 \, s\]
explanation:
As velocity is positive the body is moving in only one direction throughout the time. In this case displacement and distance are same.
Distance $ = $ Displacement $ = $ Area of the graph
$ = \left( {\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right) + (4 \times 4) + \left( {\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4} \right)$
$ = 24\,m$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The slope of displacement-time of a body moving with high velocity is steeper than the slope of displacement-time graph of a body with low velocity.

Reason: Slope of displacement-time graph = Velocity of the body.
explanation: Slope of displacement-time measures velocity of an object. So option (1) is correct

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Displacement of a body is vector sum of the area under velocity-time graph.

Reason: Displacement is a vector quantity.
explanation:
Displacement = velocity $ \times $ time.
Displacement is a equal to the sum of the area under velocity-time graph. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Displacement of a body may be zero when distance travelled by it is not zero.

Reason: The displacement is the longest distance between initial and final position.
explanation: The displacement is the shortest distance between initial and final position. When final position of a body coincides with its initial position, displacement is zero, but the distance travelled is not zero. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
