question: A wheel of radius 1 $m$ rolls forward half a revolution on a horizontal ground.  The magnitude of the displacement of the point of the wheel initially in contact with the ground is
explanation:
Horizontal distance by the wheel in half revolution $ = \pi R$
So the displacement of the point which was initially in contact with ground
$ = AA' = \sqrt {{{\left( {\pi R} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {2R} \right)}^2}} $
$ = R\sqrt {{\pi ^2} + 4} $
$ = \sqrt {{\pi ^2} + 4} \, ({\rm{as}} \, R = 1 \, m)$
subject: Physics

question: The position of a particle along $x$-axis at time $t$ is given by $x = 2 + t - 3{t^2}$. The displacement and the distance travelled in the interval $t = 0\,{\rm{and}}\,t = 1$ are respectively
explanation:
$x = 2 + t - 3{t^2},\,v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 1 - 6t,$ velocity will become zero at time, $0 = 1 - 6 {t_0}\,{\rm{or}}\,{t_0} = \frac{1}{6}s,$
Since the given time $t = 1\,s$ is greater than
${t_0} = \frac{1}{6}s,$
${\rm{distance}} > \left| {{\rm{displacement}}} \right|$
Displacement $S = {x_f} - {x_i}$
$ = (2 + 1 - 3) - (2 + 0 - 0) = - 2\,m$
Distance $d = \left| {{S_0} - {t_0}} \right| + \left| {{S_t}_{ - {t_0}}} \right|$
$ = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2\left| a \right|}} + \frac{1}{2}\left| a \right|{\left( {t - {t_0}} \right)^2}$
Comparing $v = 1 - 6t$ with $v = u + ut,$ we have
$u = 1\,m{\rm{/}}s\,{\rm{and}}\,a = - 6\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
$\therefore {\rm{Distance}} = \frac{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times 6}} + \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times {\left( {1 - \frac{1}{6}} \right)^2}$
$ = 2.1\,m$
subject: Physics

question: An insect crawls a distance of $4 \, m$ along north in $10 \, s$ and then a distance of $3 \, m$ along east in $5 \, s$. The average velocity of the insect is
explanation:
${v_{av}} = \frac{{{\rm{Displacement}}}}{{{\rm{Time}}}}$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {{{\left( 4 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 3 \right)}^2}} }}{{10 + 5}} = \frac{1}{3}m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A person moves towards east for $4 \, m$, then towards north for $3 \, m$ and then moves vertically up by $5 \, m$.  What is his distance now from the starting point?
explanation:
Distance from starting point
$\sqrt {{{\left( 3 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 4 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 5 \right)}^2}} = 5\sqrt 2 \,m$
subject: Physics

question: Match the terms related to velocity and speed in Column I with their corresponding definitions in Column II. Choose the correct answer using the codes given below.
explanation:
Instantaneous velocity is defined at a particular instant and is given by the slope of the tangent to the position-time graph at that instant, matching with Q.
Average velocity is defined as the total displacement divided by the total time taken, which matches with R .Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of the instantaneous velocity, matching with S . Average speed is calculated as the total path length divided by the total time taken, matching with P. So correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - R, D - S
A - R, B - P, C - Q, D - S
A - S, B - R, C - P, D - Q
A - Q, B - R, C - S, D - P

subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The velocity at a particular instant is equal to the slope of the tangent to the position-time graph at that instant.
Instantaneous velocity is always equal to instantaneous speed.
explanation: Statement A is true because the slope of the tangent to the position-time graph gives the rate of change of position, which is the velocity at that instant. Statement B is true as well because magnitude of instantaneous velocity and speed are same. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The instantaneous speed is always equal to the magnitude of instantaneous velocity.

Reason: Instantaneous speed, a scalar quantity, considers only the magnitude of the velocity vector at a specific instant.
explanation: Instantaneous speed is the absolute value of instantaneous velocity. It measures how fast an object is moving at a particular moment without considering its direction of motion, which is exactly what the reason states.Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: The displacement-time graphs of two moving particles make angles of $30^{\circ}$ and $45^{\circ}$ with the time axis. The ratio of their velocities is
explanation:
Velocity is given by the slope of displacement time graph. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952036/preptracker/questions/q_106163_explanation_1764952036076.png $\tan \theta=$ velocity $=$ slope of displacement - time graphs.
For $1^{\text {st }}$ particle
$v_{1}($ velocity $)=\tan \theta_{1}$
For $2^{\text {nd }}$ particle
$v_{2}=\tan \theta_{2}$
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{\tan \theta_{1}}{\tan \theta_{2}}=\dfrac{\tan 30^{\circ}}{\tan 45^{\circ}}=1: \sqrt{3}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A particle covers a distance from $A$ to $B$ over a period of time; the distance versus time plot is shown below. Then which of the following is true for the motion of the particle?
explanation:
The distance versus time plot for movement of a particle from $A$ and $B$ is shown in the diagram. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952025/preptracker/questions/q_106130_explanation_1764952025502.png From graph, it is evident that slope of distance time graph for position $A$ to $C$ and $D$ to $B$ is always positive, therefore both the average speed and instantaneous speed are not always zero, therefore, option (1) is not correct.
Average velocity
$=\dfrac{\text { Total displacement from } A \text { to } B}{\text { Total time interval }}$
Since, total displacement depends on straight distance between initial and final points, hence, displacement between $A$ to $B$ always non zero, therefore the average speed is always non zero. Since, no distance is travelled between point $C$ to $D$ instantaneous speed at every instant between $C$ to $D$ is zero. Hence, option(2) is correct and option (3) and (4) are incorrect. So, the correct option is (2)

options.text: Both average speed and instantaneous speed are always zero
Average speed is always non-zero but instantaneous speed can be zero
Instantaneous speed is always non-zero but average speed can be zero
Both average speed and instantaneous speed are always non-zero.

subject: Physics

question: The graph shows position as a function of time for two trains running on parallel tracks. Which one of the following statements is true?
explanation:
Slope of curve ${B}$ and slope of curve ${A}$ is same before time ${T_{B}}$ only.
Acceleration of ${A}$ is always zero, whereas that of ${B}$ is not zero.

options.text: At time ${t_{B}}$, both trains have the same velocity.
Both trains have the same velocity at some time after ${t_{B}}$.
Both trains have the same velocity at some time before ${t_{B}}$.
Somewhere on the graph, both trains have the same acceleration.

subject: Physics

question: The displacement ${x}$ of a body in motion is given by ${x=a \sin (\omega t+\theta)}$. The time at which the displacement is maximum is
explanation:
Displacement ${(x)}$ of a body in motion $x = a\sin \,(\omega t + \theta )$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\omega \,\,\cos \,\,(\omega t + \theta )$
For maximum displacement, put ${\dfrac{d x}{d t}=0}$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad \cos (\omega t+\theta)=0=\cos \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \omega t+\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \omega t=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\theta \\& \therefore \quad t=\dfrac{\pi}{2 \omega}-\dfrac{\theta}{\omega}\end{aligned}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following graphs cannot possibly represent one-dimensional motion of a particle?
explanation:
Graph (I) does not represent the one dimensional motion, because total distance
covered by a particle always increases with time.
Graph (II) and Graph (III) do not represent the
one-dimensional motion,because at a particular time, there cannot be two values of position or velocity.
Graph (IV) is not possible, because speed can
never be negative.
subject: Physics

question: The distance covered by an object (in metre) in given by $s=8 t^{3}-7 t^{2}+5 t$, find its speed at $t=2 \mathrm{sec}$.
explanation:
We know that,
$v=\dfrac{d s}{d t}$
Here, $\quad s=8 t^{3}=-7 t^{2}+5 t$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{d s}{d t}=24 t^{2}-14 t+5$
$\because$ At $t=2 s$,
$v = 24{(2)^2} - 14(2) + 5 = 73\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: The position ${(x)}$ and time ${(t)}$ of a particle in one-dimensional motion vary, as shown in the following figure. How many times the particle changes its direction?
explanation:
${\dfrac{d x}{d t}=0}$; particle in at rest
${\dfrac{d x}{d t}>0 ;}$ velocity is positive
${\dfrac{d x}{d t} < 0 ;}$ velocity is negative.
From the given graph velocity of the particle becomes zero nine times. So correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question:
The position-time graphs for two students \(A\) and \(B\) returning from the school to their homes are shown in figure.
(A) \(A\) lives closer to the school
(B) \(B\) lives closer to the school
(C) \(A\) takes lesser time to reach home
(D) \(A\) travels faster than \(B\)
(E) \(B\) travels faster than \(A\). Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
explanation:
As the slope of $x-t$ graph gives speed and slope of $B$ is more than slope of $A$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951010/preptracker/questions/q_39987_explanation_1764951009641.png Thus, the speed of $B$ is more than $A$.
As $x_{1} < x_{2}$, so $A$ lives closer to school.
Hence, option $A$ and $E$ are correct.
subject: Physics

question: Match Column-I with Column-II. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
A-Q: The $x-t$ graph may be described by the equation $x=c t^{2}$ where $e$ is a constant.
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=2 c \cdot t$ which represents a straight line passing through the origin.
B-S: The $x-t$ graph may be described by the equation $x=c / t_{3}$ where $c$ is a constant.
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-\dfrac{c}{t^{2}}$ therefore $v-t$ graph is correctly shown in option S.
C-R:
From the $x-t$ graph,
$v=$ Slope of $v-t$ graph $=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=$ positive
constant in the first half and negative constant in the second half.
D-P: The $x-t$ graph is a straight line with positive slope.
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=$ slope of $x-t$ graph $=$ positive constant.
subject: Physics

question: The distance travelled by a particle is related to time $t$ as $x=4 t^{2}$ The velocity of the particle at $t=5 s$ is
explanation:
Given, $x=4 t^{2}$
$v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=8 t$ at $t = 5\,\,\sec $
$v = 8 \times 5 = 40\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: The position of a particle related to time is given by $x=\left(5 t^{2}-4 t+5\right) m$. The magnitude of velocity of the particle at $t=2 s$ will be
subject: Physics

question: The distance travelled by an object in time $t$ is given by $s=(2.5) t^{2}$. The instantaneous speed of the object at $t = 5\,s$ will be
explanation:
Distance travelled by object, $s=(2.5) t^{2}$.
Instantaneous speed is, ${v_{ins{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{{ds}}{{dt}}$
${v_{ins{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {2.5{t^2}} \right) = 5t\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
At, $t = 5\,\sec ,{v_{ins{\rm{ }}}} = 5(5) = 25\,m{s^{ - 1}}$.
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The instantaneous velocity does not depend on instantaneous position vector.

Reason: The instantaneous velocity and average velocity of a particle are always same.
explanation: Instantaneous velocity depends solely on the rate of change of position with respect to time, and not on the position itself. Instantaneous velocity and average velocity are not always the same. Average velocity is calculated over a finite time interval, while instantaneous velocity refers to the velocity at a single point in time. (i.e, at an instant) So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A particle moves along a straight line $OX$.At a time $t$ (in seconds) the distance $x$ (in metres) of the particle from $O$ is given by $x=20+14 t-t^{3}$. How long would the particle travel before coming to rest:
explanation:
Given condition is $x=20+14 t-t^{3}$Differentiating both side
$\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\dfrac{d\left(20+14 t-t^{3}\right.}{d t}$
$V=0+14-3 t^{2} \Rightarrow 0=0+14-3 t^{2}$ (body comes to rest)
$-14=-3 t^{2}$
$\left(\dfrac{14}{3}\right)^{1 / 2}=t \Rightarrow(4.66)^{1 / 2} \approx(5)^{1 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: The coordinates of a moving particle at any time $t$ are given by $x=\alpha t^{3}$ and $y=\beta t^{3}$. The speed of the particle at time $t$ is given by:
explanation:
$x=\alpha t^{3}, y=\beta t^{3}$
$\dfrac{d x}{d t}=3 \alpha t^{2}, \dfrac{d x}{d t}=3 \beta t^{2}$
$V=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{d y}{d t}\right)^{2}}, V=\sqrt{\left(3 \alpha t^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(3 \beta t^{2}\right)^{2}}$
$V=3 t^{2} \sqrt{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Velocity is given by $v=4 t(1-2 t)$, then find the value of time at which velocity is maximum.
explanation:
When velocity is maximum acceleration should be zero
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d v}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow 4-16 t=0$
$t=\dfrac{1}{4} \mathrm{sec}$
subject: Physics

question: The engine of a train passes an electric pole with a velocity ‘$u$’ and the last compartment of the train crosses the same pole with a velocity $v$. Then the velocity with which the mid-point of the train passes the pole is
explanation:
Let $l$ be the total length of the train and $v'$ be the velocity of the midpoint of train
$v{'^2} - {u^2} = 2a\frac{l}{2}$   (1)
$v' - u{'^2} = 2a.l$   (2)
From (1) and (2) we get $v' = \sqrt {\frac{{{v^2} + {u^2}}}{2}} $
subject: Physics

question: The displacement-time graphs of two particles $A$ and $B$ are straight lines making angles of $30^\circ \,{\rm{and}}\,45^\circ $ respectively with the time axis. If the velocity of $A$ is ${v_A}$ and that of $B$ is ${v_B}$. The value of ${v_A}/{v_B}$ is
explanation:
${v_{\rm{A}}} = \tan 30^\circ $ and ${v_{\rm{B}}} = \tan 45^\circ $
$\therefore \frac{{{v_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{v_{\rm{B}}}}} = \frac{{\tan 30^\circ }}{{\tan 45^\circ }} = \frac{{1/\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 1 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following is a one dimensional motion?

options.text: Motion of wheels of moving train
Train running on a straight track

subject: Physics

question: A 150 $m$ long train is moving with a uniform velocity of 45 $km/h$. The time taken by the train to cross a bridge of length 850 $m$ is
explanation:
Total distance to be covered for crossing the bridge $ = $ length of train $ + $ length of bridge
$ = 150\,m + 850\,m = 1000\,m$
$\rm{Time} = \frac{{\rm{Distance}}}{{\rm{Velocity}}} = \frac{{1000}}{{45 \times \frac{5}{{18}}}} = 80\sec $
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves a straight line $OX$. At a time $t$ (in seconds) the distance (in metres) of the particle from $O$ is given by $x = 40 + 12t - {t^3}.$ How long would the particle travel before coming to rest?
explanation:
Distance travelled by the particle is
$x = 40 + 12t - {t^3}$
We know that, speed is rate of change of distance i.e., $v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
$\therefore v = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {40 + 12t - {t^3}} \right) = 0 + 12 - 3{t^2}$
But final velocity $v = 0$
$\therefore \,12 - 3{t^2} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {t^2} = \frac{{12}}{3} = 4 \Rightarrow t = 2s$
Hence, distance travelled by the particle before coming to rest is given by
$x = 40 + 12\left( 2 \right) - {\left( 2 \right)^3}$
$ = 40 + 24 - 8 = 64 - 8 = 56 \, m$
At, $t = 0,\,{x_1} = 40 \, m$
$t = 2s,\,{x_2} = 56 \, m$
$\therefore S = {x_2} - {x_1} = 16 \, m$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement $y$ (in metre) of a body varies with time $t$ (in second) as $y =  - \frac{2}{3}{t^2} + 16t + 2$. How long does the body take to come to rest?
explanation:
Velocity, $v = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = - \frac{4}{3}t + 16$
For body to be at rest, $v = 0$
$ - \frac{4}{3}t + 16 = 0 \Rightarrow t = 12$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of the body as a function of time is $s = 5{t^2} - 6t.$ The time at which the velocity of the body becomes zero is
explanation:
$s = 5{t^2} - 6t$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 10t - 6 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{3}{5}s$
subject: Physics

question: A body is thrown vertically upwards. If air resistance is to be taken into account, then the time during which the body rises is
explanation:
Let the initial velocity of ball be $u$.
Time of rise ${t_1} = \frac{u}{{g + a}}$ and height reached
$ = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2(g + a)}}$
Time of fall is given by
$\frac{1}{2}(g - a)t_2^2 = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2(g + a)}}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_2} = \frac{u}{{\sqrt {(g + a)(g - a)} }}$
$\therefore {t_2} > {t_1}$ because $\frac{1}{{g + a}} < \frac{1}{{g - a}}$

options.text: Greater than the time of fall
Equal to the time of fall
Less than the time of fall

subject: Physics

question: A particle located at $x = 0$ at time $t = 0$, starts  moving along the positive $x$ - direction with a velocity $'v'$ that varies as $v = a\sqrt x .$ The displacement of the particle varies with time as
explanation:
$v = \alpha \sqrt x \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \alpha \sqrt x \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt x }} = \alpha \,dt$
By integrating both sides $\int {{x^{ - 1/2}}dx = \int {\alpha \,dt} } $
$\therefore \,x \propto {t^2}$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement-time graph for two particles $A$ and $B$ are straight lines inclined at angles of $30^\circ $ and $60^\circ $ with the time axis. The ratio of  vilocities of  ${v_A}:{v_B}$ is
explanation: $\frac{{{v_A}}}{{{v_B}}} = \frac{{\tan {\theta _A}}}{{\tan {\theta _B}}} = \frac{{\tan {{30}^ \circ }}}{{\tan {{60}^ \circ }}} = \frac{{1/\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{1}{3}.$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of a body along $x$-axis depends on time as $\sqrt x  = t + 1.$ Then the velocity of body
explanation:
$\sqrt x = t + 1$
Squaring both sides, we get,
$x = {\left( {t + 1} \right)^2} = {t^2} + 2t + 1$
Differentiating it w.r.t. time $t$, we get, $\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2t + 2$
velocity, $v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2t + 2.$
subject: Physics

explanation: Conceptual

explanation: The slope of the tangent drawn to the curve is zero at five instants.

Initial speed of particle was zero
The velocity remains positive for $t = 0\,s$ to $t = 6s$

question: Acceleration time graph is given below. If the body started from zero velocity find out the final velocity after 3 seconds of motion in a straight line.
explanation:
Change in velocity $\Delta v = \int\limits_0^3 {a\,dt} $
$\Delta v = $ Area of the graph
$\Delta v = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 4 + 2 \times 4$
$v - 0 = 10 \, m/s$ (initial velocity is zero)
$v = 10 \, m/s$

explanation: The slope of the graph is negative at this point.

explanation: The slope of the graph changes, decreases continuously $\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = v} \right)$ and finally it becomes zero.

The velocity of particle is constant throughout motion.

question:
The average and instanteneous velocities have same value in a uniform motion.
In uniform motion, the velocity of an object increases uniformly.
explanation:
An object is said to be in uniform motion if it undergoes equal displacement in equal intervals of time.
Thus, in uniform motion average and instantaneous velocities have same value. Velocity is constant in uniform motion, so option (1) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
The position - time graph of a body moving uniformly in a straight line is parallel to position axis.
The position - time graph in a non - uniform motion gives constant velocity at all instants of time.
explanation: If the position - time graph of a body moving uniformly in a straight line is parallel to position axis, it means that the position of body is changing at constant time, which  shows that the velocity of the body is infinite. In non uniform motion velocity is constant. So  both  statements are wrong and option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question: If the velocity of a particle is $v = At + B{t^2},$ where $A$ and $B$ are constants, then the distance travelled by it between $1 \, s$ and $2 \, s$ is
explanation:
$v = At + B{t^2} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = At + B{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \int_0^x {dx} = \int_1^2 {\left( {At + B{t^2}} \right)dt} $
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{A}{2}\left( {{2^2} - {1^2}} \right) + \frac{B}{3}\left( {{2^3} - {1^3}} \right) = \frac{{3A}}{2} + \frac{{7B}}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: Two cars $P$ and $Q$ start from a point at the same time in a straight line and their positions are represented by ${x_P}(t) = at + b{t^2}$ and ${x_Q}(t) = ft - {t^2}.$ At what time do the cars have the same velocity?
explanation:
$\frac{{d{x_p}}}{{dt}} = a + 2bt$
$\frac{{d{x_q}}}{{dt}} = f - 2t$
$a + 2bt = f - 2t$
$t = \frac{{f - a}}{{\left( {2b + 2} \right)}}$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement-time graphs of two moving particles make angles of $30^\circ $ and $45^\circ $ with the $x$-axis as shown in the figure. The ratio of their respective velocities is:
explanation:
Velocity is slope of $s-t$ graph
$v = \frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \tan \theta $
$\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{{\tan {\theta _1}}}{{\tan {\theta _2}}} = \frac{{\tan 30^\circ }}{{\tan 45^\circ }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle shows distance-time curve as shown in the figure. The maximum instantaneous velocity of the particle is around the point
explanation: The slope of the tangent to the distance-time curve at any point gives the instantaneous velocity at that point. Thus, the maximum instantaneous velocity of the particle is aroud the point $R$ as the slope is steepest at that point.

question: Look at the graphs (1) to (4) carefully and indicate which of these possibly represents one dimensional motion of a particle?
explanation: Graphs 1 and 4 depict scenarios where velocity and position each have two values  respectively at every instant of time which is physically not possible. Graph 3 shows negative speed which is also not possible. Only Graph 2 represents a realistic one dimensional motion where the particle first accelerates, reaches a constant velocity, then decelerates and finally stops.
subject: Physics

question: The displacement time graphs of two moving particles make angles of $30^\circ $ and $45^\circ $ with the $X$-axis. The ratio of their velocities is
explanation:
Slope of displacement-time graph gives velocity.
$\therefore \,\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{{\tan {\theta _1}}}{{\tan \,{\theta _2}}} = \frac{{\tan 30^\circ }}{{\tan \,45^\circ }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of the particle starting from rest (at $t = 0$) is given by $s = 6{t^2} - {t^3}$. The time  in second at which the particle will attain zero velocity again, is
explanation:
$v = \frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 12t - 3{t^2}$
Velocity is zero for $t = 0$ and $t = 4s$

question: The position $x$ of a particle w.r.t. time $t$ along $x$-axis is given by $x = 9{t^2} - {t^3},$ where $x$ is in metre and $t$ in sec. What will be the position of this particle when it achieves maximum speed along the $ + x$ direction?
explanation:
Given, the position $x$ of a particle w.r.t. time $t$ along $x$- axis
$x = 9{t^2} - {t^3}$
$v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}\,(9{t^2} - {t^3})$
$v = 18t - 3{t^2}$
$a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}(18t - 3{t^2})$
$a = 18 - 6t$
When speed of particle is maximum, its acceleration is zero, i.e.,
$a = 0 \Rightarrow t = 3s$
Putting in Eq. (1), we obtain position of particle at the time
$x = 9{(3)^2} - {(3)^3} = 81 - 27 = 54\,m$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement ' $x$ ' (in meter) of a particle of mass '$ m$ ' (in $kg$) moving in one dimension under the action of a force, is related to time ' $t$ ' (in sec) by, $t=\sqrt{x}+3$. The displacement of the particle when its velocity is zero, will be
explanation:
$t = \sqrt x + 3\,\,\,({\rm{given}})\,\,\, \Rightarrow x = {(t - 3)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \nu = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2(t - 3)$
$\therefore$ When $v = 0, t = 3\,s$.
$\text { At } t=3 s, x=(3-3)^{2}=0$
subject: Physics

question: The given graph shows the variation of velocity \[\left( v \right)\] with position ($x$) for a particles moving along a straight line. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957679/preptracker/questions/q_106199_question_1764957679257.png Which of the following graphs shows the variation of acceleration ($a$) with position ($x$)?
explanation:
$v = - kx + c$ (given line with negative slope)
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - k\frac{{\,dx}}{{dt}} = - kv$
$ \Rightarrow a = - k( - kx + c) = {k^2}x - kc$
Slope of $a$ - $x$ graph is positive and $a=-\mathrm{ve}$ at, $x=0$.
subject: Physics

question: The position $x$ (in metre) of a particle moving along a straight line is given by $x=t^{3}-12 t+3$, where ' $t$ ' is time (in second). The acceleration of the particle when its velocity becomes $15\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ is
explanation:
For a particle moving along straight line.
$x=t^{3}-12 t+3$
$\therefore$ Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=3 t^{2}-12=15$
$\therefore t=3 s$
$\therefore$ Acceleration at time, $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
$a = {\left( {\frac{{dv}}{{dt}}} \right)_{t = 3s}} = {(6t)_{t = 3s}} = 6 \times 3 = 18\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A body is falling freely from the top of a tower of height $125\,m$. The distance covered by the body during the last second of its motion is $x \%$ of the height of the tower. Then $x$ is (Acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Hence $u=0$
So $h=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$ or, $125=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times t^{2}$
$\therefore t=5 s$
The distance covered by the body during the last second of its motion is $x \%$ of the height of the tower
$\therefore 125-125 \times \dfrac{x}{100}=\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}$
or, $125-\dfrac{125 \times x}{100}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10(5-1)^{2}=80$
or, $12500-125 x=80 \times 100 \Rightarrow 4500=125 x$
$\therefore x=\dfrac{4500}{125}=36$
subject: Physics

question: A stone is thrown vertically up from the top end of a window of height $1.8\,m$ with a velocity of $8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The time taken by the stone to cross the window during its downward journey is (Acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Particle will have the same speed as it crosses the point of projection during its downward motion. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952040/preptracker/questions/q_106196_explanation_1764952039710.png $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow-1.8=-8 t-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 5 t^{2}+8 t-1.8=0$
$t=\dfrac{-8 \pm \sqrt{64+36}}{10}$
$=\dfrac{-8+10}{10}=0.2 s$
subject: Physics

question: A body is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of $35\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ from the ground. The ratio of the speeds of the body at times $3\,s$ and $4\,s$ of its motion is (Acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
$u=35 m s^{-1}$
Now time of ascent $\left(t_{1}\right)=$ Time of descent $\left(t_{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad {t_1} = {t_2} = \frac{u}{g} = \frac{{35}}{{10}} = 3.5\,s$
$\therefore \quad$ The particle at time $3 s$ and $4 s$ is at same level. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957678/preptracker/questions/q_106195_explanation_1764957677834.png $\therefore {v_3} = {v_4} = 0 + 10 \times 0.5 = 5\,m/s$
${v_3}:{v_4} = 5:5 = 1:1.$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A body moving with uniform acceleration, travels a distance of $25\,m$ in the fourth second and $37\,m$ in the sixth second. The distance covered by the body in the next two seconds is
explanation:
For uniformly accelerated motion, distance in ${n^{th{\text{ }}}}$ second is,
$S_{n}=u+(2 n-1) \dfrac{a}{2}$ where u is initial velocity.
$\therefore \quad 25 = u + (2 \times 4 - 1)\frac{a}{2} = u + \frac{7}{2}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Again, $\,37 = u + (2 \times 6 - 1)\frac{a}{2} = u + \frac{{11}}{2}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq (1) and (2), we get
$a = 6\,m{s^{ - 2}},u = 4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore$ At time, $t=6 s$
$v = u + at = 4 + 6 \times 6 = 40\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore S = v{t_1} + \frac{1}{2}at_1^2$
$ = 40 \times 2 + \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 2 \times 2 = 92\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Match the kinematic equations of motion and graphical concepts listed in Column I with their appropriate interpretations or descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct option from the codes given below.
explanation:
$v=v_{0}+a t$ relates the final velocity to the initial velocity and acceleration, thus A matches with Q.
$x=v_{0} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$ gives the displacement as a function of initial velocity and acceleration, so B matches with R.
$v^{2}=v_{0}^{2}+2 a x$ is obtained by using the slope of the velocity-time graph for uniform acceleration, thus C matches with S The area under the velocity-time curve is equivalent to the displacement over a time. So D matches with P. So correct option is (3).

options.text: A - R,  B - S,          C - P,   D - Q
A - P,  B - Q,        C - R,   D - S
A - Q,  B - R,          C - S,   D - P

subject: Physics

question: Match the terms related to acceleration or equations in Column I with their correct descriptions in Column II. Select the correct option using the codes given below
explanation:
The rate of change of velocity with time gives acceleration this concept was developed by Galileo while studying motion, thus A matches with S.
If velocity charges continuously, the motion is known as non-uniform motion. B matches with P.
The equation $v = {v_0} + at$ relates the final velocity $v$, the initial velocity $v_{0}$, and acceleration $a$ over time $t$, so C matches with Q.
Average acceleration is defined as the change of velocity over a time interval, so D matches with R. So correct option is (3).

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - R, D - S
A - S,     B - R,   C - Q,     D - P
A - S,     B - P,   C - Q,     D - R

explanation: Statement A is true and it is rearranged from the first kinematic equation $v=v_{0}+a t$. Statement B is true as per the definition of acceleration; if it is zero, it implies that there is no change in velocity, hence the velocity is constant. Correct option is (1).

question:

The velocity of an object at a certain moment can be zero even if its acceleration is not zero.

explanation: Statement A is true, as seen in the case of an object thrown upward: at its highest point, the velocity is zero but the acceleration due to gravity is not. Statement B is false because an object can have zero velocity at an instant (like at the top of its path in projectile motion) but still have acceleration acting on it (gravity in this case). explanation: Statement A is false because negative acceleration (deceleration) can also mean an object is speeding up in the negative direction. question: At time $t=0$, a particle leaves the origin and moves in the positive direction of the $X$-axis. If the velocity of the particles varies as$v(t)=v_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{t}{t_{0}}\right),\left|v_{0}\right|=10 {~m} / {s}$ and $t_{0}=10 {~s}$, then the distance covered by the particle during the first $20 s$ is
explanation:
Given that,
At $t=0, x=0$ and $u=v_{0}=10 {~m} / {s}$
At any time $t$, velocity of particle,$v=v_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{t}{t_{0}}\right)=v_{0}-\dfrac{v_{0}}{t_{0}} t$
Velocity will become zero at time $t_{1}$
$\therefore \quad 0=v_{0}-\dfrac{v_{0}}{t_{0}} t_{1} \Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{v_{0}}{v_{0}} \times t_{0}=t_{0}=10 {~s}$
Acceleration,
$a=\dfrac{d \nu}{d t}=-\dfrac{\nu_{0}}{t_{0}}=-\dfrac{10}{10}=-1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
At $t=t_{0}=10 {~s}$, the particle momentarily stops and reverses its direction of motion
Now, displacement in time 0 to 10 s
$s_{1}=u t_{1}+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{1} t_{1}^{2}=v_{0}(10)-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1(10)^{2}$
$=10 \times 10-50=50 {~m}$ and displacement for time $10 s$ to $20 s$,
$t_{2}=20-10=10 {~s}$
$s_{2}=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{2} t_{2}^{2}=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times(-1) \times(10)^{2}=-50 m$
Total distance travelled,
$d=\left|s_{1}\right|+\left|s_{2}\right|=50+50=100 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped from rest at time $t=0$ from a certain height. A second ball is dropped from the same height at time $t=1 {~s}$. At what time $t$, the distance between two balls becomes $10\,m$ ?Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
explanation:
For the first ball,
$h_{1}=0 \times t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
For the second ball,
$h_{2}=0 \times(t-1)+\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}$
Given, $h_{1}-h_{2}=10$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}=10$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{g}{2}(2t - 1) = 10 \Rightarrow 2t - 1 = \frac{{2 \times 10}}{{10}}$
$ \Rightarrow t = 1.5\,s$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving along the $Y$-axis. The position of the particle from the origin as a function of time ( $t$ ) is given as $y(t)=10 t e^{-2 t}$. How far is the particle from the origin when it stops momentarily? ( $y$ is given in units of metre and $t$ is in units of second)
explanation:
The position of the particle is given as
$y(t) = 10t{e^{ - 2t}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Velocity of the particle is given as
$v=\dfrac{d}{d t} y(t)=\dfrac{d}{d t}\left(10 t e^{-2 t}\right)$
$=10\left(e^{-2 t}-2 t e^{-2 t}\right)=10 e^{-2 t}(1-2 t)$
When body stops, then $v=0$
$\Rightarrow e^{-2 t}(1-2 t)=0 \Rightarrow(1-2 t)=0 \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1}{2} s$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (1), displacement of the particle from origin is given as
$y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=10 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times e^{-2 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}=5 e^{-1}=\dfrac{5}{e} m$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The nature of a graph drawn for a freely falling body with time on the $X$-axis and speed on the $Y$-axis is (Assuming initial speed to be zero.)
explanation:
As we know that, for free falling body the equation of velocity is$v=u+g t$
Since, $u=0$ then $v=g t\left(\because g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)$
$v=10 t$
Which represents a straight line passing through the origin.
So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: a straight line with positive $Y$-axis intercept.
a straight line passing through the origin.
a straight line parallel to $y$-axis with positive $x$-axis intercept.

subject: Physics

question: The relation between time $t$ and distance $x$ of a particle is $t=a x^{2}+b x$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. If $v$ is the velocity of the particle, then its acceleration is
subject: Physics

question: A body starts from rest with uniform acceleration. If its velocity after $n^{\text {th }}$ second (last second) is $v$ then its displacement in the last two seconds is
explanation:
Given initial velocity $u=0$.
Let the acceleration be $a$
Thus, using $v=u+a t$, we get $a=\dfrac{v}{n}$
For displacement in last two seconds,
$s = \left[ {0 \times n + \frac{1}{2}a \times {n^2}} \right] - \left[ \begin{gathered} 0 \times (n - 2) \hfill \\ + \frac{1}{2}a{(n - 2)^2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{v}{n}\right)\left[n^{2}-(n-2)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{2 v}{n}(n-1)$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A bird flies with a velocity $(t-2) m s^{-1}$ along a straight line, where $t$ is the time in seconds. The distance covered by it in a time of 4 seconds is
explanation:
Acceleration of the bird for $t=0$ to $t=2 s$
will be $a=\dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{[0-(-2)]}{2}=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
distance travelled during $t=0$ to $t=2 s$
$2as$ $=v^{2}-u^{2}$
$2 \times 1 \times s=0-(-2)^{2}$
$2 s=-4 s \Rightarrow s=-2 m$
Acceleration of bird for $t=2 s$ to $t=4 s$ will be
$a=\dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{2-0}{2}=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Distance covered will be
$2as$ $=v^{2}-u^{2}$
$2(1) s=(2)^{2}-0 \Rightarrow s=2 m$
Total distance covered $=2+2=4 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration of a vertically projected body at its highest reaching position is
explanation:
Given, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952038/preptracker/questions/q_106164_explanation_1764952037888.png For a body moving under gravity,The acceleration at highest point where $v=0$, is equal to acceleration due to gravity at that place.
So, the correct option is (2).

question: A body starts from the rest and acquires a velocity of $10\,m/s$ in $2\,s$. What is the acceleration of the body and the distance travelled?
explanation:
We know,$a=\dfrac{v-u}{t}=\dfrac{10-0}{2}=5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Now, $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 4=10 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A bullet fired into a target losses one-third of its velocity after travelling a distance $x$ metre into the target. If the bullet comes to rest by travelling a further distance $x^{\prime}$, then the ratio $\dfrac{x^{\prime}}{x}$ is
explanation:
Let $u$ be initial velocity of bullet, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957674/preptracker/questions/q_106161_explanation_1764957674268.png Final velocity of bullet after travelling a distance $x$ is equal to $\left(u-\dfrac{u}{3}\right)=\dfrac{2 u}{3}$
We know, $v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{2 u}{3}\right)^{2}=u^{2}+2 a x \Rightarrow \dfrac{4 u^{2}}{9}-u^{2}=2 a x$
$ \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{{ - 5}}{{18a}}{u^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Again after embedding further distance of $x^{\prime}$, it comes to rest https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957675/preptracker/questions/q_106161_explanation_1764957675074.png $v^{\prime 2}=v^{2}+2 a x^{\prime}$(Now, $v=$ initial velocity for $x^{\prime}$ distance)
$ \Rightarrow 0 = \frac{{4{u^2}}}{9} + 2 \times a \times {x^\prime } \Rightarrow {x^\prime } = \frac{{ - 4{u^2}}}{{18a}}{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\dfrac{x^{\prime}}{x}=\dfrac{\dfrac{-4 u^{2}}{18 a}}{\dfrac{-5 u^{2}}{18 a}}=\dfrac{4}{5} \quad$ [from Eqs.(1) and (2)]
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A rocket moves straight upward with zero initial velocity and with an acceleration of $20\,m/{s^2}$.It runs out of fuel and stops accelerating at the end of $5{ }^{\text {th }}$ second. It reaches a maximum height and falls back to the earth. The speed when it hits the ground is (take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Initial velocity of rocket, $u=0 {~m} / {s}$ acceleration, $a=20 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Upward velocity of rocket at the end of $5 s$,
$v=u+a t=0+20 \times 5=100 {~m} / {s}$
Distance travelled by rocket in $5 s$,
$s_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5^{2}=250 {~m}$
After $5 s$, rocket moves under the effect of gravitational acceleration and with initial velocity of $100 {~m} / {s}$.
$\therefore$ Maximum distance travelled by rocket in this situation is $s_{2}$, then using equation,
$v^{2}=u^{2}-2 g s_{2}$
$\Rightarrow 0=100^{2}-2 g s_{2} \Rightarrow s_{2}=\dfrac{100^{2}}{2 g}=\dfrac{100 \times 100}{2 \times 10}$
$=500 {~m}$
$\therefore$ Total upward distance covered by rocket, $s=s_{1}+s_{2}=250+500=750 {~m}$.
Now, rocket starts falling from this height and if $v^{\prime}$ be the velocity of rocket when it reaches the ground, then
$\left(v^{\prime}\right)^{2}=u^{2}+2 g s=0+2 \times 10 \times 750=15000$
$\Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\sqrt{15000}=50 \sqrt{6} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A ball projected up passes the same height $H$ at $2\,s$ and $10\,s$. The value of $H$ is(use, $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Ball has same displacement $H$ at $t=2 s$ and $t=10 {~s}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957673/preptracker/questions/q_106156_explanation_1764957672572.png Now we use,
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
For $t=2 s$, (upward motion of ball)
$H=u \times 2+\dfrac{1}{2}(-9.8) \times 2^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow H = 2u - 19.6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For $t=10 s$ (upward motion of ball)
$H=u \times 10+\dfrac{1}{2}(-9.8) \times 10^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad H = 10u - 490{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we have
$10 u-490=2 u-19.6$
$\Rightarrow 8 u=470.4$
$\Rightarrow u=58.8 {~m} / {s}$
Substituting $u$ in Eq. (1) we get,
$H=2 \times(58.8)-19.6$
$\Rightarrow H=98 m$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A stone falls freely under gravity. It covers distance $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ in the first $4\,s$ and the next $8\,s$, respectively. The ratio of $d_{2} / d_{1}$ is
explanation:
Given, time taken by the stone to cover distance $d_{1}, t_{1}=4 {~s}$
Time taken by the stone to cover distance$d_{2}, t_{2}=8 s$
$\because$ Stone is falling freely, hence its initial speed $u$ is zero and acceleration due to gravity,
$g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
As we know that,
$h = ut + \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)g{t^2}{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore d_{1}=0+\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \times 10 \times 4^{2}=80 {~m}$
Let distance travelled by stone in total time
$t=4 s+8 s=12 s$ be $d$.
Again from Eq. (1), we get$d=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 12^{2}=5 \times 144=720 {~m}$
$\therefore d_{2}=d-d_{1}$
$d_{2}=720-80=640 {~m}$
Now, $\dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}=\dfrac{640}{80}=8$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of a particle moving along the $X$-axis varies as a function of time $t$ as $v(t) = \left( {1 - 3{t^2} + 2{t^3}} \right)m/s$. If its position at $t=0$ is $x=0$, then at $t=2 s$, its position is
explanation:
Given, equation of velocity,$v=\left(1-3 t^{2}+2 t^{3}\right) {m} / {s}$
At $t=0, x=0$ and at $t=2 s$ its position is $x$.
We know that, $v=d x / d t$
$\therefore d x=v d t$
$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{x} d x=\int_{0}^{2}\left(1-3 t^{2}+2 t^{3}\right) d t$
$\Rightarrow[x]_{0}^{x}=\left[t-\dfrac{3 t^{3}}{3}+\dfrac{2 t^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x=\left(2-8+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 16\right) m$
$\Rightarrow x=2 m$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A rocket lifts off from ground and accelerate upwards at $1\,m{s^{ - 2}}.20\,s$ after lift off, a piece breaks off from the bottom of rocket. After breaking off, how much time it takes approximately to reach the ground? (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Let acceleration of rocket, $a_{1}=1 m s^{-2}$, ${1^{st}}$ piece of rocket breaks off after time, ${t_1} = 20\,s$
and $s_{1}$ be the distance covered by rocket in time $t_{1}$.
As we know that,
$s_{1}=u_{1} t_{1}+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{1} t_{1}^{2}$
where, $u_{1}$ is the initial speed of rocket while going up.
$\therefore \quad s_{1}=0 \times 20+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times(20)^{2} m$
${s_1} = 200\,m$
and $v_{1}=u_{1}+a_{1} t_{1}$
where, $v_{1}$ is speed of rocket after time $20\,s$.
$\therefore \quad {v_1} = 0 + 1 \times 20 = 20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Now, after $20\,s$ the piece of rocket breaks off, let the time taken by the piece to reach the ground be $t$.
Speed of rocket after $20 s\left(v_{1}\right)=$ speed of broken part of rocket ( $u_{2}$ )
$i.e.$ $u_{2}=v_{1}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954867/preptracker/questions/q_106149_explanation_1764954866687.png $\therefore \quad s_{2}=-u_{2} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{2} t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad s_{1}=-v_{1} t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \quad\left[\therefore s_{1}=s_{2}=200 m\right]$
$\Rightarrow 200=-20 t+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 40=-4 t+t^{2} \Rightarrow t^{2}-4 t-40=0$
On solving quadratic equation, we get
$t = 8.5\,s$ or $t = - 4.63\,s$
Since, time cannot be negative,
$\therefore t = 8.5\,s$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Two balls are released from the same position at a height of $500\,m$ above ground, one after the other, with an interval of $1\,s$. What is the distance between the two balls when the first hits the ground? (Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Let height of ball from ground be $H$ and time taken by ball 1 to reach on ground be $t$.
$\therefore \quad H = 500\,m$
$\therefore$ Time taken by ball 2 to reach on ground
$t_{2}=(t-1)$
As we know that
$H=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 500=0 \times t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \quad$ [for the first ball]
$\Rightarrow 500=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 100=t^{2} \Rightarrow t=10 s$
$\therefore H_{2}$ (distance covered by ball 2)
$H_{2}=u(t-1)+\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow H_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} g(10-1)^{2} \quad[\because u=0]$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {9^2} = 5 \times 81 = 405\,m$
Hence, distance between the two balls, when first ball is in contact with ground is equal to $\Delta H$, then
$\Delta H=H_{1}-H_{2}=500-405=95 m$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A ball thrown upwards vertically reaches a height of $25\,m$ in $1\,s$. The ratio of total distance covered by the ball in time $t = 2\,s$ and $t = 4\,s$ is (Use, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Let $u$ be the initial velocity in upward direction.
$h=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952032/preptracker/questions/q_106145_explanation_1764952031341.png $\therefore 25=u(1)-\dfrac{1}{2}(10)(1)^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 25 = u - 5 \Rightarrow u = 30\,m/s$
$\therefore$ Time taken to reach at maximum height is calculated as
$v=u-g t \Rightarrow 0=u-10 t \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{30}{10}=3 s$
By equation of motion,
$d_{1}=s_{1}=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$=30(2)-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(2)^{2}$
$ = 60 - 5 \times 4 = 60 - 20 = 40\,m$
Maximum height achieved by ball in (3$s$)
$H=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=30(3)-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10(3)^{2}$
$=90-\dfrac{10}{2} \times 9$
$ = 90 - 45 = 45\,m$
Displacement after $4 s$,
$s_{2}=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=30 \times 4-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10(4)^{2}$
$ = 120 - (5 \times 16) = 120 - 80 = 40\,m$
Now, distance travelled, $d_{2}=H+\left(H-S_{2}\right)$
$ = 45 + (45 - 40) = 45 + 5 = 50\,m$
Ratio, $\dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}=\dfrac{40}{50}=\dfrac{4}{5}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped from a bridge that is $45\,m$ above the water. It falls directly into a boat which is moving with constant velocity. The boat is $12\,m$ away from the point of impact when the ball is dropped. The speed of the boat is (take, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
In given situation, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952030/preptracker/questions/q_106144_explanation_1764952029893.png Time taken by ball to cover $45 m=$ Time taken by boat to cover $12\,m$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}}=\dfrac{\text { Distance }(x)}{\text { Speed }(v)} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 45}{10}}=\dfrac{12}{v}$
$ \Rightarrow v = 4\,m/s$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped from a height $H$ from rest. The ball travels $\dfrac{H}{2}$ in last $1.0\,s$. The total time taken by the ball to hit the ground is
explanation:
In last $1 s$, ball covers $H / 2$ distance. Let total time for falling by $H$ height is $t$ second.
So, $H = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957666/preptracker/questions/q_106143_explanation_1764957665779.png second, ball falls by $\dfrac{H}{2}$ height.
So, $\frac{H}{2} = \frac{1}{2}g{(t - 1)^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Now, dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{H}{H / 2}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} g(t-1)^{2}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{t}{t-1}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{2} t-\sqrt{2}=t$
$t=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}-1}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(\sqrt{2}-1)
(\sqrt{2}+1)}$
$=2+\sqrt{2}=2+1.41=3.41 s$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The position of an object moving along $X$ axis is given by $x=\alpha+\beta t^{2}$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants with appropriate dimensions and $t$ is time in seconds. The average velocity between $t=2 s$ and $4 s$ is $12 {~m} / {s}$. If $\alpha=8 m$, then the value of $\beta$ is
explanation:
Given, position along $X$-axis,
$x=\alpha+\beta t^{2}$
At $t=2 s, x_{1}=\alpha+4 \beta$ and at
$t=4 s, x_{2}=\alpha+16 \beta$
$\therefore$ Displacement of object, $\Delta x=x_{2}-x_{1}$
$=(\alpha+16 \beta)-(\alpha+4 \beta)=12 \beta$
Average velocity,
$v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{12 \beta}{(4-2)}=6 \beta$
$ \Rightarrow 12 = 6\beta \quad \left( {\because {v_{avg{\text{ }}}} = 12\,m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)$
$\Rightarrow \beta=2 m s^{-2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A particle moving along $X$-axis has an acceleration $f$ at time $t$ is given by$f=f_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{t}{T}\right)$, where $f_{0}$ and $T$ are constants. The particle at $t=0$ has zero velocity. In the time interval between $t=0$ and the instant when $f=0$, the particle's velocity is
explanation:
Acceleration of the particle is given as
$f = {f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore$ Velocity of particle is given as,
$\int_{0}^{v} d v=\int_{0}^{t} f d t=\int f_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{t}{T}\right) d t$
$ \Rightarrow v = {f_0}\left( {t - \frac{{{t^2}}}{{2T}}} \right) + C\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Given that, at $t=0, v=0$
Hence, from Eq. (1), we get
$0=f_{0}(0-0)+C \Rightarrow C=0$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (2), we have
$v = {f_0}\left( {t - \frac{{{t^2}}}{{2T}}} \right){\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Suppose, acceleration of the particle becomes zero at time $t=t_{1}$.
Hence, from equation (1)
$0=f_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{t_{1}}{T}\right) \Rightarrow 1-\dfrac{t_{1}}{T}=0 \Rightarrow t_{1}=T$
Hence, velocity of particle at $t=t_{1}=T$, from Eq. (3),
$v=f_{0}\left(t_{1}-\dfrac{t_{1}^{2}}{2 T}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \nu=f_{0}\left(T-\dfrac{T^{2}}{2 T}\right)=f_{0}\left(T-\dfrac{T}{2}\right)=\dfrac{f_{0} T}{2}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Consider that a truck is moving initially with $54\,km/h$. It has stopped by the driver after looking at an obstacle with a deceleration of $10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. The distance travelled by truck before coming to rest is
explanation:
Given, initial velocity of truck,
$u = 54\,km/h = 54 \times \frac{5}{{18}} = 15\,m/s$
Final velocity, $v=0$
Deceleration, $a = - 10\,m/{s^2}$
As we know that, $v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s$
$\Rightarrow(0)^{2}=(15)^{2}+2 \times(-10) s \Rightarrow 20 s=(15)^{2}$
$s = \frac{{15 \times 15}}{{20}} = 11.25\,m$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown straight upward from ground with a speed of $20\,m/s$. The ball was caught on its way down at a point $5\,m$ above the ground. The time taken by the ball during entire trip is (assume, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
The given situation of entire motion of ball is shown in the following figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957662/preptracker/questions/q_106136_explanation_1764957662346.png Initial speed of ball, $u = 20\,m/s$
Total time taken by the ball to reach at highest point $P$ is $t_{1}$, then from equation of vertical motion
$v=u-g t \Rightarrow 0=20-10 t_{1} \Rightarrow t_{1}=2 s$
Maximum height attained by the ball is $h$, then from equation
$h=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=20 \times 2-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 2^{2}$
$ = 40 - 20 = 20\,m$
If $t_{2}$ be the time taken by the ball to reach at $B$ from point $P$, then
$\Rightarrow 20-5=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t_{2}^{2}$
$15 = 5t_2^2 \Rightarrow {t_2} = \sqrt 3 \,s$
$\therefore$ Time taken by the ball during entire trip,
$t = {t_1} + {t_2} = 2 + \sqrt 3 \,s$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A motor-bike starts from rest, attains a velocity of $10\,m/s$ with an acceleration of $0.5\,m/{s^2}$, travels $10\,km$ with this uniform velocity and then comes to halt with a uniform deceleration of $0.2\,m/{s^2}$. The total time of travel is
explanation:
The given situation of entire motion of motor bike is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957659/preptracker/questions/q_106135_explanation_1764957659241.png If time taken by the motorbike to reach from point $A$ to $B$ is $t_{A B}$, then for $A$ to $B$, from equation, $v=u+a t$
$v_{B}=v_{A}+a t_{A B} \Rightarrow 10=0+0.5 t_{A B}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_{AB}} = \frac{{10}}{{0.5}} = 20\,s$
Since, the motorbike moves from point $B$ to $C$ with constant velocity. Hence, time taken to travel distance $B C$ is given as
${t_{BC}} = \frac{{BC}}{{{v_B}}} = \frac{{10\,km}}{{10\,m{s^{ - 1}}}} = \frac{{10000\,m}}{{10\,m{s^{ - 1}}}} = 1000\,s$
For motion of motorbike from point $C$ to $D$, time $ = {t_{CD}},{v_B} = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}},{v_f} = 0,{a^\prime } = 0.2\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
From equation,$v=u-a t, v_{f}=v_{B}-a^{\prime} t_{C D}$
$0 = 10 - 0.2 \times {t_{CD}} \Rightarrow {t_{CD}} = 50\,s$
$\therefore$ Total time $=t_{A B}+t_{B C}+t_{C D}$
$=20 s+1000 s+50 s=1070 s$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Ball- 1 is dropped from the top of a building from rest. At the same moment, ball- 2 is thrown upward towards ball -1 with a speed $14\,m/s$ from a point $21\,m$ below the top of building. How far has the ball-1 been dropped from the position where it passes ball-2. (Assume, acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$).
explanation:
Suppose that both the balls meet at a distance $h$ from ball-1 after time $t$ from the start as shown in the figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957656/preptracker/questions/q_106134_explanation_1764957655900.png For downward motion of ball-1, from second equation of the motion,
$h=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \Rightarrow h=0 \times t+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2}[\because u=0]$
$ \Rightarrow h = 5{t^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For upward motion of ball-2, from second equation of the motion,
$h = vt - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Given, speed of projection of ball-2,
$v=14 m / s$,
acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Substituting these values in Eq. (2), we get$21-h=14 t-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 21 - h = 14t - 5{t^2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Adding Eq. (1) and (2), we get
$21=14 t \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{3}{2} s=1.5 s$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (1), we get
$h=5 \times(1.5)^{2}=11.25 {~m}=45 / 4 {~m}$
Therefore, the ball -1 has been dropped
$45 / 4 m$ above the position where it passes ball-2. So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a car initially at rest, starts to move along a straight road first with acceleration $5\,m/{s^2}$, then with uniform velocity and finally, decelerating at $5\,m/{s^2}$, before coming to a stop. Total time taken from start to end is $t = 25\,s$. If the average velocity during that time is $72\,km/hr$, the car moved with uniform velocity for a time of
explanation:
Given, acceleration of car, $a = 5\,m/{s^2}$, deceleration of car $a = 5\,m/{s^2}$, total time taken from start to end is, $t = 25\,s$ and average velocity of car, ${v_{avg{\rm{ }}}} = 72\,km/hr = 20\,m/s$
$\left(\because \quad 1 \dfrac{k m}{h r}=\dfrac{5}{18} m / s\right)$
Since, $v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{\text { total displacement }}{\text { total time taken }}$
$2 t$, total time taken by the car during acceleration and deceleration
$v_{\text {avg }}=20=\dfrac{d_{t}+d_{(25-2 t)}+d_{t}}{25}=\dfrac{2 d_{t}+d_{(25-2 t)}}{25}$
Since, $d_{t}=0+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{5}{2} t^{2}$ and$d_{(25-2 t)}=v_{\text {uni. }}(25-2 t)$
where, $v_{\text {uni. }}=5 t$
now, $v_{\text {avg }}=20=\dfrac{2\left(\dfrac{5}{2} t^{2}\right)+5 t(25-2 t)}{25}$
$\therefore 20 \times 25=5 t^{2}+5 t(25-2 t)$
$\Rightarrow 500=5 t^{2}+125 t-10 t^{2} \Rightarrow t^{2}-25 t+100=0$
So, it gives $t=20$ and $5 s$.
Hence, the time of uniform motion,$t_{20}=25-2 t=25-2 \times 20=-15 s$
( $\because$ Not possible)
or ${t_5} = 25 - 10 = 15\,s$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is projected vertically up from ground. Boy $A$ standing at the window of first floor of a nearby building observes that the time interval between the ball crossing him while going up and the ball crossing him while going down is $2\,s$. Another boy $B$ standing on the second floor notices that time interval between the ball passing him twice, during up motion and down motion is $1 s$. Calculate the difference between the vertical positions of boy $B$ and boy $A$. (Assume, acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
The figure given below, shows the vertical motion of a ball, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952027/preptracker/questions/q_106132_explanation_1764952027020.png Here, ball crosses the ${1^{st}}$ floor in $2 s$, so it goes up in $1 s$ and goes down in next one second.
$v-v_{A}=-g t \Rightarrow 0-v_{A}=-10(1)$
$ \Rightarrow {v_A} = 10\,m/s$
$\because$ Boy, $B$ observed the ball crossing him while up and down motion in $1 s$.
So, time taken by the ball to reached the floor $B$ from floor $A$ is $0.5 s$.
so, $s=v_{A} t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=10 \times 0.5-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(0.5)^{2}$
$ = 5 - 1.25 = 3.75\,m$
so, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A car moves in positive $Y$ - direction with velocity $v$ proportional to distance travelled $y$ as $v(y) \propto {y^\beta },$ where $\beta$ is a positive constant. The car covers a distance $L$ with average velocity $ < v > $ proportional to $L$ as $ < v>\propto L^{1 / 3}$. The constant $\beta$ is given as
explanation:
Relation between average velocity and instantaneous velocity is
$ < v>=\dfrac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}} \int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} v(t) d t=\dfrac{1}{t} \int_{0}^{t} v(t) \cdot d t$
Given $v=K y^{\beta}$ [where $K=$ constant]
$\therefore \dfrac{d y}{d t}=K y^{\beta} \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{y} y^{-\beta} . d y=K \int_{0}^{t} d t$
$\Rightarrow(y)^{1-\beta}=K(1-\beta) t \Rightarrow y \alpha(t)^{1 / 1-\beta}$
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d y}{d t} \alpha(t)^{\frac{1-(1-\beta)}{1-\beta}} \Rightarrow v \alpha t^{\beta / 1-\beta}$
$\therefore\langle v\rangle \dfrac{y}{t} \Rightarrow\langle v\rangle \alpha \dfrac{(t)^{1 / 1-\beta}}{t}$
$\Rightarrow < v>\alpha(t)^{\beta / 1-\beta} \Rightarrow < v>\alpha y^{\beta}$
But according to question, $\langle v\rangle \propto y^{1 / 3}$
So, $\quad \beta=\dfrac{1}{3}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown vertically upward from the ground at time, $t = 0{\rm{ }}s$. It passes the top of a tower at $t = 3{\rm{ }}s$ and $2\,s$ later it reaches at the maximum height. The height of the tower is (Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
According to question, ball reaches top of a tower at the time, $t_{1}=3 s$ and further reaches at the maximum height at time, ${t_2} = 2\,s$.
$\therefore$ Total time taken by ball to reached the maximum height,
$t = {t_1} + {t_2} = 3 + 2\quad \therefore t = 5\,s$
If $u$ be velocity of ball at time, $t=0$, then from the first equation of the motion,
$v = u - gt\quad \left[ {\because g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right]$
$0 = u - 10 \times 5 \Rightarrow u = 50\,m/s$
If $h$ be the height of the tower, then
$h=u t-\dfrac{1}{2} \times g t^{2}=50 \times 3-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 3^{2}$
$ = 150 - 45 = 105\,m$
Hence, the height of the tower is $105\,m$.
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: Consider a particle moving along the positive direction of $X$-axis. The velocity of the particle is given by $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$ ( $\alpha$ is a positive constant). At time $t=0$, if the particle is located at $x=0$, the time dependence of the velocity and the acceleration of the particle are respectively
explanation:
Given, $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$ and at $x=0, t=0$
To find velocity and acceleration in terms of time, we need to find $x$ as function of $t$.
Now $\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\alpha \sqrt{x}$
$\Rightarrow \int \dfrac{d x}{\sqrt{x}}=\int \alpha d t$
$\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{x}=\alpha t+C$
Using, $x=0$ at $t=0 \Rightarrow C=0$
$\therefore x=\dfrac{1}{4} \alpha^{2} t^{2}$
Hence, velocity, $v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2} t$
and acceleration, $a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=\dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: An object moves in a straight line with deceleration whose magnitude varies with velocity as $3\,{v^{2/3}}$. If at an initial point, the velocity is $8\,m/s$, then the distance travelled by the object before it stops is
explanation:
Given that, $a=$ deceleration
$ = - 3{v^{2/3}}\,m/{s^2}$
At initial point $(t=0)$ velocity $=8 m / s$
We know that,$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t} \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{d v}{d s} \cdot \dfrac{d s}{d t} \Rightarrow a=v d v / d s$
$\Rightarrow a d s=v d v$
$-3 v^{2 / 3} d s=v d v \Rightarrow d s=-\dfrac{1}{3} v^{1 / 3} d v$
Integrate both sides, we get
$\int d s = \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\int {{v^{1/3}}} dv \Rightarrow s = \frac{{ - 1}}{4}{v^{4/3}} + C{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\Rightarrow$ At $t=0, v=8 m / s, s=0$
So, $C=\dfrac{1}{4}(8)^{4 / 3}=\dfrac{16}{4}=4$
Therefore, from Eq. (1)
$s=\dfrac{-1}{4} v^{4 / 3}+4$
Body stops, when $v=0$.
So, $s=4 m$.
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A particle starts from origin at time $t=0$ and moves in positive $X$-direction. Its velocity $v$ varies with times as $v = 10t\hat i\,cm/s$. The distance covered by the particle in $8 s$ will be
explanation:
Given that,$t = 0,v = 10t\hat i\,cm/s$
So, acceleration, $a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 10\hat i\,cm/s$
Now, By using $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$,
$ \Rightarrow s = 1/2 \times 10 \times {(8)^2} = 320\,cm\quad [\because u = 0]$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A car starts from rest and moves with a constant acceleration of $5\,m/{s^2}$ for $10\,s$ before the driver applies the brake. It then decelerates for $5\,s$ before coming to rest, then the average speed of the car over the entire journey of the car is
explanation:
Schematic diagram for motion of body is From the given information: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957652/preptracker/questions/q_106124_explanation_1764957652281.png Here $v = 5 \times 10 = 50\,m/s$
Now, area under the curve gives displacement
$\Rightarrow D=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 50+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 50$
$=250+125=375 m$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{avg{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{D}{T} = \frac{{375}}{{15}}\,m/s = 25\,m/s$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A vehicle starts moving in a straight line with an acceleration, $a = 4\,m/{s^2}$, with initial velocity equal to zero. After accelerating for time $t_{1}$, the vehicle moves uniformly for time $t_{2}$, the vehicle finally decelerates for time $t_{1}$ eventually coming to a stop. The total time taken during the motion is $10\,s$ and the average velocity during the motion is $5.1\,m/s$. The time upto which the vehicle accelerates is
explanation:
By using given information :$v$ - $t$ graph is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954808/preptracker/questions/q_106123_explanation_1764954808350.png Here $t_{1}+t_{2}+t_{1}=10 s \Rightarrow t_{2}=10 s-2 t_{1}(1)$
$v_{1}=u+a t_{1}=4 t_{1}$
Now disp. is area under $v-t$ curve
$D=\dfrac{1}{2} a t_{1}^{2}+v_{1} t_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} a t_{1}^{2}=a t_{1} \cdot t_{1}+a t_{1} t_{2}$
$D=4 t_{1}^{2}+4 t_{1}\left(10-2 t_{1}\right)$ [From equ.(1)]
$D=4 t_{1}^{2}+40 t_{1}-8 t_{1}^{2}$
Now $v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{D}{T}$
$5.1=\dfrac{40 t_{1}-4 t_{1}^{2}}{10} \Rightarrow 51=40 t_{1}-4 t_{1}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 4 t_{1}^{2}-40 t_{1}+51=0$
$\Rightarrow 4 t_{1}^{2}-34 t_{1}-6 t_{1}+51=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(2 t_{1}-3\right)\left(2 t_{1}-17\right)=0 \Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{3}{2}$ or $t_{1}=\dfrac{17}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_1} = 1.5\,s$
(As ${t_1} = 8.5\,s$ is not possible. $\left(2 t_{1}+t_{2}\right)$ exceeds total time.)
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A body travels in a straight line from point $A$ to point $B$ with an initial velocity zero and uniform acceleration, covering $1\,m$ during the first second and $39\,m$ during the last second. The distance between $A$ and $B$ in metre is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957650/preptracker/questions/q_106122_explanation_1764957649545.png Distance travelled in first $1\,s$,
$s_{1}=1 m=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times a \times 1^{2}$
$\Rightarrow a=$ acceleration $ = 2\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
If $t$ be the total time of journey from $A$ and $B$, then using
$S_{n}=u+\dfrac{1}{2} a(2 t-1)$,
$39 = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 2(2t - 1) \Rightarrow t = 20\,s$
$\therefore$ Distance between $A$ and $B$ is,
$s = 0 \times 20 + \frac{1}{2} + 2 \times {(20)^2}\,m = 400\,m$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: In some appropriate units, time $(t)$ and position ($x$) relation of a moving particle is given by $t=x^{2}+x$. The acceleration of the particle is
subject: Physics

question:
Two stones begin to fall from rest from the same height, with the second stone starts to fall ' $\Delta t$ ' seconds after the first falls from rest. The distance of separation between the two stones becomes
' $H$ ', ' $t_{0}$ ' seconds after the first stone starts its motion. Then $t_{0}$ is equal to
explanation:
For ${1^{st{\text{ }}}}$ stone, $t=t_{0}$.
For ${2^{nd{\text{ }}}}$ stone, $t = {t_0} - \Delta t\left( {\because {2^{nd{\text{ }}}}} \right.$ stone starts falling after $\Delta t$ seconds)
Using $S=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$ and noting $u=0$
$S_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} g t_{0}^{2}$ and $S_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} g\left(t_{0}-\Delta t\right)^{2}$
Now, $H=S_{1}-S_{2}$
$\Rightarrow H=\dfrac{1}{2} g\left(t_{0}^{2}-\left(t_{0}-\Delta t\right)^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow H=\dfrac{1}{2} g\left(t_{0}^{2}-t_{0}^{2}-\Delta t^{2}+2 t_{0} \Delta t\right)$
$\Rightarrow t_{0}=\dfrac{H}{g \Delta t}+\dfrac{\Delta t}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: For given velocity-time $(v-t)$ graph, find distance travelled at 0.5 sec .
explanation:
Area under $v-t$ curve is equal to the distance travelled https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952019/preptracker/questions/q_106117_explanation_1764952018986.png $\tan \theta = \frac{{400 - 200}}{2} = \frac{{v - 200}}{{0.5}}$
$v = 250\,m/s$
$\therefore$ Distance travelled $=$ area under $v-t$ curve till $t = 0.5\,s$
$\Rightarrow D=\dfrac{1}{2}[200+250] \times 0.5$
$ = \frac{{450}}{4} = 112.5\,m$
So Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A wooden block, initially at rest on the ground, is pushed by a force which increases linearly with time \(t\). Which of the following curve best describes acceleration of the block with time?
explanation:
As force increases linearly, with time $t$, $F=K t$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951737/preptracker/questions/q_40448_explanation_1764951737064.png
$a=\dfrac{F}{m}=\dfrac{K t}{m} \Rightarrow a \propto t$
It is the form of $y=m x$. Hence it is a straight line passing through origin, as initially mass is at rest.
So, curve will be a straight line.
subject: Physics

question: A boy standing at the top of a ${20 m}$ high tower drops a stone. Assuming ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ the velocity with which it hits the ground is
explanation:
${v=\sqrt{2 g h}}$
$v=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 20}=20 {~m} / {s}$
subject: Physics

question: A body projected vertically upwards with a certain speed from the top of a tower reaches the ground in $t_{1}$. If it is projected vertically downwards from the same point with the same speed, it reaches the ground in $t_{2}$. Time required to reach the ground, if it is dropped from the top of the tower, is
explanation:
I. When body is projected upwards, Using second equation of motion, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951231/preptracker/questions/q_40428_explanation_1764951231080.png
$h=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} ;$
$\Rightarrow-h=u t_{1}-\dfrac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow-2 h=2 u t_{1}-g t_{1}^{2}$
$g t_{1}^{2}-2 u t_{1}-2 h=0$
$\Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{2 u \pm \sqrt{4 u^{2}+8 g h}}{2 g}$
$\Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{u+\sqrt{u^{2}+2 g h}}{g}$ $\quad \,(1)$
II. When body is projected downwards.
$-h=-u t_{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} g t_{2}^{2} ;-2 h=-2 u t_{2}-g t_{2}^{2}$
$g t_{2}^{2}+2 u t_{2}-2 h=0 \Rightarrow t_{2}=\dfrac{-2 u \pm \sqrt{4 u^{2}+8 g h}}{2 g}$
$t_{2}=\dfrac{-u+\sqrt{u^{2}+2 g h}}{g}$ $\quad \,(2)$
III. When body is dropped, $h=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}}$ $\quad \quad $$\quad \quad $$\quad \quad $$\quad \,(3)$
From equations (1), (2) and (3)
so,
$t_{1} t_{2}=\dfrac{1}{g^{2}}\left(\sqrt{u^{2}+2 g h}+u\right)\left(\sqrt{u^{2}+2 g h}-u\right)$
$t_{1} t_{2}=\dfrac{1}{g^{2}}\left(u^{2}+2 g h-u^{2}\right)=\dfrac{2 h}{g}=t^{2}$
so,
$t=\sqrt{t_{1} t_{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A body starts moving from rest with constant acceleration covers displacement $S_{1}$ in first $(p-1)$ seconds and $S_{2}$ in first $p$ seconds. The displacement $S_{1}+S_{2}$ will be made in time
explanation:
Displacement $S_{1}$ in first $(p-1) s$ and $p$ seconds are given as :
$S_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} a(p-1)^{2} \quad(\because$ Initial velocity is 0$)$
$S_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} a p^{2}$
Total displacement,
$S_{1}+S_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} a(p-1)^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} a p^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow S_{1}+S_{2}=(p-1)^{2}+p^{2}=t^{2}$
$t=\sqrt{(p-1)^{2}+p^{2}} s ; t=\sqrt{\left(2 p^{2}-2 p+1\right)} s$
subject: Physics

question: A train starting from rest first accelerates uniformly up to a speed of $80\,km/h$ for time $t$, then it moves with a constant speed for time $3\,t$. The average speed of the train for this duration of journey will be (in $km/h$ )
explanation:
Let the train attain maximum velocity in time $t$.
$\therefore \quad v=u+a t$
$\Rightarrow 80=a t$
Distance covered in time $t$,
$S_{1}=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=0+\dfrac{1}{2}(a t) t=\dfrac{80 \times t}{2}$
Now, distance covered in time $3\,t$ ,
$S_{2}=80 \times 3 t$
Using basic formula of average speed
Average speed $=\dfrac{\text { total distance }}{\text { total time taken }}=\dfrac{S_{1}+S_{2}}{t_{1}+t_{2}}$
$\therefore {V_{avg{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{{80 \times t}}{2} + 80 \times 3t = 70Km/h$
subject: Physics

question: A body travels $102.5 m$ in $n^{\text {th }}$ second and  $115.0\,\,m$  in $(n+2)^{\text {th }}$ second. The acceleration is
explanation:
Distance travelled in $n^{\text {th }}$ second is
Use ${S_{{n^{th}}}} = u + \frac{1}{2}a\,(2n - 1)$
$102.5=u+\dfrac{1}{2} a(2 n-1)$ $\quad $ $\quad \,(1)$
$115 = u + \frac{1}{2}a(2 \times (n + 2) - 1)$
$115 = u + \frac{1}{2}a\,(2n + 3)\quad \quad \quad (2)$
Subtract equation (1) from (2), we get
$115-102.5=\dfrac{1}{2} a(2 n+3-2 n+1)$
$ \Rightarrow a = 6.25\,\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics

question: The relation between time ' $t$ ' and distance ' $x$ ' is $t=\alpha x^{2}+\beta x$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants.The relation between acceleration $(a)$ and velocity $(v)$ is
explanation:
Given, $t=\alpha x^{2}+\beta x$
On differentiating, the above equation of $t$ w.r.t
time $1=2 x \alpha \cdot \dfrac{d x}{d t}+\beta \dfrac{d x}{d t}$$\quad \,(1)$
$v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\dfrac{1}{2 \alpha x+\beta}$ $\quad \,\,\,$$\quad \,(2)$
On differentiating eq (1) again w.r.t. time
$\begin{aligned}& a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=\dfrac{-1}{(2 \alpha x+\beta)^{2}} 2 \alpha \cdot \dfrac{d x}{d t} \\& =-v^{2} \cdot 2 \alpha \cdot v=-2 \alpha v^{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The figure shows a method for measuring the acceleration due to gravity. The ball is projected upward by a "gun". The ball passes electronic "gates" 1 and 2 as it rises and again as it falls. Each gate is connected to a separate timer. The first passage of the ball through each gate starts the corresponding timer, and the second passage through the same gate stops the timer. The time intervals $\Delta t_{1}$ and $\Delta t_{2}$ are thus measured. The vertical distance between the two gates is $d$. If $d=5 {~m}$, $\Delta t_{1}=3 {~s}, \Delta t_{2}=2 {~s}$, find the measured value of acceleration due to gravity.
explanation:
$1.5 = \frac{{{v_1}}}{a}$
$1 = \frac{{{v_2}}}{a}$
$5 = \frac{{v_1^2 - v_2^2}}{{2a}},a = 8$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956750/preptracker/questions/q_40367_explanation_1764956749905.png
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves vertically with an upward initial speed $v_{0}=10.5 {~ms}^{-1}$. If its acceleration varies with time as shown in $a-t$ graph in figure, find the velocity of the particle at $t=4 {~s}$.
explanation:
The velocity of the particle at
$t=4 {~s}$ can be given as
${\vec v_4} = {\vec v_0} + \Delta \vec v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
where $\Delta \vec{v} \equiv A$ (= area under $a-t$ graph during first 4 $s$)
Referring to $a-t$
graph (shown infigure), we have https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956137/preptracker/questions/q_40347_explanation_1764956136492.png
$A = {A_1} + {A_2} - {A_3} - {A_4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
where
${A_1} = 5 \times 1 = 5,{A_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times x \times 5$
${A_3} = \frac{1}{2} \times (1 - x) \times 10$ and
${A_4} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10 = 10$
We can find the value of $x$ as follows:
Using properties of similar triangles, we have $\dfrac{x}{5}=\dfrac{1-x}{10}$.
This yields $x=\dfrac{1}{3}$.
Substituting $x=\dfrac{1}{3}$ in $A_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times x \times 5$ and $A_{3}=\dfrac{1}{2}(1-x) \times 10$,
we have
$A_{2}=5 / 6$ and $A_{3}=10 / 3$.
Then substituting $A_{1}, A_{2}, A_{3}$ and $A_{4}$ in Eq. (2), we have $A=-7.5 \Rightarrow \Delta v=-7.5$ and $\vec{v}_{0}=10.5 {~m} {~s}^{-1}$.
Hence, we have
$v_{4}=v_{0}+\Delta v=10.5-7.5=3 {~ms}^{-1}$.
subject: Physics

question: A balloon rises from rest on the ground with constant acceleration $1 {~ms}^{-2}$. A stone is dropped when the balloon has risen to a height of 40 $m$ . Find the time taken by the stone to reach the ground.
explanation:
$h=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{a}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 40 \times 8}{10}}=8 {~s}$
Velocity after $8 {~s}, v=0+1 \times 8=8 {~ms}^{-1}$
$40 = - 10t + \frac{1}{2}10{t^2}$
$10{t^2} - 2 \times 10t - 2 \times 40 = 0$
${t^2} - 2t - 8 = 0$
$t - 4t + 2t - 8 = 0$
$t = 4\,s$
subject: Physics

question: Find the time (in second) taken by the particle to move from $x=3 {~m}$ to $x=15 {~m}$.
explanation:
The equation of the straight line shown in the graph is
$(x - 3) = \left( {\frac{{15 - 3}}{{8 - 2}}} \right)\,\,\left( {\frac{1}{v} - 2} \right)$
$(x - 3) = 2\left( {\frac{1}{v} - 2} \right) = \frac{2}{v} - 4$
which gives, $x = \frac{2}{v} - 1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
But $v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From (1) and (2), we get.
$\therefore x = \frac{{2dt}}{{dx}} - 1 \Rightarrow x\,\,dx = 2dt - dx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Integrating both sides,$\int_3^{15} {x\,\,} dx = 2\int_0^t d t - \int_3^{15} d x$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}[225-9]=2 t-[15-3] \Rightarrow t=60 {~s}$
subject: Physics

question: A car is moving towards check post with velocity of $54\,\,km{h^{ - 1}}$. When the car is at a distance of 400 $m$ from the check post, the driver applies brakes which causes a deceleration of $0.3 {~ms}^{-2}$. Find the distance  of car from the check post for 2 minutes after applying the brakes.
explanation:
Here, $u=54 \times \dfrac{5}{18}=15 {~ms}^{-1}$
$a=-0.3 {~ms}^{-2}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\because \quad }&{v = u + at} \\ {{\text{ or }}}&{0 = 15 - 0.3\,\,{t_0}} \end{array}$
$\therefore \quad t_{0}=50 {~s}$
After 50 s , car permanently comes to rest.
$\therefore s=\left(\dfrac{u+v}{2}\right) t=\left(\dfrac{15+0}{2}\right) \times 50=375 {~m}$
Thus, the distance of car from the check post is$(400-375) {m}=25 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question: If a particle is moving on a straight line, then its velocity-time graph is sinusoidal as shown in the figure. The distance travelled by the particle in $2\,s$ is
explanation:
${\because v=a \sin \omega t}$
Or ${\quad v=\pi \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{2} t}$
Or ${v=\pi \sin \pi t}$
The speed-time graph is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951574/preptracker/questions/q_40271_explanation_1764951574047.png ${\because \quad}$ Speed ${=|v|}$
${\therefore \quad}$ Distance ${=\int_{0}^{2}|v| d t=2 \int_{0}^{1}|v| d t}$
${=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\pi}[-\cos \pi t]_{0}^{t}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\pi} \times 2=4 {~m}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving along ${X}$-axis. At instant ${t_{0}}$, instantaneous velocity is equal to average velocity from ${t=0}$ to ${t=t_{0}}$. The value of ${t_{0}}$ is ${\dfrac{16}{n} {~s}}$. The value of $n$ is
explanation:
From figure, the average velocity ${=\tan \theta=\dfrac{3}{4}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951524/preptracker/questions/q_40263_explanation_1764951523593.png Also, ${\cos \theta=\dfrac{t_{0}}{4}}$ or ${\dfrac{4}{5}=\dfrac{t_{0}}{4}}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & t_{0}=\dfrac{16}{5} \\\therefore & n=5\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question: A truck is moving at a speed of ${72 {kmh}^{-1}}$ on a straight road. The driver can produce deceleration of ${2 {~ms}^{-2}}$ by applying brakes. The stopping distance of truck is ${13 x {~m}}$, if the reaction time of the driver is 0.2 s . The value of ${x}$ is
explanation:
${\because \quad v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{0^2} = {\left( {72 \times \frac{5}{{18}}} \right)^2} - 2 \times 2\,{s_0}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {s_0} = \frac{{400}}{4} = 100\,m$
Thus, stopping distance ${=\left(72 \times \dfrac{5}{18}\right) \times 0.2+s_{0}}$
$ = 4 + 100 = 104 = 13x$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = \frac{{104}}{{13}} = 8$
subject: Physics

question: A body moving with a speed ${v}$ can be stopped over a distance ${x}$. If its kinetic energy is doubled, how much would be the stopping distance, for the same retarding force?
explanation:
Given : speed ${=v}$, stopping distance ${=x}$ kinetic energy of body, $K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now, kinetic energy is doubled, $2K = \frac{1}{2}m{v'^2}\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From eq.(1) and (2),we get ${v^\prime } = \sqrt 2 v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Now, from eq. of motion
${v^2} - {u^2} = 2{\rm{ as }}$
$ \Rightarrow 0 - {v^2} = - 2ax$ $\quad \quad $ (final velocity $ = 0$)
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 2ax\quad \quad \quad \quad (4)$
