When kinetic energy is doubled, let new stopping distance be $x'{\mkern 1mu} {(\sqrt 2 v)^2} = 2ax'\quad \quad \quad (5)$
Dividing eq.(4) eq.(5), we get
$\dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{x}{x^{\prime}} \Rightarrow x^{\prime}=2 x$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A stone is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity ${30 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$. If the acceleration due to gravity is ${10 {~m} {~s}^{-2}}$, what is the distance travelled by the particle during the first second of its motion?
explanation:
Given : Initial velocity, ${u=20 {~m} / {s}}$
Distance travelled during the first second of motion will be
$S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow S = 30 \times 1 - \frac{1}{2}g{(1)^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because a = - g]$
$ \Rightarrow S = 30 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10$
$ \Rightarrow S = 25\,m{\text{.}}$
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards. It has a speed of ${10 {~m} / {s}}$ when it has reached one half of its maximum height. How high does the ball rise? ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
explanation:
Maximum height reached by particle is
given by
${H_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ (1)
$ [u = initial velocity]
Now, from eq. of motion
$v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s$
At half the maximum height, $v = 10\,m/s$
$100 - {u^2} = - 2g\left( {\frac{{{u^2}}}{{4g}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 100 - {u^2} = \frac{{ - {u^2}}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{u^2}}}{2} = 100$
$ \Rightarrow {u^2} = 200\,m/s$
Substituting in eq. (1), we get$H_{\max }=\dfrac{200}{2 \times 10}=10 {~m}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: The position of a particle varies with time ${t}$ as ${x=a t^{2}-b t^{3}}$. The acceleration of the particle will be zero at time ${t}$ equal to
explanation:
Position of a particle varies with time ${t}$ as
$x = a{t^2} - b{t^3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \,(1)$
Differentiating eq. (1) w.r.t. to time ${t}$, we get velocity
$\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = v = 2at - 3b{t^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Differentiating eq. (2) w.r.t. to time ${t}$, we get acceleration
$\dfrac{d v}{d t}=2 a-6 b t$
For acceleration to be zero, ${2 a-6 b t=0}$
${\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{2 a}{6 b}=\dfrac{a}{3 b}}$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Distance travelled by a body falling from rest in the first, second and third second is in the ratio of
explanation:
Given, initial velocity ${u=0}$
Distance travelled in first second
$s_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 1=5 {~m}$
Velocity acquired in first second of motion
$v=u+a t=g t=10 \times 1=10 {~m} / {s}$
This will be the initial velocity for the next one second.
${\therefore \quad}$ the distance covered in next one second
$\begin{aligned}s_{2} & =u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=10 \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(1)^{2} \\& =10+5=15 {~m}\end{aligned}$
Velocity acquired in the first two seconds of the motion
$v = u + gt = 10 \times 2 = 20\,m/s\quad [\because \,\,\,u = 0]$
This will be initial velocity for the ${3^{\text {rd }}}$ second of the motion. Distance covered in ${3^{\text {rd }}}$ second is
${s_{3}=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=20 \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 1=25 {~m}}$
Now, ${s_{1}: s_{2}: s_{3}=1: 3: 5}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question:
An electron starting from rest has a velocity that increases linearly with time as ${v=k t}$, where ${k=2 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$. The distance covered in the first
3 seconds will be :
explanation:
Variation of velocity with ${t}$ is given as ${v=k t}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{d x}{d t}=k t}$
${\Rightarrow d x=k t d t}$
Distance covered in first 3 seconds will be
$\int_0^x d x = k\int_0^3 t dt$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = \frac{1}{2}k\left[ {{t^2}} \right]_0^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 9$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = 9\,m$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A body moving with uniform acceleration ${6 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ starts from rest. The distance covered by it in ${4^{\text {th }}}$ second will be
explanation:
Initial velocity, ${u=0}$
Acceleration of the body, ${a=6 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
Velocity acquired in first three seconds of the motion
$v=u+a t=v=6 \times 3 {~m} / {s}=18 {~m} / {s}$
This will be the initial velocity for the last one second of the motion
${\therefore}$ the distance covered in ${4^{\text {th }}}$ second
$s=18 \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 1^{2}=18+3=21 {~m}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped from a height of 19.6 $m$ . The distance covered by it in the last second is
explanation:
Height, ${h=19.6 {~m}}$
Time taken ${(t)}$ to cover this distance will be given by
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
Here, initial velocity, ${u=0}$
${\therefore \quad h=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \Rightarrow t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}}}$
${\Rightarrow t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 19.6}{9.8}}=2 {~s}}$
Velocity acquired in first one second of the motion
${v=u+a t=v=9.8 \times 1 {~m} / {s}=9.8 {~m} / {s}}$
This will be the initial velocity ${\left(u^{\prime}\right)}$ for the last one second of the motion.
${\therefore}$ the distance covered in last second will be
$s = {u^\prime }t + \frac{1}{2}g + {t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow s = 9.8 \times 1 + \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times {1^2}$
$s = 9.8 + 4.9 = 14.7\,m$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: When a ball is thrown up vertically with a velocity ${v_{0}}$ it reaches a height ${h}$. If one wishes to triple the maximum height, then the ball should be thrown with a velocity of
explanation:
Here initial velocity, ${u=v_{0}}$
Height reached ${=h}$
At highest point final velocity $(v)$ will be zero.
From equation of motion, ${v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s}$
${0-v_{0}^{2}=-2 g h \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2 g}}$
For thrice the maximum height (3h),
$0 - v_0^{\prime 2} = - 2g\left( {\frac{{3v_0^2}}{{2g}}} \right)$
($v_0^1$ new initial velocity)
$ \Rightarrow v_0^{\prime 2}{\rm{ = 3}}v_0^2 \Rightarrow {{v'}_0} = \sqrt 3 {v_0}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A body, thrown upwards with some velocity reaches the maximum height of $50\,m$. Another body with double the mass thrown up with double the initial velocity will reach a maximum height of
explanation: ${H_{\max }} \propto {u^2}$, if body is projected with double velocity then maximum height will become four times $i.e.\,\,200\,m.$
subject: Physics

question: A balloon starts rising from the ground with an acceleration of $1.25\,m{s^{ - 2}}$. After $8\,s$, a stone is released from the balloon. The stone will (Taking $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)

options.text: begin to move down after being released
cover a distance of $40\,m$ in reaching the ground

subject: Physics

question: The velocity acquired by a body moving with uniform acceleration is $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in 2 seconds and $60\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in four seconds. The initial velocity is
subject: Physics

question: The speed-time graph of a particle moving along a solid curve is shown below. The distance traversed by the particle from $t=0 \mathrm{~s}$ to $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951020/preptracker/questions/q_40098_explanation_1764951019864.png Distance travelled by the particle ,
$s=$ Area under the curve
$=s_{01}+s_{12}+s_{23}$
$s=$ Area of $\triangle O A B$
+ Area of trapezium A B C D
$\quad + {\rm{Area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,\Delta DCE$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times OB \times AB + \frac{1}{2}(AB + CD) \times BD + \frac{1}{2}$
$ \times DE \times DC$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times \left( {1 - 0} \right) \times 1 + \frac{1}{2} \times \left( {1 + 1.5} \right) \times \left( {2 - 1} \right)$
$ + \frac{1}{2} \times \left( {3 - 2} \right) \times 1.5$
$ = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 + \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5$
[ $\because $ area of triangle $ = \,\,\frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \times $ base $\,\, \times \,\,$height]
${\rm{area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{trapezium}} = \frac{1}{2} \times $
$\left( {{\rm{sum}}} \right.{\rm{of}}\left. {\left. {{\rm{parallel}}\,\,{\rm{sides}}} \right) \times {\rm{height}}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {1 + 2.5 + 1.5} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 = \frac{5}{2}m$
subject: Physics

question: A metro train starts from rest and in $5 s$ achieves $180\,km{h^{ - 1}}$ After that it moves with constant velocity and comes to rest after travelling $45\,m$ with uniform retardation. If total distance travelled is   $395\,m,$ find total time of travelling.
explanation:
Given, $v = 108\,km\,{h^{ - 1}} = 30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and $t = 5\,s$
From first equation of motion,
$v = u + at$
$ \Rightarrow 30 = 0 + a \times 5$
$(\therefore \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} u = 0) \Rightarrow a = 6\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
So, distance travelled by metro train in $5\,s,$
${s_1} = \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times (6) \times {(5)^2} = 75\,\,m$
Distance travelled before coming to rest $ = 45\,m$
So, from third equation of motion,
${0^2} = {(30)^2} - 2{\mkern 1mu} a'{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \times {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 45{\mkern 1mu} a'$
$a' = \frac{{30 \times 30}}{{2 \times 45}} = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
Time taken in travelling $45\,m,$
$t_{3}=\dfrac{30}{10}=3 \mathrm{~s}$
Now, total distance $ = 395\,m$
i.e., $75 + s' + 45 = 395\,m$
$ \Rightarrow s' = 395 - (75 + 45) = 275\,m$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {t_2} = \frac{{275}}{{30}} = 9.2\,s$
Hence, total time taken in whole journey
$=t_{1}+t_{2}+t_{3}$
$=5+9.2+3=17.2 s$
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of a particle at an instant is  $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ After $3 s$ its velocity will become $16\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The velocity at $2 s$, before the given instant would have been
explanation:
At time $t$, velocity of particle is
${v_t} = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}},\,\,{v_{t + 3}} = 16\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and ${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {v_{t - 2}} = {v_x}$
From equation of motion,
$ \Rightarrow v = u + at$
$ \Rightarrow {v_t} = 0 + at$
$ \Rightarrow t = \left( {\frac{{10}}{a}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{t - 3}} = u + a(t + 3)$
$ \Rightarrow 16 = 0 + \left( {\frac{{10}}{a} + 3} \right)a = 3a + 10$
$ \Rightarrow a = 2m{s^{ - 2}}$
and $t = 5s\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[{\rm{from Eq}}{\rm{.}}(1)]$
Now, ${v_{t - 2}} = u + a(t - 2) = 2(t - 2)$
$ \Rightarrow {v_x} = {v_{t - 2}} = 6m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration of a particle is increasing linearly with time $t$ as $b t$. The particle starts from the origin with an initial velocity $v_{0}$. The distance travelled by the particle in time $t$ will be
explanation:
According to the question
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = bt \Rightarrow dv = bt\,\,dt$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{b{t^2}}}{2} + {k_1}$
At $t = 0,v = {v_0} \Rightarrow {k_1} = {v_0}$
We get $\quad v = \frac{1}{2}b{t^2} + {v_0}$
Now,$\quad \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{2}b{t^2} + {v_0}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{b{t^3}}}{3} + {v_0}t + {k_2}$
At, $t = 0,x = 0 \Rightarrow {k_2} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{6}b{t^3} + {v_0}t$
subject: Physics

question: Two bodies $A$ (of mass $1\,kg$ ) and $B$ (of mass $3\,kg$) are dropped from heights of $16\,m$ and 25 $m$, respectively. The ratio of the time taken by them to reach the ground is
explanation:
For free fall, from a height, $u = 0$
(initial velocity).
By second equation of motion, $h = ut + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{h_1}}}{{{h_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}}} \right)^2}$
Given, ${h_1} = 16m,\,\,{h_2} = 25\,m$
$\therefore \frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{h_1}}}{{{h_2}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{{25}}} = \frac{4}{5}$
subject: Physics

question: A man throws balls with the same speed vertically upwards one after the other at an interval of $2 s$. What should be the speed of the throw, so that more than two balls are in the sky at any time? (Take, $g = 9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
Time taken by ball to reach maximum
height,
$v = u - gT$
At maximum height, final speed is zero,
i.e. $v = 0,\,\,$so,$u = gT.$
In $2s,u = 2 \times 9.8 = 19.6\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
If a man throws the ball with velocity of $19.6\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ then after $2 s$ it will reach the
maximum height. When he throws 2nd ball, 1st is at the top. When he throws third ball, 1 st will
come to ground and 2 nd will go at the top.
Therefore, only 2 balls are in air. If he wants to
keep more than 2 balls in air he should throw
the ball with a speed greater than $19.6\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball thrown vertically upwards with an initial velocity of $1.4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ returns in $2 s$. The total displacement of the ball is
explanation:
Displacement can be defined as the
distance between initial and final positions of the ball. Since, the ball returns back to its initial
position, then displacement is zero.
subject: Physics

question: A body $A$ is thrown up vertically from the ground with a velocity $v_{0}$ and another body $B$ is simultaneously dropped from a height $H$. They meet at a height $H / 2$, if $v_{0}$ is equal to
explanation:
Suppose the two bodies $A$ and $B$ meet at
time $t$, at a height $\dfrac{H}{2}$ from the ground https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951049/preptracker/questions/q_40063_explanation_1764951049368.png
For body $B,u = 0$,
$h = \frac{H}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad h = ut + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
Hence $\quad \frac{H}{2} = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For body $A,\quad u = {v_0},h = \frac{H}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad h = ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
Hence, $\frac{H}{2} = {v_0}t - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
So,from Eq.(1) and (2) we get,
${v_0}t - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} \Rightarrow {v_0}t = g{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{{v_0}}}{g}$
Now putting this value in Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{1}{2}g \times \frac{{v_0^2}}{{{g^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow H = \frac{{v_0^2}}{g}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_0} = \sqrt {gH} $
subject: Physics

question: A body is projected vertically upwards. The times corresponding to height $h$ while ascending and while descending are $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$, respectively. Then, the velocity of projection will be (take $g$ as acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Let $v$ be initial velocity of vertical projection
and $t$ be the time taken by the body to reach a
height $h$ from ground.
Here, $\quad u = u,a = - g,s = h,t = t$
Using, $\quad s = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$, We have
$h = ut + \frac{1}{2}( - g){t^2}$
or $g{t^2} - 2ut + 2h = 0$
$\therefore \quad t = \frac{{2u \pm \sqrt {4{u^2} - 4g \times 2h} }}{{2g}}$
$ = \frac{{u \pm \sqrt {{u^2} - 2gh} }}{g}$
It means $t$ has two values, i.e.
${t_1} = \frac{{u + \sqrt {{u^2} - 2gh} }}{g}$
${t_2} = \frac{{u - \sqrt {{u^2} - 2gh} }}{g}$
${t_1} + {t_2} = \frac{{2u}}{g}\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,u = \frac{{g\left( {{t_1} + {t_2}} \right)}}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown upwards with a speed  $u$  from a height $h$ above the ground. The time taken by the ball to hit the ground is
explanation:
Time taken to reach the highest point from
the height $h$ is obtained from equation, $v = u - gt$
$\therefore \quad 0 = u - gt\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,t = \frac{u}{g}$
Height attained above $h$ is obtained from
${v^2} - {u^2} = 2( - g){h_1}$
or $0 - {u^2} = 2( - g){h_1}$ or ${h_1} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}}$
Total height, ${h_2} = \left[ {{h_1} + h = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} + h} \right]$
Time taken to hit the ground is obtained
from $\quad {h_2} = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
or$\quad \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} + h = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
$\therefore $Total time taken $t = \sqrt {\frac{{\left( {{u^2} + 2gh} \right)}}{g}} $
subject: Physics

question: The motion of a particle along a straight line is described by equation ${x=8+12 t-t^{3}}$, where ${x}$ is in meters and ${t}$ in seconds. The retardation of the particle when its velocity becomes zero is
explanation:
${v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=0+12-3 t^{2}=0 \Rightarrow t=2 s}$
$\text { At } t=2 s$
Retardation ${=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-(-6 t)=12 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
Here, retardation means negative acceleration.
subject: Physics

question: Displacement of a "HONDA CITY" moving along $\,\,\,$${x}$-axis is given as ${x=\left(3 t^{3}-2 t^{2}+6 t\right)}$ unit. Then,
explanation:
${v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=6 t^{2}-4 t+6}$
${a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=12 t-4}$
Acceleration is time dependent.
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Honda City is moving with uniform velocity
Honda City is moving with uniform acceleration
Honda City is moving with non-uniform acceleration
Initial velocity of Honda City is zero

subject: Physics

question: A particle starts with an initial velocity of $10.0\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ along $x$-direction and accelerates uniformly at the rate of $2.0\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ The time taken by the particle to reach the velocity of $60.0\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ is
explanation:
$u = 10.0\,m{\rm{/}}s,a = 2.0\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2},v = 60.0\,m{\rm{/}}s$,
$t=$ ?
From the equation of motion, $v-u=a t$
$(60 - 10)m{\rm{/}}s = 2\,m/{s^2} \times t$
$\therefore t=25 s$
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of $150\,m{\rm{/}}s$. The ratio of velocity after $3\,s$ and $5\,s$ is $\dfrac{x+1}{x}$. The value of $x$ is (take, $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
Given, initial velocity,
$u = 150\,m{\rm{/}}s;{v_3} = u - g{t_3}$
${v_3} = 150 - 10 \times 3;{v_3} = 120\,m{\rm{/}}s$
$v_{5}=v_{3}-g\left(t_{5}-t_{3}\right)$ $\Rightarrow v_{5}=120-10(5-3)$
$ = 100\,m{\rm{/}}s$
$\therefore \dfrac{x+1}{x}=\dfrac{v_{3}}{v_{5}}=\dfrac{120}{100}=\dfrac{6}{5}$
$\Rightarrow x=5$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Two balls of different masses are thrown vertically upward with same speed. They will pass through their point of projection in the downward direction with the same speed.

Reason: The maximum height and downward velocity attained at the point of projection are independent of the mass of the ball.
explanation: The equations $s=u t-(1 / 2) g t^{2}$ and$v^{2}=u^{2}-2 g s$ are independent of the mass of the object. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A bullet is fired into a fixed target loses one third of its velocity after travelling $4\,cm$. If penetrates further $D \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$ before coming to rest. The value of $D$ is :
explanation:
Using third equation of motion $v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s$
${\left( {\frac{{2v}}{3}} \right)^2} - {v^2} = 2a \times 4 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
As find speed is zero
$\therefore 0 - {v^2} = 2a\left( {D \times {{10}^{ - 3}} + 4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$ \Rightarrow D = 32$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Two balls of different masses are thrown vertically upward with same speed. They will pass through their point of projection in the downward direction with the same speed.

Reason: The maximum height and downward velocity attained at the point of projection are independent of the mass of ball.
explanation:
When two objects are thrown upwards with the same initial velocity, they will reach the same maximum height and then fall back down with the same speed.
The maximum height reached by an object depends solely on its initial velocity and the acceleration due to gravity. The downward velocity at the point of projection (when the object starts its descent) is also determined by the initial velocity and gravity, not the mass of the object. $H_{\max }=\left(u^{2} / 2 g\right)$
Therefore, both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason provides the correct explanation for the assertion. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: If air resistance is considered, then time of ascent and time of descent will be different.

Reason: Magnitudes of acceleration will be different in upward and downward motion.
explanation:
When air resistance is considered, it affects the motion of the object differently during ascent and descent, leading to different times of ascent and descent.
When air resistance is considered, the magnitude of acceleration due to gravity is affected by the opposing force of air resistance. This can result in different accelerations during upward and downward motion. The reason provides a correct explanation for why the assertion is true. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Area under velocity-time graph gives displacement.

Reason: Area under acceleration-time graph gives velocity.
explanation: The area under an acceleration-time graph provides the change in velocity, not the velocity itself. However, these areas are 'vector-sums' of areas as $\vec{s}, \vec{v}, \vec{a}$ are vectors. So correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A body falling (in some case), may do so with constant velocity.

Reason: The body falls freely, when acceleration of a body is equal to acceleration due to gravity.
explanation:
When an object falls freely, it experiences only gravitational force acting vertically downward. This downward force causes the object to accelerate, increasing its velocity, which reaches its maximum when the object makes contact with the ground. $\Rightarrow$ Reason is true.
If the downward acceleration due to gravity is counteracted by an upward retarding force, the object falls at a constant velocity. This constant velocity is referred to as the terminal velocity of the object. $\Rightarrow$ Assertion is true.
But they are different facts. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Velocity - time graph for an object in uniform motion along a straight path is a straight line parallel to the time axis.

Reason: In uniform motion of an object velocity increases as the square of time elapsed.
explanation: In uniform motion, an object moves with a constant velocity, meaning that the magnitude of its velocity remains the same at different instants, such as at $t=0,1$ second, 2 seconds, and so on. Consequently, the velocity-time graph for an object in uniform motion along a straight path is a straight line that runs parallel to the time axis." So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The displacement -time graph of a body moving with uniform acceleration is a straight line.

Reason: The displacement is proportional to time for uniform motion.
explanation:
The displacement of a body moving in straight line with constant acceleration is given by, $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$.
This is a equation of a parabola, not straight line. Therefore the displacement-time graph is a parabola. The displacement time graph will be straight line, if and only if acceleration of body is zero or body is moving with uniform velocity. Uniform motion means constant velocity. So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Two balls are dropped from same height at 1 second interval of time. The separation between the two balls after 4 second of the drop of the $1^{\text {st }}$ ball is:
explanation:
Motion of first ball $u = 0,a = g,t = 4\,{\rm{sec}}$. We are consider that the $s_{1}$ is the distance covered by the first ball in 4 seconds.
$s_{1}=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {(4)^2} = 80\,m$
Let $s_{2}$ be the distance covered by the second ball in 2 seconds. $t = 3\,{\rm{sec}}$.
${s_2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {(3)^2} = 45\,m$
Separation between the two balls
${S_1} - {S_2} = 80{\rm{ }}m - 45{\rm{ }}m = 35{\rm{ }}m$
subject: Physics

question: Two balls of different masses $m_{a}$ and $m_{b}$ are dropped from two different heights $a$ and $b$. The ratio of the time taken by balls to cover these distances are:
explanation:
According to the relation $h=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$h=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \quad(u=0)$
$h \propto t^{2} \quad \therefore t \propto \sqrt{h}$
Hence, $\dfrac{t_{a}}{t_{b}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{b}}$
subject: Physics

question: A man throws a ball vertically upward and it rises through $40\,m$ and returns to his hands, what was the ascent initial velocity of the ball and for how much time $(T)$ it remained in the air?
explanation:
Velocity of descent or ascent always equal.
$u = \sqrt {2 \times g \times h} = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 40} $
$ = \sqrt {800} = 20\sqrt 2 \,m{\rm{/}}s$
Time of ascent and descent always equal.
$T=$ time of ascent + time of descent
$T=t_{1}+t_{2}$
Let $t_{1}$ is the time of ascent and $t_{2}$ be that of descent
$v=u-g t$
$0=u-g t_{1} \quad v=u+g t_{2}$
$u=g t_{1} \quad u=0+g t_{2}$
$t_{1}=\dfrac{u}{g} \quad \dfrac{u}{g}=t_{2}$
$T=t_{1}+t_{2} \quad T=\dfrac{u}{g}+\dfrac{2 u}{g}=\dfrac{2 \times \sqrt{800}}{10}$
$T=4 \times \sqrt{2} \sec T=4 \times 1.41=5.64$ seconds
subject: Physics

question: A particle start from rest with a velocity of $10\,m{\rm{/}}s$ and moves with a constant acceleration till the velocity increases to $100\,m{\rm{/}}s$. At an instant the acceleration is simultaneously reversed, what will be the velocity of the particle when it comes back to the starting point?
explanation:
According to relation $=v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s$
$(100)^{2}-(10)^{2}=2 a s$
$10000-100=2 a s \quad 9900=2 a s$
Now acceleration just reversed $=a=-a$
Particle comes back to original position $v=$ ?
$\begin{array}{ll}v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s & (v)^{2}-(100)^{2}=2(-a) s \\v^{2}=10000-2 a s & v^{2}=10000-2 a s\end{array}$
Put 2 as $ = 9900{v^2} = 100 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {100} = 10\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A juggler maintains four balls in motion making each of them to rise of height of $40\,m$ from his hand, what time interval should be maintain for the proper distance between them?
explanation:
If $t$ is the total time of flight of ball in going up and coming back, then total displacement in time $t$ is zero because ball comes back in hand of juggler.
when ball is going at the highest point then $v=0$
$v^{2}-u^{2}=2$ as $0-u^{2}=2 \times-g \times 40$
$-u^{2}=-80 \times 10$
$u=\sqrt{800} \quad u=2 \sqrt{2} \times 10=20 \sqrt{2}$
Displacement of the ball is zero.
So, $S=u t+\dfrac{1}{2}(-g) t^{2}$
( $g$ is negative because acts in opposite direction)
$0=20 \sqrt{2} t-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2} \quad 20 \sqrt{2} t=5 t^{2}$
$\dfrac{20 \sqrt{2}}{5}=t \quad 4 \sqrt{2}=t$
$\therefore$ Time interval of each ball $\frac{{4\sqrt 2 }}{4} = \sqrt 2 = 1.414\,{\rm{sec}}$
subject: Physics

question: A body starting from rest moves with constant acceleration. The ratio of distance covered by the body during $8^{\text {th }}$ second to that covered in 8 seconds is:
explanation:
Distance covered in $8^{\text {th }}$ second
$S_{8^{t h}}=u+\dfrac{a}{2}(2 n-1)=0+\dfrac{a}{2}(2 \times 8-1)$
$S_{8^{t h}}=\dfrac{a}{2}(15)$
Distance covered in 8 second-$S=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow 0+\dfrac{1}{2} a \times 64=64\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)$
Ratio, $\dfrac{S_{8^{t h}}}{S_{8}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{a}{2}(15)}{\dfrac{a}{2}(64)}=\dfrac{15}{64}$
subject: Physics

question: A car starting from rest accelerates at the rate $f$ through a distance $s$, then continues at a constant speed for time $t$ and then decelerates at the rate $f / 2$ to come to rest. If the total time taken is $15 s$, then
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956103/preptracker/questions/q_39771_explanation_1764956103132.png $v=\sqrt{2 f s} \Rightarrow 12 s=\sqrt{2 f s t}$
$\Rightarrow 144 s^{2}=2 f s t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow s=\dfrac{1}{72} f t^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A parachutist after bailing out falls $50\,m$ without friction. When parachute opens, it decelerates at $2\,m{s^{ - 2}}$. He reaches the ground with a speed of $3\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. At what height, did he bail out?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956102/preptracker/questions/q_39767_explanation_1764956101675.png ${h_1} = 50\,m$
$v=\sqrt{2 g h_{1}}=\sqrt{980}$
$\Rightarrow h_{2}=\dfrac{v^{2}-9}{2 a}=\dfrac{980-9}{4}=\dfrac{971}{4}$
$\Rightarrow h_{1}+h_{2}=\dfrac{1174}{4} \approx 293 m$
subject: Physics

question: When a ball is thrown up vertically with velocity $v_{0}$, it reaches a maximum height of $h$. If one wishes to triple the maximum height, then the ball should be thrown with velocity
explanation:
As, $v^{2}=u^{2}-2 g h \Rightarrow u^{2} \propto h$
Hence, $\dfrac{u_{1}}{u_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{h_{1}}{h_{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{0}}{u_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{h}{3 h}}$ (given)
$\Rightarrow u_{2}=\sqrt{3} v_{0}$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet from a gun is fired on a rectangular 	wooden block with velocity $u$. When bullet 	travels $24\,cm$ through the block along its length horizontally, velocity of bullet becomes $\frac{u}{3}$. Then it further penetrates into the block in the same direction before coming to rest exactly at the other end of the block. The total length of the block is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950801/preptracker/questions/q_39755_explanation_1764950801020.png ${\upsilon ^2} - {u^2} = 2\,as$
for $s = {x_1},\,\,\upsilon = \frac{u}{3}$
${\left( {\frac{u}{3}} \right)^2} - {u^2} = 2\,a{x_1}$
$ \Rightarrow 2\,a{x_1} = \frac{{ - 8\,{u^2}}}{9}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Then, it comes to rest at $s = {x_2} \Rightarrow \upsilon = 0$
$0 - {u^2} = 2\,a{x_2}$
$2\,a{x_2} = - {u^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2)
$\frac{{{x_1}}}{{{x_2}}} = \frac{8}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{{24}}{{{x_2}}} = \frac{8}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow {x_2} = 27\,cm$
Correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A horizontal bridge is built across a river. A 	student standing on the bridge throws a small ball vertically upwards with a velocity $4\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$The ball strikes the water surface after $4{\rm{ }}s$. The height of bridge above water surface is ( Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957794/preptracker/questions/q_39751_explanation_1764957793979.png $S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
$ - H = 4 \times 4 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {4^2}$
$ \Rightarrow H = 64\,m$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The speed $v$ of a car moving on a straight road changes according to the equation, ${v^2} = a + {\rm{b }}x$, where $a$ and $b$ are positive constants. The magnitude of acceleration in the course of such motion: ($x$ is the distance travelled)
explanation:
Given that
$v^2 = a + bx$
$2v \frac {dv}{dt} = b \frac {dx}{xt}$
$\Rightarrow \frac {dv}{dt} = \frac {b}{2}$ = Constant
subject: Physics

question: A body $A$  starts  from rest with an acceleration ${a_1}$. After 2 seconds, another body $B$ starts  from rest with an acceleration ${a_2}$. If they travel  equal distance in the ${5^{th}}$ second, after the start of $A$, then the ratio ${a_1}:{a_2}$ is equal to:
explanation:
Distance covered by first body in ${5^{th}}$ second after start,
${S_5} = 0 + \frac{{{a_1}}}{2}\left( {2 \times 5 - 1} \right) = \frac{{9{a_1}}}{2}$
${5^{th}}$ second of first body will be ${3^{rd}}$ second of second body. Distance covered by the second body in the ${3^{rd}}$ second after its start.
${S_3} = 0 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{2}\left( {2 \times 3 - 1} \right) = \frac{{5{a_2}}}{2}$
Since ${S_5} = {S_3} \Rightarrow {a_1}:{a_2} = 5:9$
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration $a$ of a particle starting from rest varies with time according to relation $a = \alpha t + \beta $. The velocity of the particle after a time $t$ will be
explanation:
According to given relation acceleration $a = \alpha t + \beta$
As $a = \frac {dv}{dt} \Rightarrow \alpha t + \beta = \frac {dv}{dt}$
Since particle starts from rest, its initial velocity is zero i.e., at time $t = 0$, velocity = $0$
$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{v} dv = \int_{0}^{t} (\alpha t + \beta) \, dt \Rightarrow v = \frac {\alpha{t^2}}{2} + \beta t$.
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of a bullet is reduced from $200\,m/s$ to 100 $m/s$, while travelling through a wooden block of thickness $10\,cm$. Assuming it to be  uniform, the magnitude of acceleration will be:
explanation:
From equation of motion
${v^2} = {u^2} + 2as$
Given $v = 100\, m/s, \, u = 200\, m/s$
$s = 10\, cm = 10 \times {10^{ - 2}} \, m$
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {100} \right)^2} = {\left( {200} \right)^2} - 2 \times a \times 10 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$a = 15 \times {10^4} \, m/s^2$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving along $x$ - axis, under the action of a variable force which is providing  position varying acceleration described by the equation, $a = 3x - 4$ at $t = 0,x = \frac{4}{3}m$ and $v = 0$. Find the velocity and position of particle at $t = 5s$.
explanation:
In the given question, acceleration at $t = 0$ is $3 \times \frac {4}{3} - 4 = 0$ and velocity is also zero at $t = 0$.
So, particle will remain at rest. Hence, $v = 0$ and $x = \frac {4}{3} \, m$.
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of a body depends on time according to the equation $v = 20 + 0.1{t^2}$. The body is undergoing
explanation: Acceleration $a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 0.1 \times 2t = 0.2t$ Which is time dependent, i.e., non-uniform acceleration
subject: Physics

question: A bullet fired into a fixed target loses half of its velocity after penetrating 1 $cm$. How much  further it will penetrate before coming to rest assuming that it faces constant resistance to motion?
explanation:
Let initial velocity of the bullet be $u$
After penetrating 1 $cm$ its velocity becomes $\frac{u}{2}$
From ${v^2} = {u^2} - 2as$
${\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} = {u^2} - 2a\left( 1 \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 2a = \frac{{3{u^2}}}{4} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{3{u^2}}}{8}$
Further it will penetrate through distance $x$ and stops.
For distance $x,v = 0,\,u = u/2,s = x,a = 3{u^2}/8$
From ${v^2} - {u^2} = 2as \Rightarrow 0 = {\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} - 2\left( {\frac{{3{u^2}}}{8}} \right) \cdot x$
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{3} \, cm$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet emerges from a barrel of length $1.2\,m$ with a speed of $640\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. Assuming contant acceleration, the approximate time that it spends in the barrel after the gun is fired is
explanation:
$s = 1.2m,\,v = 640 \, m{s^{ - 1}}$
$a = ?;u = 0;\,t = ?$
$2as = {v^2} - {u^2}$
$ \Rightarrow 2a \times 1.2 = 640 \times 640 \Rightarrow a = \frac{{8 \times 64 \times {{10}^3}}}{3}$
$v - u = at \Rightarrow t = \frac{v}{a} = \frac{{15}}{4} \times {10^{ - 3}}$
$ = 3.75 \times {10^{ - 2}}s = 4 \,ms$
subject: Physics

question: You drop a ball from a window located on an upper floor of a building. It strikes the ground with speed $v$. You now repeat the drop, but your friend down on the ground throws another ball upward at the same speed $v$, releasing her ball at the same moment that you drop yours from the window. At some location, the balls pass each other. This location is
explanation: The ball has zero initial speed and smaller average speed during the time of flight to the passing point. So the ball must travel a smaller distance to the passing point than the ball your friend throws.

options.text: At the halfway point between window and ground

subject: Physics

question: If two balls of same density but of different masses are dropped from a height of 100 $m$, then

options.text: First will come first and second after that
Second will come first and first after that

subject: Physics

question: A body is dropped from a certain height
explanation: During first half, velocities are lesser as compared to velocities during second half. So more time is taken in first half.

options.text: The time taken to travel first half of the height is less than that for second half
The time taken to travel first half of the height is greater than that for second half
The time taken to travel first half of the height is equal to that for second half
Any one of the above three situations may be correct depending upon the value of  height

subject: Physics

question: A body is dropped from a height of 40 $m$. After it crosses half distance, the acceleration due to gravity ceases to act. The body will hit the ground with velocity (Take $g = 10m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Suppose $v$ be the velocity of the body after falling through half the distance. Then
$s = \frac{{40}}{2} = 20m,\,u = 0\,{\rm{and }}g = 10m/{s^2}$
${v^2} = {u^2} + 2gh = {0^2} + 2 \times 10 \times 20$
$ \Rightarrow \quad v = 20 \, m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A small body is dropped from a rising balloon. A person $A$ stands on ground, while another person $B$ is on the balloon. Choose the correct  statement : Immediately, after the body is   released.

options.text: $A$ and $B$, both feel that the body is coming (going) down.
$A$ and $B$, both feel that body is coming up.
$A$ feels that the body is coming down, while $B$ feels that the body is going up.
$A$ feels that the body is going up, while $B$ feels that the body is going down.

subject: Physics

question: A balloon is at a height of 81 $m$ and is ascending upwards with a velocity of $12\,m/s$. A body of $2 \, kg$ weight is dropped from it. If $g = 10\,m/{\sec ^2},$ the body will reach the surface of the earth in
explanation:
$h = ut + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} \Rightarrow 81 = - 12t + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow t = 5.4\sec $
subject: Physics

question: With what velocity a ball should be projected vertically so that the distance covered by it in ${5^{th}}$ second is twice the distance it covers in its ${6^{th}}$ second $(g = 10m/{s^2})$?
explanation:
Let $u$ be the initial speed
Given that
$u - 10(5 - \frac{1}{2}) = 2\left[ {u - 10(6 - \frac{1}{2})} \right]$
$u - 45 = 2u - 110$
$u = 65 \, m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: ${B_1},{B_2}\,{\rm{and}}\,{B_3}$ are three balloons ascending with velocities $v,2v\,{\rm{and}}\,3v$, respectively. If a bomb is dropped from each balloon when they are at the same height, then
explanation:
Let $h$ is the initial height of the balloons and $u$ be the initial velocity of the bomb with respect to ground.
$ - h = ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} \Rightarrow h = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} - ut$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} - ut - h = 0$
$t = \frac{{u + \sqrt {{u^2} + 2gh} }}{g}$
So as $u$ increases, $t$ increases.
subject: Physics

question: A body dropped from top of a tower fall through 40 $m$ during the last two seconds of its fall. The height of tower is $(g = {\rm{10\, m/}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}})$
explanation:
Let $t$ is the total time taken by the ball.
From $D_n = u + \frac{a}{2}\left( {2n - 1} \right)$ we have total distance travelled in last 2 seconds of fall is
$D = {D_t} + D_{\left( {t - 1} \right)}$
$ = \left[ {0 + \frac{g}{2}\left( {2t - 1} \right)} \right] + \left[ {0 + \frac{g}{2}\left\{ {2\left( {t - 1} \right) - 1} \right\}} \right]$
$ = \frac{g}{2}\left( {4t - 4} \right) = \frac{{10}}{2} \times 4\left( {t - 1} \right)$
or, $40 = 20\left(t - 1 \right)$ or $t = 2 + 1 = 3 \, s$
Distance travelled in $t$ second is
$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^{_2}} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {3^2} = 45 \, m$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet fired into a fixed target loses half of its velocity after penetrating $3\,cm$. How much  further it will penetrate before coming to rest assuming that it faces constant resistance to motion
explanation:
Let initial velocity of the bullet $ = u$
After penetrating $3\,cm$ its velocity becomes $\frac{u}{2}$
From ${v^2} = {u^2} - 2as$
${\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} = {u^2} - 2a(3)$
$ \Rightarrow 6a = \frac{{3{u^2}}}{4} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{{u^2}}}{8}$
Further it will penetrate through distance $x$
For distance $x,\,v = 0,\,u = u/2,\,s = x,\,a = {u^2}/8$
From ${v^2} = {u^2} - 2as \Rightarrow 0 = {\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} - 2{\left( {\frac{u}{8}} \right)^2} \cdot x$
$ \Rightarrow x = 1\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A body starting from rest moves with constant acceleration. The ratio of distance covered by the body during the ${5^{th}}$ sec to that covered in 5 sec is
explanation:
Distance covered by the body ${5^{th}}$ second.
${S_{{5^{th}}}} = ut + \frac{a}{2}\left( {2n - 1} \right) = 0 + \frac{a}{2}\left( {2 \times 5 - 1} \right) = \frac{{9a}}{2}$
and distance covered in 5 second,
${S_5} = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}a \times 25 = \frac{{25a}}{2}$
$\therefore \,\frac{{{S_{{5^{th}}}}}}{{{S_5}}} = \frac{9}{{25}}$
subject: Physics

question: An object moving with a speed of $6.25\,m/s$, is decelerated at a rate given by $\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} =  - 2.5\sqrt v $ where $v$ is the instantaneous  speed. The time taken by the object, to come to rest, would be
explanation:
$\int\limits_{6.25}^0 {\frac{{dv}}{{\sqrt v }}} = - 2.5\int\limits_0^t {dt} $
$\left| {2\sqrt v } \right|_{6.25}^0 = - 2.5t$
$2\sqrt {6.25} = 2.5t$
$t = 2s$
subject: Physics

question: The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest and moving with an acceleration $\frac{4}{3} \,ms^{-2}$, in the third second is
explanation:
Distance travelled in the nth second is given by $d = u + \frac{a}{2}\left(2n - 1 \right)$
Put $u = 0, \, a = \frac{4}{3} \, ms^{-2}, \, n = 3$
$\therefore d = 0 + \frac{4}{{3 \times 2}}\left( {2 \times 3 - 1} \right) = \frac{4}{6} \times 5 = \frac{{10}}{3} \, m$
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration of a particle is increasing linearly with time as $bt$. The particle starts from the  origin with aninitial velocity ${v_0}$. The distance travelled by the particle in time $t$ will be
explanation:
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = bt \Rightarrow dv = bt$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{b{t^2}}}{2} + {K_1}$
At $t = 0,\,v = {v_0}\, \Rightarrow {K_1} = {v_0}$
Again $\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{2}b{t^2} + {v_0} \Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{b{t^3}}}{3} + {v_0}t + {K_2}$
At $t = 0,\,x = 0 \Rightarrow {K_2} = 0$
$\therefore \quad x = \frac{1}{6}b{t^3} + {v_0}t$
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped on the floor from a height of $10\,m$. It rebounds to a height of $2.5\,m$. If the ball is in contact with the floor for $0.01\,s$, then the average acceleration during contact is nearly
explanation:
Before collision the initial velocity is
${{{\rm{\vec v}}}_i} = - \sqrt {2g{h_1}} \hat j$ , after collision the velocity is $\overrightarrow {{v_f}} = \sqrt {2g{h_2}} \hat j$
${\rm{\vec a}} = \frac{{{{\vec v}_{\rm{f}}} - {{\vec v}_{\rm{i}}}}}{{\Delta {\rm{t}}}} = \frac{{\left( {\sqrt {2{\rm{g}}{{\rm{h}}_2}} {\rm{ + }}\sqrt {2{\rm{g}}{{\rm{h}}_1}} } \right){\rm{\hat j}}}}{{\Delta {\rm{t}}}}$
$\left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {2g} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {{h_1}} + \sqrt {{h_2}} } \right]}}{{\Delta t}}$
$ = 1500\sqrt {2\,} m/{s^2}$ upward
subject: Physics

question: To reach the same height on the moon as on the earth, a body must be projected up with
explanation:
Value of $g$ is small on the moon.
So a lower velocity is required to project on the moon.

options.text: Same velocity on the moon and earth.
It depends on the mass of the body.

subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown straight up in the air. For which situation are both the instantaneous velocity and the acceleration zero?
explanation: Once the ball has left the thrower’s hand, it is a freely falling body with a constant, non-zero, acceleration of $a =  - g$

options.text: At the top of its flight path

subject: Physics

question: At the maximum height of a body thrown vertically up

options.text: Velocity is not zero but acceleration is zero.
Acceleration is not zero but velocity is zero.
Both acceleration and velocity are zero.
Both acceleration and velocity are not zero.

subject: Physics

question: A stone thrown upward with a speed $u$ from the top of the tower reaches the ground with a velocity 3$u$. The height of the tower is
explanation: ${v^2} = {u^2} + 2gh \Rightarrow {\left( {3u} \right)^2} = {\left( { - u} \right)^2} + 2gh \Rightarrow h = \frac{{4{u^2}}}{g}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball is thrown up under gravity $\left( {g = 10\,m/{{\sec }^2}} \right)$. Find its velocity after $1.0\,\sec $ at a height of $10\,m$.
explanation:
$h = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} \Rightarrow 10 = u \times 1 - 5$
${(1)^2} \Rightarrow u = 15 \, m{s^{ - 1}};\,V = u + at$
$ = 15 - 10 \times 1 = 5 \, m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two bodies of different masses are dropped simultaneously from the top of a tower.  If air resistance is proportional to the mass of the body, then,
explanation:
$f = mk$
$mg + mk = ma$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,a = g + k$ (where $k$ is proportionality constant).
Acceleration is same for both bodies

options.text: The heavier body reaches the ground earlier
The lighter body reaches the ground earlier.
Both the bodies reach the ground simultaneously.

subject: Physics

question: A man standing in a lift falling under gravity releases a ball from his hand.  As seen by him,  the ball
subject: Physics

question: A body is falling freely. Find the ratio of distances in ${1^{st}},{2^{nd}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{3^{rd}}$ seconds.
explanation:
Consider the downward direction as positive
Given $u = 0,\,a = g$
${D_n} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}a\left( {2n - 1)} \right)$
${D_1}:{D_2}:{D_3} = \frac{1}{2} \, g\left( {2\left( 1 \right) - 1} \right):$
$\frac{1}{2} \, g\left( {2\left( 2 \right) - 1} \right):\frac{1}{2} \, g\left( {2\left( 3 \right) - 1} \right)$
$ = 1\,:3\,:5$
subject: Physics

question: If a freely falling body travels in the last second a distance equal to the distance travelled by it in the first three second, the time of the travel is
explanation:
The distance travelled in last second.
${S_{Last}} = u + \frac{g}{2}\left( {2t - 1} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8\left( {2t - 1} \right) = 4.9\left( {2t - 1} \right)$
and distance travelled in first three seconds,
${S_{Three}} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times 9 = 44.1m$
According to problem ${S_{Last}} = {S_{Three}}$
$ \Rightarrow 4.9\left( {2t - 1} \right) = 44.1 \Rightarrow 2t - 1 = 9$
$ \Rightarrow t = 5\sec .$
subject: Physics

question: A ball thrown vertically upwards after reaching a maximum height $h$ returns to the starting point after a time of 10 $s$. Its displacement is
explanation: The net displacement of the body is equal to zero
subject: Physics

question: From a building two balls $A$ and $B$ are thrown such that $A$ is thrown upwards and $B$ downwards (both vertically) with the same speed. If ${V_A}\,{\rm{and}}\,{V_B}$ are their respective velocities on reaching the ground then :-

options.text: These velocities depend on their masses

subject: Physics

question: A particle is released from rest from a tower of height 3 $h$. The ratio of times to fall equal heights $h$, is
subject: Physics

question: A ball is released from the top of a tower of height $h$ meters. It takes $T$ seconds to reach the ground. What is the position of the ball at  $\frac{T}{3}$second
explanation:
We have, $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{ }}h = \frac{1}{2}g{T^2}\,\,\left( {\therefore u = 0} \right)$
In $T/3$ second, vertical distance moved is given by $h' = \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {\frac{T}{3}} \right)^2} \Rightarrow h' = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{g{T^2}}}{9} = \frac{h}{9}$
$\therefore $ Position of ball from ground $ = h - \frac{h}{9} = \frac{{8h}}{9}$
subject: Physics

question: Acceleration is given as a function of displacement $a = 2x - 3{x^2}$ starting with velocity is $2m/s$. Find the final velocity after a  displacement from 0 to 3 $m$.
explanation:
Given that $a = 2x - 3{x^2}$
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}v = 2x - 3{x^2}$
$\int\limits_2^v {vdv = \int\limits_0^3 {\left( {2x - 3{x^2}} \right)dx} } $
$\frac{{{v^2} - 4}}{2} = \left[ {{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]_0^3$
${v^2} - 4 = - 36$
${v^2} = - 32$
Velocity cannot be imaginary that means particles cannot move from 0 to 3 $m$. Particle will stop before reaching 3 $m$.
subject: Physics

question: A particle of unit mass undergoes one-dimensional motion such that its velocity varies according to $v\left( x \right) = b{x^{ - 2n}}$. Where $b$ and $n$ are  constants and $x$ is the position of the particle. The acceleration of the particle as function of $x$, is given by
subject: Physics

question: A small block slides without friction down an inclined plane starting from rest. Let ${S_n}$ be the distance travelled from time $t = n - 1\,{\rm{to}}\,t = n$. Then $\frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_{n + 1}}}}$ is
explanation:
${S_n} = \frac{1}{2}g\sin \theta \left( {2n - 1} \right),$
${S_{n + 1}} = \frac{1}{2}g\sin \theta \{ 2\left( {n + 1} \right) - 1\} $
$\frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_{n + 1}}}} = \frac{{2n - 1}}{{2n + 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two cars $A$ and $B$ are travelling in the same direction with velocities ${v_{\rm{A}}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{v_B}\left( {{v_{\rm{A}}} > {v_{\rm{B}}}} \right)$. The initial seperation between the cars is $d$.  When the car $A$ applies brakes producing a uniform retardation $a$. There will be no collision when
explanation:
Let $d$ is the initial separation between the cars.
When the two cars meet each other.
${v_A}t - \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = d + {v_B}t$
$\frac{a}{2}{t^2} + \left( {{v_B} - {v_A}} \right)t + d = 0$
The cars meet when
${\left( {{v_B} - {v_A}} \right)^2} - 4\frac{a}{2}d \ge 0$
${\left( {{v_B} - {v_A}} \right)^2} \ge 2ad$
$d \le \frac{{{{\left( {{v_A} - {v_B}} \right)}^2}}}{{2a}}$
The cars will not collide if $d > \frac{{{{\left( {{v_A} - {v_B}} \right)}^2}}}{{2a}}$
subject: Physics

question: A frictionless wire $AB$ is fixed on a sphere of radius $R$. A very small spherial ball slips on this wire. The time taken by this ball to slip from $A$ to $B$ is
explanation:
Acceleration of a body along $AB$ is $g\,\cos \theta \,$
Distance travelled in time
$t\sec = AB = \frac{1}{2}(g\cos \theta ){t^2}$
From
$\Delta ABC,AB = 2R\cos \theta \Rightarrow 2R\cos \theta = \frac{1}{2}g\cos \theta {t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {t^2} = \frac{{4R}}{g}{\rm{ or }}t = 2\sqrt {\frac{R}{g}} \,$
Here ‘$t$’ is independent of $'\theta '\,$
subject: Physics

question:
A body is projected vertically downwards from $A$, the top of the tower, reaches the ground in ${t_1}$ seconds. If it is projected upwards with same speed, it reaches the ground in ${t_2}$ seconds. At what time will it reach the ground if it is dropped from $A$?
$[{t_1} = 8\,{\rm{units}},\,{t_2} = 16\,{\rm{units}}]$
explanation:
For downward motion,
$h = u{t_1} + \frac{1}{2}gt_1^2$   (1)
For upward motion,
$h = u{t_2} + \frac{1}{2}gt_2^2$   (2)
From (1) & (2)
$u{t_1} + \frac{1}{2}gt_1^2 = - u{t_2} + \frac{1}{2}gt_2^2$
$ \Rightarrow \quad u\left( {{t_1} + {t_2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}g\left( {t_2^2 - t_1^2} \right)\,$
$\therefore \quad h = \frac{g}{2}\left( {{t_1} - {t_1}} \right){t_1} + \frac{g}{2}gt_1^2 = \frac{{g{t_2}{t_1}}}{2}$
$\therefore \,$ Required time $t = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{g}} = \sqrt {{t_1}{t_2}} $
$ = \sqrt {8 \times 16} = 8\sqrt 2 \,$
subject: Physics

question: A man throws balls with the same speed vertically upwards one after the other at an   interval of 2 $s$. What should be the speed of the throw so that more than two balls are in the sky at any  time? (Given $g$  =  9.8 $m^{-2}$)
explanation:
Let time taken by ball to reach maximum height be $T$.
Also $v = u - gT$
At maximum height, $v = 0\,$
So, $u = gT$
$u = 2 \times 9.8 = 19.6 \, ms^{-1}$
hence, the speed should be more than
$19.6 \, ms^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball is dropped into a well in which the water level is at a depth $h$ below the top. If the speed of sound is $c$, then the time after which the splash is heard will be given by
explanation:
Time of fall $ = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{g}} $
Time taken by the sound to come out $ = \frac{h}{c}$
Total time $ = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{g}} + \frac{h}{c} = h\left[ {\sqrt {\frac{2}{{gh}}} + \frac{1}{c}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: A car, starting from rest, accelerates at the rate $f$ through a distance $S$, then continues at constant speed for time $t$ and then decelerates at the rates $\frac{f}{2}$ to come to rest. If the total distance travelled is 15 $S$, then
explanation:
Let car start from point $A$ from rest and moves up to point $B$ with acceleration $f$
Velocity of car at point $B$, $\upsilon = \sqrt {2f\,S} $
$[{\rm{As}}\,{\upsilon ^2} = {u^2} + 2as]$
Car moves distance $BC$ with this constant velocity in time $t$
$x = \sqrt {2fS} .t[{\rm{As}}\,s = ut]$   (1)
So the velocity of car at point $C$ also will be
$\sqrt {2fS} $ and finally car stops after covering distance $y$.
Distance $CD \Rightarrow y = \frac{{{{\left( {\sqrt {2fS} } \right)}^2}}}{{2\left( {f/2} \right)}} = \frac{{2fS}}{f} = 2S$   (2)
So, the total distance $AD = AB + BC + CD = 45S$ [Given]
$ \Rightarrow S + x + 2S = 15S \Rightarrow x = 12 \, S$
Substituting the values of $x$ in equation (1) we get
$x = \sqrt {2fS} .t \Rightarrow 12S = \sqrt {2fS} .t \Rightarrow 144{S^2} = 2fS.{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow S = \frac{1}{{72}}f{t^2}$.
subject: Physics

question: A car acceleration from rest at a constant rate $\alpha $ for some time, after which it decelerates at a constant rate $\beta $ and comes to rest. If the total time elapsed is $t$, then the maximum velocity acquired by the car is
explanation:
Let the car accelerate at rate $\alpha $ for time ${t_1}$ then maximum velocity attained,
$v = 0 + \alpha {t_1} = \alpha {t_1}$
Now, the card decelerates at a rate $\beta $ for time $(t - {t_1})$ and finally comes to rest. Then ,
$0 = v - \beta (t - {t_1}) \Rightarrow 0 = \alpha {t_1} - \beta t + \beta {t_1}$
$ \Rightarrow {t_1} = \frac{\beta }{{\alpha + \beta }}t$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,v = \frac{{\alpha \beta }}{{\alpha + \beta }}t$
subject: Physics

question: A body $A$  starts  from rest with an acceleration ${a_1}$. After 2 seconds, another body $B$ starts from rest with an acceleration ${a_2}$. If they travel  equal distance in the ${5^{th}}$ second, after the start of $A$, then the ratio ${a_1}:{a_2}$ is equal to:
explanation:
According to problem, distance travelled by body $A$ in $5^{th}$ sec and distance travelled by body $B$ in $3^{rd}$ sec of its motion are equal.
$0+\frac {a_1}{2}(5^2 - 4^2) = 0 + \frac{a_2}{2} [3^2 -2^2]$
$9a_1 = 5a_2 \Rightarrow \frac {a_1}{a_2} = \frac {5}{9}$
subject: Physics

question: A body is projected downward with speed $20\,m/s$ from the top of a tower of height 50 $m$. Find the time at which it hits the ground.
explanation:
Method-1
Consider the origin at the top of the tower and downward is positive and upward is negative
${S_0} = 0$
$u = 20\,m/s$ (Going towards $\left( + \right)ve$ direction)
$a = g$ (Accelerated motion)
$s = 20t + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
When it hits the ground
$s = 50\,m$
$50 = 20t + \frac{1}{2}10{t^2}$
${t^2} + 4t - 10 = 0$
$t = \frac{{ - 4 \pm \sqrt {56} }}{2} = \sqrt {14} - 2$
Method 2
If we do the problem graphically we can avoid quadratic equation. When big numbers are involved solving quadratic equation is difficult.
The graph between $v$ and $t$ is a straight line
since $a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = g = {\rm{constant}}$
The velocity when the body touches the ground is ${\rm{v}}_{\max }^2 = {20^2} + 2 \times 10 \times 50 \Rightarrow {v_{\max }} = 10\sqrt {14} $
Total distance travelled = Area of the graph
$50 = 20 \times t + \frac{1}{2} \times t \times \left( {10\sqrt {14} - 20} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{10}}{{2 + \sqrt {14} }}$
on rationalising we get $t = \sqrt {14} - 2$
subject: Physics

question: An object is dropped from a height $h$, then the distance travelled in times $t,2t,3t$ are in the ratio
subject: Physics

question: The velocity $v$ of a particle is given by the equation $v = 6{t^2} - 6{t^3}$, where $v$ is in $m/\sec $ and $t$ is time in seconds then :-
explanation:
Given $v = 6{t^2} - 6{t^3}$
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12t - 18{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{t^2}}} = 12 - 36t$
When $v$ is maxima or minima
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12t - 18{t^2} = 0 \Rightarrow t = \frac{2}{3}s,t = 0$
${\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{t^2}}}} \right|_{t = 0}} = 12({\rm{minima}})$
${\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{t^2}}}} \right|_{t = \frac{2}{3}s}} = - 12{\rm{(maxima)}}$
${v_{\min }} = 6{(0)^2} - 6{(0)^3} = 0$
subject: Physics

question: A stone thrown upwards with speed $u$ attains maximum height $h$. Another stone thrown upwards from the same point with speed $2u$ attains maximum height $H$. What is the relation between $h$ and $H$?
explanation:
Maximum height attained $\alpha \,{u^2}$
$\frac{H}{h} = \frac{{\left( {2{u^2}} \right)}}{{{u^2}}} = 4$
$H = 4\,h$
subject: Physics

question: A person throwing two balls in the air one after the other. He throws the second ball when the first ball is at the highest point. If he is throwing the balls every second, how high do they rise?
explanation:
Time of ascent
$ = 1s \Rightarrow \frac{u}{g} = 1 \Rightarrow u = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
${\rm{height }}(h) = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} = \frac{{{{10}^2}}}{{2 \times 10}} = 5 \, m$
subject: Physics

question: From a tower of height $H$, a particle is thrown vertically  upwards with a  speed $u$. The time taken by the particle, to hit the ground, is $n$ times that is taken by it to reach the highest point of its path. The relation between $H,u\,{\rm{and}}\,n\,{\rm{is}}\,:$
explanation:
Time to reach the maximum height
${t_1} = \frac{u}{g}$
If ${t_2}$ be the time taken to hit the ground
$ - H = u{t_2} - \frac{1}{2}gt_2^2$
But ${t_2} = n{t_1}$(given)
$ \Rightarrow - H = \frac{{n{u^2}}}{g} - \frac{1}{2}g\frac{{{n^2}{u^2}}}{{{g^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow 2gH = n{u^2}(n - 2)$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform moving cricket ball is turned back by hitting it with a bat for a very short time interval. The correct representation of variation of its acceleration with time from the following graphs. (Take acceleration in the backward direction as positive)
explanation: When the bat hits the ball first the ball decelerates and then it accelerates. In both cases the bat exerts force in backward direction only so $a = ( + )ve$ throughout the motion. It increases from 0 and reaches maximum value and then decreases to zero.
subject: Physics

question: A body is projected vertically upward from the surface of the earth, then the velocity time graph is
explanation: The velocity of the body as a function of time is $v = u - gt$. The graph is a straight line having $( - )ve$ slope. The correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A car moving along a straight highway with speed of $126\,km/h$ is brought to a stop within a distance of 200 $m$. What is the retardation of the car (assumed uniform) and how long does it take for the car to stop?
explanation:
Initial velocity of the car $(u) = 126\,km/h$
$ = 126 \times \frac{5}{{18}}m/s = 35\,m/s$ $\left( {\because 1km/h = \frac{5}{{18}}m/s} \right)$
Final velocity of the car $(v) = 0$
Distance travelled $(s) = 200\,m$
From equation of motion, ${v^2} = {u^2} + 2as$
or $a = \frac{{{v^2} - {u^2}}}{{2s}} = \frac{{0 - {{\left( {35} \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times 200}} = - 3.06 \, m/{s^2}$
$\therefore $ Retardation of motion,
$v = u + at$
$t = \frac{{v - u}}{a} = \frac{{\left( {0 - 35} \right)}}{{\left( { - 49/16} \right)}} = 11.4\,s$
$\therefore $ Car will stop after $11.4\,s$
subject: Physics

question: In the rectilinear motion of a body accelearation is $a = 6 - 3v$, where $v$ is velocity. If the body  starts from rest, its velocity as a function of time $t$ is
explanation:
Given, $\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 6 - 3v$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{6 - 3v}} = dt$
Integrating it, we have
$\frac{{ - 1}}{3}\left[ {\ln [6 - 3v]} \right]_0^v = t$
$\ln \left( {\frac{{6 - 3v}}{6}} \right) = - 3t$
$\frac{{6 - 3v}}{6} = {e^{ - 3t}}$
$1 - \frac{v}{2} = {e^{ - 3t}} \Rightarrow v = 2\left( {1 - {e^{ - 3t}}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question:
Doubling the initial velocity, the stopping distance of moving object increases by a factor of 4 (for the same deceleration).
Stopping distance is proportional to the initial velocity.
explanation:
Let the distance travelled by the vehicle before it stops be $x$, then using ${v^2} = {u^2} = 2ax$.
Put $v = 0$, we get $x = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2a}}$
Thus, the stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A small block slides down on a smooth inclined plane, starting from rest at time $t = 0$. Let ${s_n}$ be the distance travelled by the block in the interval $t = n - 1$ to $t = n$. Then , the ratio $\frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_{n + 1}}}}$ is
explanation:
$S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {S_n} = un + \frac{1}{2}a{n^2} - \left[ {u\left( {n - 1} \right) + \frac{1}{2}a{{\left( {n - 1} \right)}^2}} \right]$
$ = u + \frac{1}{2}a\left( {2n - 1} \right)$
$\frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_{n + 1}}}} = \frac{{u + \frac{1}{2}a\left( {2n - 1} \right)}}{{u + \frac{1}{2}a\left\{ {2\left( {n + 1} \right) - 1} \right\}}}$
Given that $u = 0$
$ = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}a\left( {2n - 1} \right)}}{{\frac{1}{2}a\left( {2n + 1} \right)}} = \frac{{2n - 1}}{{2n + 1}}$
subject: Physics

question:
A ball is thrown vertically downward with a velocity of $20\,m/s$ from the top of a tower. It hits the ground after some time with a velocity of $80\,m/s$.The height of the tower is:
$(g = 10\,m/{s^2})$
subject: Physics

question: When an object is shot from the bottom of a long smooth inclined plane kept at an angle $60^\circ $ with horizontal, it can travel a distance ${x_1}$ along the plane. But when the inclination is decreased to $30^\circ $ and the same object is shot with the same velocity, it can travel ${x_2}$ distance Then ${x_1}:{x_2}$ will be:
explanation:
Stopping distance on, an incline plane that makes an angle $\theta $ w.r.t. horizontal is $x = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g\sin \theta }}$
${x_1} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g\sin 60^\circ }}$ and ${x_2} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g\sin 30^\circ }}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x_1}}}{{{x_2}}} = \frac{{\sin 30^\circ }}{{\sin 60^\circ }} = \frac{{1 \times 2}}{{2 \times \sqrt 3 }} = 1:\sqrt 3 $
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of the distances travelled by a freely falling body in the ${1^{st}},{2^{nd}},{3^{rd}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{4^{th}}$ second
explanation:
${S_{nth}} = u + \frac{a}{2}\left( {2n - 1} \right)$
$ = 0 + \frac{a}{2}\left( {2n - 1} \right)$
${S_{nth}} \propto \left( {2n - 1} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {S_{1st}},{S_{2nd}},{S_{3rd}},{S_{4th}}$
$ = \left[ {2\left( 1 \right) - 1} \right]:\left[ {2\left( 2 \right) - 1} \right]:\left[ {2\left( 3 \right) - 1} \right]:\left[ {2\left( 4 \right) - 1} \right]$
$ = 1:3:5:7$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves in a straight line with a constant acceleration. It changes its velocity from  $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ to $20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ while passing through a distance $135\,m$ in $t$ sec. The value of $t$ is
subject: Physics

question: A body travelling with uniform acceleration crosses two points $A$ and $B$ with velocities $20\,m/s$ and $30\,m/s$ respectively.  The speed of the body at mid-point of $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
${\left( {30} \right)^2} = {\left( {20} \right)^2} + 2a\left( {2s} \right)\,\rm{or}\,2as = 250$
${\rm{Now,}}\quad {v^2} = {\left( {20} \right)^2} + 2as = 650$
$\therefore \quad v = 25.5\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A person throws balls into air vertically upward in regular intervals of time of one second. The  next ball is thrown when the velocity of the ball thrown earlier becomes zero. The height to  which the balls rise is… (Assume $10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Given that time taken by the ball to reach highest point is $t = 1s$
When the first ball reaches the highest point;
using $v = u + at,$
where $v = 0,a = - g,t = 1s,$
$0 = u - (10)(1)$
$u = 10\,m/s$
Using ${v^2} - {u^2} = 2ah$, we get
${(0)^2} - {(10)^2} = 2( - 10)(h)$
$h = \frac{{{{(10)}^2}}}{{20}} = 5 \, m$
subject: Physics

question: A stone falls freely under gravity. The total distance covered by it in the last second of its journey equals the distance covered by it in first $3 \,s$ of its motion.  The time for which the stone is in air is
explanation:
${S_t}th = u + at - \frac{1}{2}a$
or $0 + 10\,t - 5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {\left( 3 \right)^2}$
or $t = 5\,s$
subject: Physics

question: A particle starts its motion from rest under the action of a constant force. If the distance covered in first  $10\,s\,{\rm{is}}\,{s_1}$ and that covered in the first  $20\,s\,{\rm{is}}\,{s_2}$, then
explanation:
Since, the body starts from rest $u = 0$
$s = \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
Now,${s_1} = \frac{1}{2}a\,{(10)^2}$   (1)
and ${s_2} = \frac{1}{2}a\,{(20)^2}$   (2)
Dividing Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), we get
$\frac{{{s_1}}}{{{s_2}}} = \frac{{{{(10)}^2}}}{{{{(20)}^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {s_2} = 4{s_1}$
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration $a$ (in $m{s^{ - 2}}$) of a body, starting from rest varies with time $t$ (in second) according to the realtion $a = 3t + 4$. The velocity of the body starting from rest at time $t = 2s$ will be
explanation:
$dv = a\,dt$
or $v = \int_0^2 {\left( {3t + 4} \right)dt = 14\,m/s} $
subject: Physics

question: A particle starting from rest with constant acceleration travels a distance $x$ in first $2 \, s$ and a distance $y$ in next $2 \, s$, then
explanation:
$\frac{1}{2} \times \left( a \right){\left( 2 \right)^2} = x\,\, {\rm{or}} \,2a = x$
Now, $y = \frac{1}{2}\left( a \right){\left( 4 \right)^2} - \frac{1}{2}\left( a \right){\left( 2 \right)^2} = 6a = 3x$
subject: Physics

question: A car moving with a velocity of $20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ is stopped in a distance of $40 \, m$. If the same car is travelling at double the velocity, the distance travelled by it for same retardation is
explanation:
Initial velocity, $u = 20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$,
Final velocity, $v = 0\,m{s^{ - 1}};$
Distance travelled, $s = 40\,m$
${v^2} - {u^2} = 2\,{\rm{as}};\,0 - {20^2} = 2 \times a \times 40$
We get,$a = - 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
For second case, $u = 2 \times 20\,m{s^{ - 1}} = 40\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$v = 0\,m{s^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{and}}\,a = - 5\,m{s^2}$
Using the third equation of motion,
$s = \frac{{0 - {{\left( {40} \right)}^2}}}{{2\left( { - 5} \right)}} = 160\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A particle starts from rest. Its acceleration "$a$" versus time '$t$' is shown in the figure. The maximum speed of the particle will be:
explanation:
As the particle, starting from rest, is accelerating continuously for $10 \, s$, its velocity will be maximum at the end of $10 \, s$.
${v_{\max }} = {\rm{area}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{the}}\,{\rm{graph}}$
${v_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}(10)\,(8) = 40\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A particle initially at rest moves along the $X$-axis. Its acceleration varies with time as $a = 4t$. If it starts from the origin, the distance covered  by it in $3 \,s$ is
explanation:
$dv = a\,dt$
$\therefore \quad \quad \int_0^v {dv} = \int_0^t {4\,t\,dt} $
${\rm{or}}\quad \quad v = 2{t^2}$
Now, $s = \int_0^3 {v\,dt} = \int_0^3 {\left( {2{t^2}} \right)\,dt} $
$ = \frac{2}{3}{t^2} = 18\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A particle starts with a velocity of $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and moves in a straight line with a retardation of $0.1\,m{s^{ - 2}}$. The first time at which the particle is $15\,m$ from the starting point is
subject: Physics

question: The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest and moving with an acceleration $\frac{4}{3}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}$, in the third second is
explanation:
Distance travelled in ${n^{th}}$ second is given by
${s_n} = u + \frac{1}{2}a(2n - 1)$
Here,$u = 0,\quad a = \frac{4}{3}$
$\therefore \quad {s_3} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{3} \times (6 - 1)$
$= \, \frac {10}{3} \, m$
subject: Physics

question: If a ball is thrown vertically upward with speed $u$, the distance covered during the last $t$ second of its ascent is
explanation:
Let $t$ second of upward journey = first $t$ second of downward journey (with zero initial velocity)
$\therefore $ Desired distance $ = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A body standing at the top of a tower of $20 \, m$ height drops a stone. Assuming, $g = \,10\,m{s^{ - 2}},$ the velocity  with which it hits the ground is
explanation:
Given $g = \,10\,m{s^{ - 2}}\,{\rm{and}}\,h = 20 \, m$
We have $v = \sqrt {2gh} $
$ = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 20} = \sqrt {400} = 20\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: The acceleration $a$ in $m/{s^2}$ of particle is given by $a = 3{t^2} + 2t + 2$ where $t$ is the time. If the particle starts out with a velocity $v = 2\,m/s$ at $t = 0$, then the velocity at the end of $2 \, s$ is
explanation:
$a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 3{t^2} + 2t + 2$
$dv = \left( {3{t^2} + 2t + 2} \right)dt$
$\int\limits_2^v {dv = 3\int\limits_0^2 {{t^2}dt + 2} \int\limits_0^2 {t\,dt + 2} } \int\limits_0^2 {dt} $
$v - 2 = 3\left[ {\frac{{{t^3}}}{3}} \right]_0^2 + 2\left[ {\frac{{{t^2}}}{2}} \right]_0^2 + 2\left[ t \right]_0^2$
$v - 2 = 8 + 4 + 4\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow v = 18\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A body falls from a height $h = 200\,m$. The ratio of distance travelled in each $2 \, s$, during $t = 0\,{\rm{to}}\,t = 6\,s$ of the journey is
explanation:
${h_1} = \frac{1}{2} \times g \times {\left( 2 \right)^2} = 2g$
${h_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times g \times {\left( 4 \right)^2} - 2g = 6g$
${h_3} = \frac{1}{2} \times g \times {\left( 6 \right)^2} - 8g = 10g$
$\therefore \quad \quad {h_1}:{h_2}:{h_3} = 1:3:5$
subject: Physics

question: A body dropped from the top of a tower covers a distance $7 \, x$ in the last second of its journey where $x$ is the distance covered in first second. How much time does it takes to reach the ground?
explanation:
In first second distance travelled
$x = \frac{1}{2} \times g \times {t^2} = 5\,m$
(for $g = 10 \, m/{s^2} $) in last second $7x = 35\,m$
Now, ${S_t}th = \left( {u + at - \frac{1}{2}a} \right)$
or, $35 = 0 + 10 \times t - \frac{1}{2} \times 10$
or $t = 4\,s$
subject: Physics

question: A stone falls freely under gravity. It covers distance ${h_1},{h_2}\,{\rm{and}}\,{h_3}$ in the first $5 \, s$, the next $5 \, s$ and the next $5 \, s$ respectively. The relation between ${h_1},{h_2}\,{\rm{and}}\,{h_3}$ is
explanation:
For free fall from a height, $u = 0$
$\therefore $ Distance covered by stone in first $5 \, s$,
${h_1} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}\,g{(5)^2} = \frac{{25}}{2}g$   (1)
$\therefore $ Distance covered in first $10 \, s$,
${s_2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g{(10)^2} = \frac{{100}}{2}g$
$\therefore $ Distance covered in second $5 \, s$
${h_2} = {s_2} - {h_1} = \frac{{100}}{2}g - \frac{{25}}{2}g = \frac{{75}}{2}g$   (2)
Distance covered in first $15 \, s$,
${s_3} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g\,{(15)^2} = \frac{{225}}{2}g$
$\therefore $ Distance covered in last $5 \,s$,
${h_3} = {s_3} - {s_2} = \frac{{225}}{2}g - \frac{{100}}{2}g = \frac{{125}}{2}g$   (3)
From Eqs (1), (2) and (3), we get
${h_1}:{h_2}:{h_3} = \frac{{25}}{2}g:\frac{{75}}{2}g:\frac{{125}}{2}g = 1:3:5$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {h_1} = \frac{{{h_2}}}{3} = \frac{{{h_3}}}{5}$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Motion can be observed in everything, from a person walking to the movement of galaxies.
The kinematic equations for uniformly accelerated motion are applicable only when the size of the object in motion can be neglected.
explanation: Statement A is true because motion is a fundamental aspect of the universe at every scale, as described in the introduction of the document. Statement B is false; the applicability of the kinematic equations for uniformly accelerated motion does not depend on the size of the object but rather on the constancy of the acceleration and the assumption that the object can be approximated as a point mass when considering its motion over large distances. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: In kinematics, we study ways to describe motion without considering the causes of motion.

Reason: Kinematics focuses on the concepts of velocity and acceleration to describe motion.
explanation: The assertion is a correct statement as kinematics indeed deals with the description of motion without delving into the forces that cause such motion. The reason is also correct, as it explains the assertion by highlighting that kinematics uses quantities like velocity and acceleration to describe how an object's position changes with time. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: In a Shopping Mall, a person walks up a stalled escalator in $90\,s$. When standing on the same escalator, now moving, he is carried in$60\,s$. The time he would take to walk up the moving escalator will be
explanation:
${t_{1}=90 s}$
$\Rightarrow 90=\dfrac{d}{v_{m}}$$w.r.t.$speed of man ${=\dfrac{d}{90}}$
${t_{2}=60 s}$
${\Rightarrow 60=\dfrac{d}{v_{s}}}$
speed of escalator, ${v_{s}=\dfrac{d}{60}}$
${t=\dfrac{d}{v_{m}+v_{s}}=\dfrac{d}{d / 90+d / 60}}$
${=\dfrac{60 \times 90}{150}=36 {~s}}$
subject: Physics

question: A lift is coming from 8th floor and is just about to reach 4th floor. Taking ground floor as origin and positive direction upwards for all quantities, which one of the following is correct?
explanation: As the lift is coming in downward direction, displacement will be negative. We have to see whether the motion is accelerating or retarding. We know that due to downward motion displacement will be negative. When the lift reaches $4^{\text {th }}$ floor is about to stop hence, motion is retarding in nature. Hence $x < 0 ; a>0$. As displacement is in negative direction, velocity will also be negative i.e., $v < 0$.
subject: Physics
